parsed_target.game_explanation: Three players each start with 10 red and 10 green chips. Green chips are common-value (0.50 each). Red chips have heterogeneous private values: dog 0.30, buffalo 0.10, cat 0.40. Because players care only about their own total value, proposers will try to convert lower-value holdings into higher-value ones for themselves (e.g., buy greens by offering reds), and responders will accept only if the trade increases their own value in expectation. Uncertainty arises from (1) private asymmetries in red values (responders know only their own valuation), (2) the rule that if multiple responders accept one is chosen at random (so expected payoff is reduced under competition), and (3) future opportunity costs across the three rounds. Given valuations, buffalo (with the lowest red value) has the most to gain from buying greens by offering reds, and cat (with moderately high red value) can be a willing counterparty when offered enough red. Dog's red value is intermediate. Forecasted pattern: many small 1:1 proposals to buy a green for 1 red will be declined because they are unfavorable to responders; profitable trades for the proposer typically require offering multiple reds per green; buffalo as buyer offering 2 red for 1 green is attractive to both dog and cat, so such proposals are likely to draw multiple acceptances and be resolved by random selection. Overall expect a few mutually beneficial asymmetric exchanges (buffalo buying greens by selling multiple reds) and many declined low-margin offers.

rounds.round_explanation: Early-game signaling and search. Proposers test simple 1-for-1 trades but responders decline unless the offered red quantity compensates for the 0.50 green value. Buffalo, who values red least, can profitably offer 2 reds for 1 green; that offer is appealing to both cat and dog (both would gain), so it is likely to draw multiple acceptances and be resolved by random selection.

Mid-game: players update holdings and re-assess attractive trades. After the round-0 exchange (buffalo bought a green for 2 reds), both buyer and recipient have adjusted inventories. Proposers again test small-margin swaps (1-for-1) which remain unattractive to responders; no mutually profitable new offer emerges, so bargaining stalls this round.

End-game: proposers can attempt the same profitable multi-red-for-green exchange that worked earlier. Buffalo again offers 2 reds for 1 green. That offer is attractive to both dog and cat (each gains in absolute value), so both accept and the recipient is chosen randomly; in this forecast cat is chosen again. With few rounds left, players accept clear positive expected gains even when competition halves the expected payoff.

parsed_target.game_explanation: Three players each start with 10 RED and 10 GREEN. GREEN has a common and known value of $0.50 to all players; RED is privately valued: badger-purple-4937 values RED at $0.80 (high), while otter-yellow-8375 and goose-purple-9353 value RED at $0.30 (low). Because GREEN is worth more than RED for otter and goose, they prefer to trade away RED for GREEN; badger prefers the opposite. Proposers must request one color and offer another; thus otter and goose will typically propose to buy GREEN (offer RED), and badger will propose to buy RED (offer GREEN). Acceptance depends on how the trade changes each responder's own total value given their private valuations. For a 1:1 RED↔GREEN swap: recipients who receive GREEN in exchange for RED (i.e., give RED to get GREEN) gain +$0.20 if they are otter/goose, so they accept badger's offers; recipients who give GREEN to receive RED (i.e., trade GREEN for RED) gain +$0.30 if they are badger, so badger accepts otter/goose offers. Because players don't know others' private RED values, proposers choose simple, clearly beneficial ratios; in equilibrium-like play here 1-for-1 swaps are plausible and repeatedly executed. When both non-senders would accept a proposal, the recipient is chosen at random among acceptors; otherwise the sole acceptor becomes the recipient. The likely flow is repeated 1-for-1 swaps moving RED toward badger and GREEN toward otter/goose, increasing each individual's private value.

rounds.round_explanation: Initial positions: all players 10 RED / 10 GREEN. Otter and goose prefer GREEN; badger prefers RED. Otter/goose proposals to buy GREEN by offering RED will be accepted by badger (who prefers RED), while badger proposals to buy RED by offering GREEN will be attractive to otter and goose (who prefer GREEN), often producing multiple acceptances and a random recipient.

Holdings after round 0 concentrate RED toward badger and GREEN toward otter/goose. The same incentive pattern continues: otter/goose propose 1-for-1 RED→GREEN swaps that badger accepts; badger proposes RED purchases that both otter and goose would accept, producing a random recipient when both accept.

By round 2 the pattern is entrenched: repeated 1-for-1 exchanges further shift RED to badger and GREEN to the low-RED valuers. Badger's proposals continue to draw acceptance from both otter and goose, and otter/goose proposals to buy GREEN are consistently accepted by badger.

parsed_target.game_explanation: This three-round, three-player bargaining game will revolve around asymmetric valuations of RED chips (GREEN is common-value at $0.50 for everyone). Peacock (RED=$0.80) strongly prefers RED and will try to acquire RED by offering GREEN; Badger (RED=$0.20) strongly prefers GREEN and will be willing to sell RED in exchange for GREEN. Otter (RED=$0.50, GREEN=$0.50) is indifferent between colors and will behave opportunistically or remain conservative when indifferent. Proposers can only offer one color in exchange for the other; they do not choose the accepting partner and acceptances are private. Because proposers do not observe others' private RED valuations, they will infer partner types from observed behavior: repeated proposals by peacock to buy RED and early acceptances by badger will make these patterns clearer over rounds. Recipients accept only when the trade increases their own total chip value (indifference typically leads to decline). Multiple acceptances would randomize the recipient; in this forecast trades generally produce a single clear beneficiary (badger selling RED, peacock buying RED), so each proposed 1-for-1 exchange of RED↔GREEN is expected to be accepted by the player who gains in value and declined by the one who would lose or be indifferent. Holdings update immediately after each accepted turn and drive subsequent incentives (badger's RED stash declines, peacock accumulates RED).

rounds.round_explanation: Early in the game players test each other's preferences. Otter (indifferent) may attempt a small 1-for-1 exchange to see who will sell RED; peacock will propose to buy RED (since RED is more valuable to them) offering GREEN, and badger will propose to buy GREEN by offering RED. Because badger values RED lowest, they will be the primary seller and likely accept offers to trade RED for GREEN; peacock will accept offers that convert their GREEN into RED.

Patterns solidify: peacock consistently prefers to trade GREEN for RED, badger consistently sells RED to obtain GREEN, and otter remains marginally willing to swap when it seems profitable or decline when indifferent. Proposers keep offers small (1-for-1) to ensure trades are appealing to the specific counterparty who stands to gain; recipients accept when they strictly increase their own chip-value.

By the final round players exploit revealed types: peacock continues accumulating RED, badger continues selling RED for GREEN until remaining RED become scarce, and otter continues opportunistic 1-for-1 trades when a counterparty clearly benefits. With history showing who accepts which side, proposers tailor 1-for-1 offers to the player likely to accept; acceptances remain driven by individual chip-value gains.

parsed_target.game_explanation: Three players each start with 10 RED and 10 GREEN. GREEN chips are worth 0.50 to everyone; RED chips are worth 0.10 to cat-orange-7489 and camel-green-9744, and 0.30 to buffalo-red-8335. Because cat and camel value RED much less than GREEN, they have a strong incentive to trade away RED to acquire GREEN. Buffalo values RED more than the other two, so it is the cheapest and most likely counterparty for cat/camel if they offer enough RED to compensate buffalo for giving up GREEN. Each player only knows their own valuations, so proposers may try small exchange ratios (e.g., 1 RED for 1 GREEN) that they think might be attractive but which, from the omniscient analyst view, will be rejected by others unless the offered RED quantity provides equal or higher value to the recipient. Acceptance decisions are private and based only on the responder's own value change; if multiple non-senders accept a proposal the realized recipient is chosen at random. Overall forecast: cat and camel repeatedly offer modest bundles of low-value RED in exchange for a GREEN; buffalo accepts when offered at least 2 RED for 1 GREEN (2*0.30 = 0.60 > 0.50). Buffalo's own proposals to buy GREEN by offering small amounts of RED (e.g., 1-for-1) will be declined by cat and camel because giving up 1 GREEN costs them 0.50 while receiving 1 RED yields only 0.10, a loss.

rounds.round_explanation: Early in play each proposer tests straightforward, small trades. Cat goes first and prefers to trade away low-value RED for GREEN; offering 2 RED for 1 GREEN is enough to make buffalo strictly better off, so buffalo is the expected acceptor. Camel follows with a similar offer. Buffalo's own proposal (offering 1 RED for 1 GREEN) looks attractive to buffalo but, given other players' low RED value, will be declined.

Having observed the first round, cat and camel know their own valuations and the public history showing buffalo accepted 2-for-1 offers. They repeat the successful pattern (2 RED for 1 GREEN) to continue improving their own payoff; buffalo still prefers the trade (2*0.30 > 0.50) and accepts. Buffalo again proposes a modest 1-for-1 swap that is unattractive to cat and camel and is declined.

Patterns from the earlier rounds continue. Cat and camel again profitably trade 2 RED for 1 GREEN with buffalo as recipient. Buffalo's attempt to buy GREEN with only 1 RED remains unattractive to cat and camel and is declined. Public history reinforces expectations: cat and camel anticipate buffalo will accept 2-for-1 offers and keep proposing them.

parsed_target.game_explanation: Three players each start with 10 red and 10 green chips. Green has a public common value of $0.50 for all, while red values are private: dog  $0.90, peacock $0.80, owl $0.50. Dog and peacock both privately prefer red to green and will try to acquire red by offering green; owl is indifferent between colors and is the natural potential trading partner because trading one-for-one is roughly neutral for owl but beneficial for dog/peacock. Proposers must offer one color and request the other; proposers will only propose terms that they expect increase (or at least do not decrease) their own value. Recipients decide based on their private values and current holdings; if a proposer offers too generous terms (many greens for a red) the proposer loses value and will not propose them, while too stingy offers (one green for one red) are attractive for the red-preferers but unattractive to other red-preferrers, making acceptance uncertain. Because multiple recipients can accept simultaneously, there is added uncertainty: a proposer cannot choose the accepting partner, and acceptors face randomness about whether they are chosen. Over the three rounds we forecast repeated small one-for-one offers: dog and peacock repeatedly propose exchanging a single green for a single red (beneficial to them but typically unattractive to each other), while owl often proposes or accepts one-for-one swaps because those trades are close to payoff-neutral for owl but profitable for the red-preferrers. When multiple players accept, the realized recipient is random.

rounds.round_explanation: Initial positions (10 red, 10 green each). Dog (red=0.90) and peacock (red=0.80) will attempt to buy red by offering green; those one-for-one offers benefit the proposer but will usually be declined by the other red-preferrer. Owl (red=0.50) is indifferent and likely to accept a one-for-one trade when it benefits others, creating opportunities for dog/peacock if owl chooses to accept.

After round 0 dog has gained a red (and owl lost one red). Dog, still valuing red highly, repeats a one-for-one green→red proposal expecting owl to accept (owl remains indifferent). Peacock continues to propose one-for-one offers but is typically rejected by dog; owl again offers neutral one-for-one swaps when profitable or acceptable to others.

By round 2 dog has accumulated several reds and continues to prefer gaining more red when one-for-one terms are available. Peacock still attempts one-for-one swaps to increase red holdings. Owl, holding many greens and neutral on colors, continues to serve as the likely counterparty for one-for-one offers; when both others accept, the recipient is selected at random.

parsed_target.game_explanation: Three players repeatedly bargain over one-for-one exchanges of RED and GREEN chips. GREEN chips have a common value of $0.50 to all players; RED chips have heterogeneous private values (ram 0.30, alligator 0.40, butterfly 1.00). Ram and Alligator both value GREEN more than their own RED, so they prefer to sell RED and buy GREEN. Butterfly values RED much more than GREEN, so she prefers to sell GREEN and buy RED. Proposers will therefore typically offer some RED to request GREEN (when the proposer is ram or alligator) or offer GREEN to request RED (when the proposer is butterfly). Recipients decide based on their private marginal gains: they accept when the chip(s) they'd receive are worth more to them than the chip(s) they'd give. This generates a pattern where butterfly frequently accepts offers that give her RED, while ram and alligator frequently accept offers that give them GREEN. When both non-senders would benefit from a proposal, both accept and the recipient is randomly selected; otherwise a single accepting player executes the trade. The main uncertainty each player faces is which opponent will accept (and when multiple acceptors will be randomly selected), and proposers choose small, mutually profitable one-for-one swaps that are likely to be accepted by at least one counterparty given their inferred preferences. The forecast below assumes 1-for-1 exchanges each turn, with acceptances driven by the private valuations given above and immediate holdings updates between turns.

rounds.round_explanation: Opening round: proposers test simple 1-for-1 swaps. Ram and Alligator request GREEN in exchange for offering RED; Butterfly prefers RED and thus accepts those offers. When Butterfly proposes trading GREEN for RED, both Ram and Alligator prefer GREEN and so both accept, producing a random recipient. Early trades shift holdings toward Butterfly accumulating RED and others gaining GREEN.

Players continue the simple sequence. Ram and Alligator again propose 1-for-1 RED->GREEN swaps; Butterfly repeatedly accepts because receiving RED is highly valuable to her. When Butterfly proposes GREEN->RED swaps, both Ram and Alligator still prefer GREEN and accept; the random selection of the recipient alternates in expectation.

Final round: incentives remain the same and players repeat profitable 1-for-1 trades. Butterfly continues to be the primary acquirer of RED, while Ram and Alligator accumulate additional GREEN. When Butterfly offers GREEN for RED, both non-senders accept and the recipient is again randomly selected in this forecast.

parsed_target.game_explanation: Three players start with equal counts of two chip types (10 RED, 10 GREEN each). Greens have the same private value to all players (0.50). Reds have heterogeneous private values: Dolphin 0.40, Ram 0.20, Iguana 0.80. Because each player seeks to maximize their own total chip value, Dolphin and Ram want to exchange away REDs (their lower-value chips) for GREENs, while Iguana wants to exchange away GREENs for REDs. Proposals must trade at least one chip of one color for at least one chip of the other color, so the smallest mutually beneficial unit is often a 1-for-1 swap. Players only know their own private valuation during play, so a proposer cannot perfectly predict who will accept; however, given valuations, the analyst expects Dolphin and Ram to propose trades selling RED and buying GREEN (they gain 0.10 and 0.30 value per 1-for-1 swap respectively), and Iguana to propose selling GREEN and buying RED (gaining 0.30 per 1-for-1). Recipients accept when a proposed exchange raises their own chip value: thus Iguana will generally accept 1 RED for 1 GREEN offers (0.80 > 0.50), while Dolphin will accept Iguana's offers (0.50 > 0.40) and decline offers that would give them RED for GREEN. Ram's low RED value (0.20) makes him an eager recipient of REDs when offered GREENs. When both non-senders would accept a proposal, the realized partner is chosen at random, introducing allocation uncertainty even for attractive offers. Given these incentives and the small, repeated-horizon (3 rounds, 3 proposals per round), the most likely path is many small 1-for-1 trades that move REDs toward Iguana and GREENs toward Dolphin and Ram, with random assignment when both non-senders accept Iguana's buy-RED offers.

rounds.round_explanation: Early play: all players start with 10 RED / 10 GREEN. Dolphin and Ram both prefer GREEN and will propose selling RED to buy GREEN; Iguana prefers RED and will propose selling GREEN to buy RED. Minimal 1-for-1 proposals are attractive to the intended counterparty (Iguana when Dolphin/Ram propose; Dolphin and Ram when Iguana proposes). Expect mostly accepted 1-for-1 swaps, with random assignment when both would accept Iguana's buy-RED offer.

Midgame: holdings have shifted toward Iguana owning more RED and fewer GREEN. Dolphin and Ram continue to propose small RED->GREEN swaps; Iguana continues to propose GREEN->RED swaps. Recipients evaluate only their own gains: Iguana remains willing to accept REDs for GREENs, and Dolphin and Ram are willing to give up REDs for GREENs when Iguana asks. Randomization can assign Iguana's accepted proposal to either Dolphin or Ram when both accept.

Endgame: Iguana has accumulated many REDs and few GREENs but still values additional REDs highly; Dolphin and Ram hold more GREENs and fewer REDs. Continued 1-for-1 swaps remain mutually beneficial across the roles (Dolphin/Ram selling RED for GREEN to Iguana, and Iguana buying RED from either). Since this is the last round, players have no future bargaining leverage; so they accept profitable immediate gains, leading to further consolidation of REDs with Iguana and GREENs with Dolphin/Ram.

parsed_target.game_explanation: Three players begin with equal endowments of red and green chips but very different private values for red (owl 0.30, iguana 0.10, whale 0.80) while green is common-value (0.50). This creates clear bilateral trading incentives: owl and iguana prefer converting low-value red into higher-value green; whale prefers acquiring red and is willing to give green. Proposers will typically offer small, simple 1-for-1 swaps that create positive surplus for both sides given private values. Because accept/reject is private and multiple acceptances lead to a random recipient, proposers face some partner-selection risk when both non-senders would benefit. Observationally, we forecast a series of repeated 1-for-1 swaps where owl and iguana repeatedly propose to buy GREEN for RED (getting accepted mainly by whale), and whale repeatedly proposes to buy RED for GREEN (getting acceptance from both owl and iguana). Uncertainty in the game comes mainly from (1) which accepter is chosen when both accept a whale proposal, and (2) whether proposers try to extract extra surplus by offering worse terms that might be declined. Using the players' private values as analyst priors, the simplest mutually beneficial terms are 1 GREEN for 1 RED, which both sides will accept whenever the proposer stands to gain, producing the forecast below.

rounds.round_explanation: Early round: all players still have ample chips. Owl and iguana prefer green and will propose 1-for-1 trades offering red to get green; whale prefers red and will accept those. When whale proposes the symmetric trade (offering green to buy red), both owl and iguana benefit and will accept, creating a random recipient outcome. Expect straightforward 1-for-1 trades that shift greens toward owl/iguana and reds toward whale.

Mid game: holdings have started to polarize (whale accumulating reds, others accumulating greens). Incentives remain the same: owl and iguana still gain from swapping red for green and thus propose that; whale continues to buy red by offering green. When whale proposes, both non-senders still prefer trading red for green and will accept; the proposer faces random partner selection risk but not downside because any partner acceptance yields the same gain.

Late round: the pattern continues but holdings become more extreme (whale concentrating red, others with more green). Players still prefer marginal improvements and will accept the same simple terms. Whale must be mindful of remaining green stock, but one-for-one exchanges remain feasible. Because all trades are small and mutually beneficial, most proposals execute.

parsed_target.game_explanation: All three players start with 10 red and 10 green chips. As the analyst I use the private valuations given as priors: duck-pink-5390 and gorilla-purple-6898 value GREEN = $0.50 and RED = $0.20, while kangaroo-purple-4743 values GREEN = $0.50 and RED = $0.60. That creates opposing incentives: duck and gorilla strictly prefer green to red and will be motivated to sell reds for greens; kangaroo strictly prefers red to green and will be motivated to sell greens for reds. Because players only know their own valuations during play, proposers must offer enough compensation (in terms of chip quantities) to make at least one counterpart expect a private gain. Observing prior acceptances gives noisy information about others' preferences, so early successful trades by proposers who offer red-for-green will reveal that someone (likely the acceptor) values red relatively highly. Given standard bargaining incentives, proposers will try to minimize what they give up while offering a package that at least one other player will accept; recipients accept when the trade increases their own total chip value given their private valuation. Random tie-breaking is relevant when multiple recipients accept. The likely dynamic is repeated small exchanges in which duck and gorilla repeatedly offer red for green and kangaroo repeatedly offers red-buying deals (selling greens) so trades execute, pushing reds toward kangaroo and greens toward duck/gorilla. Uncertainty about others' red valuations is gradually reduced by observed acceptances, which makes modest, repeated one-for-one (or two-for-one early) exchanges plausible.

rounds.round_explanation: Opening round: proposers test feasible exchange rates. Duck and gorilla, who privately prefer green, will offer red for green; kangaroo, who prefers red, will accept deals exchanging green for red. Early offers may be slightly generous (e.g., 2 red for 1 green) to ensure acceptance until patterns become clear; once acceptances are observed, proposers can step down to 1-for-1 trades.

After the first-round pattern (duck/gorilla offering red-for-green and kangaroo accepting), proposers reduce generosity to 1-for-1 trades. Duck and gorilla continue to sell red to obtain green; kangaroo continues to accept those deals because each trade increases his value. When kangaroo proposes to buy red (selling green), both opponents would benefit from accepting, so multiple acceptances and random selection can occur.

Final opportunity to reallocate before the game ends. Proposers stick to the efficient, low-cost ratios identified earlier (mainly 1-for-1). Kangaroo's green holdings are now low and red holdings high; duck and gorilla have given away red and accumulated green. Acceptance behavior stays value-driven: duck and gorilla accept trades that increase their own value (getting green for red), and kangaroo accepts trades that increase his own value (getting red for green).

parsed_target.game_explanation: All three players share the same private valuations in the analyst prior: RED = $0.80 (more valuable), GREEN = $0.50 (common value). Because each player's private goal is to maximize their own total chip value and players only know their own valuations during play, typical bargaining tensions arise from asymmetric information about others' possible proposals, but here the analyst prior is symmetric. A proposer who requests RED and offers GREEN gains value (0.80 - 0.50 = +0.30) if the proposer were to receive the RED and give away one GREEN, but any recipient who would give up one RED in exchange for one GREEN loses value (-0.30). That means there is no bilateral set of integer trades that strictly benefits both sides under the stated valuations. Proposers therefore are likely to propose trades that would help themselves (for example, buy 1 RED for 1 GREEN), but recipients will rationally decline because each such trade reduces their private payoff. Given these incentives and the absence of heterogeneous private values that would create mutually beneficial exchange, the most likely forecast for the full three-round game is that proposers repeatedly propose small one-for-one swaps favoring the proposer (buy RED 1, sell GREEN 1) and all responders decline each time. There is some uncertainty from the players' perspective because each only knows their own values, but the symmetric incentives make universal decline the robust prediction: no accepted trades and holdings remain unchanged across the game.

rounds.round_explanation: Early-round dynamics: proposers try straightforward, locally profitable proposals (request 1 RED in exchange for 1 GREEN). Because recipients would lose value on a 1-for-1 swap, they are expected to decline. Proposers prefer these offers for their own expected gain, but acceptances are unlikely.

Mid-game: proposers face the same incentives as before. Because accepted trades would require the recipient to give up higher-valued REDs for lower-valued GREENs, recipients continue to decline. Proposers may experiment with similar one-for-one offers again, hoping misaligned private information (if any) leads someone to accept, but with symmetric valuations such acceptance is unlikely.

Final-round dynamics: with the same private valuations and no previously executed trades, the strategic calculus remains unchanged. Proposers may repeat the same one-for-one proposals, but recipients should still decline because those trades reduce the recipients' private payoff. The forecast is continued rejection and no change in holdings by the end of the game.

parsed_target.game_explanation: This three-round, three-player bargaining game centers on trading two chip types: GREEN (common-value, $0.50 for everyone) and RED (private-value, different per player). Pig (pig-green-5746) values GREEN more than RED (G=0.50, R=0.30) and so will typically try to trade away RED to acquire GREEN. Bear (bear-pink-9082) slightly prefers RED to GREEN (R=0.60 vs G=0.50) and will typically trade GREEN to get RED. Hamster (hamster-pink-796) strongly prefers RED (R=1.00 vs G=0.50) and so will consistently trade GREEN to obtain RED. Proposers can only offer one color and request the other; responders accept if the proposed exchange increases their own private value (given the sizes on offer), but they face uncertainty about whether others will also accept because if multiple responders accept, one accepting partner is chosen at random. This creates two strategic forces: proposers choose offers that are personally profitable and attractive enough to at least one counterparty, and responders weigh guaranteed private-value gains against the possibility of being outcompeted if multiple accept. Given the private valuations and symmetric starting endowments (10 RED, 10 GREEN each), the forecasted play features frequent 1-for-1 exchanges where pig offers RED for GREEN and bear/hamster offer GREEN for RED; many proposals are accepted by at least one counterparty, sometimes by both, in which case a random accepting partner is selected.

rounds.round_explanation: Early in the game, all players still hold large, symmetric endowments. Pig wants to swap low-value RED for high-value GREEN, while Bear and Hamster want RED. Reasonable 1-for-1 proposals are mutually profitable for some pairs. Responders accept if the trade improves their own private value; when both non-senders accept, the proposer is matched at random to one accepting partner.

By round 1 holdings have shifted: pig accumulated more GREEN, while Bear and Hamster have more RED. Proposals remain 1-for-1 because these are minimally credible and often cross the private-value thresholds. Pig continues to offer RED for GREEN; Bear and Hamster continue to offer GREEN for RED. Responders accept if the swap increases their private value given their current holdings.

In the final round players make last adjustments. Pig still values GREEN and tends to buy GREEN with RED; Bear and Hamster continue to seek RED by selling GREEN. Because private valuations are stable and trades are small (1-for-1), many proposals remain attractive to at least one counterparty, producing several accepted trades. When both responders accept, random matching determines the recipient; when only one accepts, the trade executes deterministically.

parsed_target.game_explanation: Three players with asymmetric private values negotiate over two chip types for three rounds. GREEN chips are common-value at $0.50 for everyone; RED chips are privately different: Whale values RED high ($0.80) and will generally try to acquire RED, while Swan ($0.10) and Turtle ($0.20) value RED much less and prefer GREEN. Proposers must offer one color and request the other; they will only make proposals that they expect to increase their own total value or that are likely to be declined. Acceptors evaluate each proposal only by the immediate change to their own portfolio value given their private RED valuation and the public chip holdings. Trades that move RED toward Whale and GREEN toward Swan/Turtle are typically mutually beneficial because Whale pays greens (which he values less than RED) and Swan/Turtle pay reds (which they value less). Uncertainty arises from (1) private values (each player does not know others' RED valuations), (2) the random resolution when multiple players accept a single proposal, and (3) inventory constraints (proposers cannot offer more than they hold), which sometimes make mutually beneficial rates infeasible. The game forecast below applies these priors to predict plausible, value-improving trades early on (where Whale buys RED from others by paying GREEN) and then converges to a state where Whale accumulates RED and runs low on GREEN, leaving later proposals unprofitable and often declined.

rounds.round_explanation: Early round: everyone starts with many of both chips (10/10). Whale prefers RED and will propose trades buying RED with GREEN at exchange rates that are still attractive to the low-RED-valuing sellers (Swan and Turtle). Swan and Turtle, who value GREEN more, will typically accept good red-for-green deals. Proposers try to trade in quantities they can offer; acceptance depends purely on immediate gain to each responder.

After the initial trades Whale has accumulated RED and reduced GREEN but still finds profitable red-for-green trades. Swan and Turtle have fewer RED and more GREEN; Swan makes small, low-cost trades to convert remaining RED to GREEN where profitable. Turtle continues selling small bundles of RED for GREEN when Whale can afford the greens. Whale's inventory of GREEN falls as he repeatedly buys RED.

By the final round Whale has accumulated a large RED stock and is low on GREEN; making any trade that sells RED to buy GREEN would reduce his own value, so he prefers to propose deals that will be declined rather than accept value-losing trades. Swan has too few RED to propose offers generous enough to induce Turtle to accept, and Whale lacks GREEN to accept green-for-red proposals. As a result, late proposals tend to be infeasible for mutual gain and are frequently declined.

parsed_target.game_explanation: This three-round, three-player bargaining game features two chip types: GREEN (common value known to all = $0.50) and RED (private values differ by player and are unknown to others). Frog (frog-orange-3732) privately values RED at $0.60 and GREEN at $0.50, so frog prefers accumulating RED. Hedgehog (hedgehog-purple-9301) values RED at $0.20 and GREEN at $0.50, so hedgehog strongly prefers GREEN and will willingly trade away RED for GREEN. Badger (badger-purple-9216) values both colors equally at $0.50, making badger indifferent on 1-for-1 exchanges but able to exploit others' asymmetries. Because GREEN's value is common knowledge and RED's value is private, proposals that exchange GREEN for RED will be evaluated against known green value and each responder's private red value. The likely dynamics: frog repeatedly proposes to buy RED by offering GREEN (frog gains a small private benefit and hedgehog, who dislikes RED, often will accept); hedgehog, when it can, buys GREEN by offering RED (but if hedgehog runs out of RED it cannot credibly offer RED and may propose trades that get declined); badger opportunistically posts neutral trades (1:1) that are accepted by the player who can profit (usually hedgehog selling RED or frog buying RED), or posts GREEN-for-RED offers to frog to secure GREEN when frog values RED more and will accept giving GREEN for RED. Uncertainty about others' private RED values means some trades that look favorable to the proposer may be declined (if responders expect no private gain), while repeated acceptances by the same responder reveal that that responder likely places low value on RED. The observer forecast below assumes players act to maximize their own chip-value given their private valuations and the public history.

rounds.round_explanation: Early in the game all players start with 10 RED and 10 GREEN. Frog (prefers RED) initiates trades to acquire RED by offering GREEN; hedgehog (dislikes RED) will accept those swaps, while badger (indifferent) typically declines 1-for-1 exchanges unless profitable. Badger will try to buy RED from hedgehog when hedgehog prefers GREEN, yielding accepted trades where hedgehog sells RED for GREEN.

After round 0 hedgehog has shown a willingness to trade RED for GREEN, so frog intensifies attempts to buy RED (now sometimes asking for multiple RED per GREEN). Hedgehog continues to sell RED for GREEN because those swaps increase hedgehog's value. Badger continues opportunistic 1-for-1 offers that get accepted by hedgehog when hedgehog benefits.

By round 2 hedgehog has low RED holdings and abundant GREEN. Frog continues to try to extract RED from hedgehog, pushing for larger multi-unit swaps while hedgehog still finds selling RED advantageous. Hedgehog, with few or no RED to offer, may post proposals that cannot credibly improve its own payoff and therefore get declined. Badger posts neutral 1-for-1 swaps (RED for GREEN or vice versa) that are accepted by the player who gains privately (often frog), while badger remains roughly indifferent.

parsed_target.game_explanation: All players value GREEN identically at $0.50 (common-value) and value RED differently (tiger $0.10, mouse $0.30, unicorn $0.20). This creates a clear, ongoing incentive: proposers want to trade away low-value REDs to acquire higher-value GREENs. Responders accept only when the red quantity offered compensates them for giving up greens according to their private red value. Because responders' red values differ, a given red-for-green exchange will be acceptable to some players (mouse, who values RED highest) but not to others (tiger and unicorn, who value RED less). Proposers face uncertainty about others' private valuations and thus tend to make offers that are cheap enough for themselves but still attractive to at least one likely partner. Over three rounds we expect repeated small red→green exchanges, with mouse (highest red value) frequently chosen as recipient because they require the least premium to give up greens. Trades are executed immediately and holdings evolve, reinforcing who is the marginal supplier/recipient in later rounds.

rounds.round_explanation: Initial symmetry: all start 10R/10G. Tiger (low RED value) can cheaply offer multiple REDs to buy GREEN and will target the player most likely to accept (mouse, who values RED highest). Mouse's early holdings and red valuation make them the most frequent acceptor. Proposals that do not sufficiently compensate responders (e.g., 1R for 1G) are likely to be declined.

Holdings after round 0 concentrate REDs with mouse and GREENs with tiger/unicorn. Tiger and unicorn continue to find it efficient to offer a small bundle of REDs (2R→1G) because that is inexpensive for them but enough to be accepted by mouse. Mouse's proposals still struggle to find partners without offering large premiums that would hurt mouse's own payoff.

Patterns solidify: mouse accumulates REDs and is repeatedly the acceptor for modest red-for-green offers (2R→1G). Tiger and unicorn continue to reduce their RED holdings in exchange for extra GREENs; mouse declines symmetric 1:1 proposals because they would require the other responders to accept losses.

parsed_target.game_explanation: Three rounds, three proposal opportunities per round. Players start with 10 red and 10 green each. Greens are common-value (0.50) for all; red values differ (parrot 0.10, fox 0.70, bear 0.50). Parrot strongly prefers green over red, fox strongly prefers red, bear is intermediate/indifferent. Proposers request one color and offer another; recipients accept if the trade increases (or does not decrease, in borderline cases) their personal chip value given their private valuations. Uncertainty stems from each player only knowing their own red valuation and inferring others' preferences from past trades. Expect parrot to repeatedly offer red to acquire green, fox to offer green to acquire red, and bear to act opportunistically — accepting trades that convert their less-preferred chips into more-valued ones and proposing trades that yield positive expected value given their holdings. Because multiple acceptances lead to random selection, proposers may make offers attractive to a specific partner (the one most likely to accept) rather than splitting surplus optimally across all players.

rounds.round_explanation: Initial holdings are 10R/10G each. Parrot has low red value and will try to buy green by offering red; fox will try to buy red by offering green; bear is middle and will accept profitable exchanges, especially when selling green to obtain red if that increases their value. Early proposals favor small exchanges (1–3 chips) that are individually profitable for the proposer and for at least one counterparty.

Holdings after round 0 favor parrot receiving green and reducing red, fox accumulating red, and bear accumulating red as well. Parrot continues to prefer green and will make small buy-green / sell-red offers that fox (who values red highly) is inclined to accept. Fox will continue to purchase red cheaply using green. Bear will propose larger red-for-green swaps that are profitable for both bear and the specific counterparty who has many reds to offload.

By the final round parrot has few or no reds left and many greens; fox and bear hold large red stacks. Parrot will still be willing to exchange any remaining red for green. Fox will continue buying red (offering green) when profitable for itself; bear will pursue small profitable conversions and may accept or propose trades that produce modest gains. Because acceptances are simultaneous, proposers tailor offers that appeal to one likely partner (not to everyone) to avoid a contested acceptance lottery.

parsed_target.game_explanation: All three players start with identical chip counts but different private valuations for RED (monkey: 0.80, buffalo: 0.70, goose: 0.70) while GREEN is common-value (0.50). Because values are discrete and chips trade in integer units, many small one-for-one swaps have no mutually beneficial integer price: giving up one GREEN (0.50) to acquire one RED is profitable for monkey (+0.30) but costly for buffalo/goose (-0.20), and the converse trades are similarly unattractive. This creates a tension: proposers who most value RED (monkey, goose when low on RED) will propose to buy RED by offering GREEN, but sellers will typically refuse unless offered a bundle large enough to cover their higher valuation of RED. Thus the likely pattern is many small proposals get declined, interspersed with occasional larger bundle proposals where quantities are set so both sides gain (or at least one side is indifferent), producing accepted trades. Because accept/reject decisions are private and proposers cannot choose among multiple acceptors, some accepted proposals will be resolved by random recipient selection when more than one other player accepts. Throughout, each player acts to maximize their own total chip value given their private RED value and the public holdings and history.

rounds.round_explanation: Early in play all players have 10 RED and 10 GREEN. Monkey prefers RED most strongly and will attempt small RED-for-GREEN proposals (which will typically be declined). Buffalo and goose may propose larger bundled swaps that create integer prices where both sides are at least not worse off; such bundle offers can be accepted by the player(s) who benefit. Expect mostly declines for small proposals and a larger accepted bundle in this round as players test feasible exchange ratios.

Holdings after round 0 concentrate RED with buffalo and monkey and more GREEN with monkey and goose. Buffalo, short on GREEN after the big trade, can propose another bundled purchase of GREEN using RED; such a bundle can be structured to be mutually beneficial with monkey (who holds many GREEN) and thus accepted. Small single-chip proposals remain unlikely to succeed.

By the final round holdings are asymmetric: monkey holds many RED, buffalo regained some GREEN, and goose retains a lot of GREEN but fewer RED. Players can still find bundle ratios that yield small positive gains for proposer and seller (or make the seller indifferent). Expect one more accepted medium-sized bundle (buffalo buying GREEN or goose buying RED with appropriately chosen integer quantities) and some declined small proposals.

parsed_target.game_explanation: Three players trade two chip types (RED, GREEN). GREEN has a common value of $0.50 for everyone; RED values differ (dog 0.30, cat 0.70, rabbit 0.50). This creates a clear directional incentive: cat prefers RED over GREEN, dog prefers GREEN over RED, and rabbit is essentially indifferent. Proposers can exploit these asymmetries by offering chips that another player values less in exchange for chips that player values more. Because players only know their own private values during play, there is uncertainty in who will accept a given offer, but the observable pattern is that dog-made offers that request GREEN and offer RED tend to be attractive to cat, and cat-made offers that request RED and offer GREEN tend to be attractive to dog. Rabbit, being value-neutral, will often decline offers that hurt them or accept neutral trades opportunistically; they may accept when it benefits them or is payoff-neutral. If multiple responders accept, the recipient is chosen at random; in this forecast I assume single-acceptance outcomes when a clear mutually beneficial match exists and declines otherwise. Each player acts to maximize their own total chip value, so proposals that would lower the proposer's own value are typically declined or structured to be rejected.

rounds.round_explanation: Initial holdings are 10 RED and 10 GREEN each. Dog prefers GREEN, cat prefers RED, rabbit is indifferent. Early trades are likely to be small 1-for-1 swaps: dog will offer RED for GREEN (profitable for both dog and cat), cat will reciprocate buying RED for GREEN (profitable for cat and dog), and rabbit will try small neutral trades that cat may accept.

After round 0 cat has accumulated more RED and fewer GREEN; dog has more GREEN and fewer RED. The same incentive pattern continues. Dog and cat can still execute small beneficial swaps (dog gives RED to get GREEN; cat gives GREEN to get RED). Rabbit continues to propose neutral 1-for-1 trades that cat tends to accept because they value RED more.

Near the end, cat has relatively few GREEN and many RED; dog has relatively few RED and many GREEN. Dog continues to request GREEN in exchange for RED and can still find willing partners in cat. Cat, with few GREEN left, faces a constraint: they cannot sell GREEN and so would have to offer RED to buy GREEN (a self-damaging move) if they proposed a trade; anticipating rejection, such proposals are likely to be declined. Rabbit can still execute small neutral/profitable (for the counterparty) swaps with dog or cat if partners accept.

parsed_target.game_explanation: All three players start with identical chip counts but different private values for red chips: Ram values red and green equally (0.50 each), while Squid and Otter value red less (0.40) and green at 0.50. That creates a clear bilateral trading motive: Squid and Otter prefer to sell red and buy green (they gain 0.10 per chip swapped), while Ram is roughly indifferent between colors and can act as a liquidity provider, offering green to acquire red or vice versa with little private loss. Because proposals require the proposer to offer one color and request another, proposers who want to accumulate green (Squid, Otter) will typically offer red for green; a proposer who wants red (Ram) will offer green for red. Acceptance decisions are private and based only on each recipient's own values and current holdings: Squid and Otter will accept trades that convert their red into green, but will decline conversions that lose them value (green→red). Ram, being value-indifferent, is the swing acceptor: he may accept neutral 1-for-1 swaps (or decline if repeatedly giving up scarce greens). Uncertainty arises because when multiple recipients accept, the proposer cannot choose the partner and a random accepting recipient is selected, and players only know their own valuations (so proposers must predict others' incentives from public holdings and past behavior). Over three rounds and nine proposal turns, we therefore expect Ram to frequently propose green-for-red trades that attract both Squid and Otter, producing several accepted trades (with random recipients), while Squid's and Otter's own proposals to buy green with red will often be declined by the others except when Ram is willing to accept neutral swaps.

rounds.round_explanation: Initial round: Ram likely opens by offering green for red because Squid and Otter privately prefer selling red for green. Squid and Otter will propose the opposite (offer red for green) but receive little acceptance except from Ram, who is indifferent and may accept occasionally. Early trades deplete Ram's green stock and shift red from Squid/Otter to Ram when accepted.

Midgame: After some redistribution, Squid and Otter continue to prefer selling red for green. Ram keeps proposing green-for-red swaps; when both non-senders accept, the recipient is chosen at random. Squid's proposals to buy green by offering red will usually be declined by Otter (who would lose green) and may be declined by Ram out of indifference or to conserve green, so many of Squid's offers go unaccepted.

Endgame: Ram has given up several greens and accumulated red; he may become more conservative about accepting further green→red swaps, but because Squid and Otter still prefer selling red for green, Ram's proposals to buy red can continue to attract both and produce accepted trades. Squid's and Otter's own proposals to buy green by offering red are unlikely to attract acceptances from the others (they would worsen the other's private payoff), so the round ends with a couple of declined attempts.

parsed_target.game_explanation: This three-round, three-player chip trade is driven by asymmetries in red-chip private values and a common, fixed green value. Green chips are worth $0.50 to everyone; red chips are worth $1.00 to camel-pink-767, $0.60 to butterfly-yellow-2139, and $0.30 to alligator-orange-9612. Observationally: camel and butterfly value red more than green and therefore prefer to trade green for red; alligator values red less than green and prefers to trade red for green. Because each player knows only their own valuations, offers are evaluated deterministically by recipients using their private values: a recipient will accept iff the chips they receive are worth more to them than the chips they give up. Under those priors we expect proposers who want reds (camel and butterfly) to offer green for red, and the green-preferring alligator to offer red for green. Camel and butterfly will rarely accept trades that require giving up green in exchange for red (they would lose value), while alligator will rarely accept giving up green and will readily give red to get green. Alligator’s proposals (sell red, buy green) therefore attract both other players (both gain by giving red and getting green); when multiple accept a random accepting player is chosen as recipient. Because all players begin with large, symmetric endowments (10 red, 10 green), the game unfolds as a sequence of small 1-for-1 exchanges: camel and butterfly repeatedly attempt to buy red with green and are typically matched with alligator; alligator repeatedly offers red for green and often draws both other players’ acceptance, with a random recipient chosen. The forecast below uses these priors to show specific 1-for-1 proposals and acceptance outcomes round-by-round.

rounds.round_explanation: Start-of-game positions are symmetric (10R,10G each). Camel (red value $1.00) and Butterfly ($0.60) both prefer red to green and will propose green-for-red swaps; Alligator ($0.30) prefers green and will propose red-for-green swaps. On camel and butterfly proposals, alligator benefits and accepts while the other high-red player declines. When alligator proposes, both camel and butterfly benefit and thus both accept; the recipient is chosen at random.

Holdings after round 0 shift slightly toward camel and butterfly accumulating red and alligator accumulating green. The incentives remain the same: camel and butterfly continue to propose green-for-red swaps (beneficial to them), and alligator continues to propose red-for-green swaps (beneficial to both other players). Alligator’s proposals again draw acceptances from both non-senders; when camel or butterfly propose, only alligator accepts.

By the final round the same preference structure holds. Camel remains the strongest buyer of red and continues to trade green for red with alligator; butterfly also continues to buy red from alligator when proposing. Alligator continues to sell red for green and attracts acceptance from both non-senders; the random selection rule determines the recipient each time multiple accept.

parsed_target.game_explanation: Three players start with equal counts of two chip types (red and green) but differ in how much they privately value red: owl values red low (0.30) and green at 0.50, while dolphin and iguana value red higher (0.60) and green at 0.50. Because green is common-value across players (0.50), and two players prefer red while one prefers green, the natural trading pattern is: the green-preferrer (owl) will repeatedly offer red in exchange for green, and the red-preferrers (dolphin and iguana) will mostly offer green in exchange for red. From each player's private perspective they accept only trades that increase their own total value (they do not optimize group surplus). Uncertainty arises because players only know their own valuations and current holdings; they must infer whether a proposal is beneficial given possible competition (multiple acceptors lead to a random recipient) and whether a recipient even has the requested chips. Proposers can sometimes exploit this by adjusting quantities (e.g., offering multiple greens for one red) to make trades attractive to a specific recipient. Over three rounds with nine proposal turns, we expect many small mutually beneficial 1-for-1 swaps when the recipient benefits, occasional multi-green offers to entice a red-rich player, and some declined proposals when no recipient gains. The observed dynamics will shift holdings so that owl accumulates green (becoming green-heavy) while dolphin and iguana accumulate red, with trades constrained by chip availability and occasional randomness when multiple acceptors compete.

rounds.round_explanation: Early-game simple swaps: owl (green-preferrer) will request green and offer red; both red-preferrers (dolphin, iguana) would like to receive red in exchange for green and so will tend to accept such proposals. When dolphin proposes to buy red for green, owl (who dislikes red) will accept; iguana will decline. Expect small 1-for-1 exchanges and occasional multiple acceptances leading to random selection.

Holdings are now skewed: owl has become greener, iguana and dolphin somewhat redder. Owl continues to buy green by offering red; both red-preferrers may accept when it benefits them but often only one will. Dolphin continues buying red from owl when owl benefits (owl prefers green), and iguana will often propose modest red purchases (selling greens) that owl accepts. Expect accepted 1-2 chip swaps that further concentrate red with dolphin/iguana and green with owl.

By the final round owl has very few red chips left and many greens. Simple 1-for-1 offers from owl are still attractive to red-preferrers, but when dolphin proposes to buy red he may face recipients without red (owl) or recipients who would lose value (iguana/dolphin themselves). Anticipating that some targets lack red or would decline 1-for-1 deals, a proposer like iguana can make a lopsided offer (e.g., 2 greens for 1 red) to entice a red-holder to accept. Expect one more small green-accumulating trade for owl, one decline where no recipient benefits or can supply the requested chip, and a final multi-green-for-one-red accepted by a red-holder.

parsed_target.game_explanation: Three players start with equal endowments of red and green chips but have different private values: two players (whale-orange-8655 and swan-blue-3036) strongly prefer red (0.80) to green (0.50), while zebra-pink-5323 prefers green (0.50) to red (0.10). This creates strong asymmetric trading incentives: zebra is a natural supplier of red in exchange for green, while whale and swan are natural buyers of red using green. Proposers will typically attempt to convert lower-value chips they hold into higher-value chips they want; recipients will accept only if the deal raises their own value. Because proposers cannot choose among multiple acceptors, offers that look favorable to multiple partners risk being randomly executed with any accepting partner. Uncertainty stems from private valuations (players observe only their own values), so proposers must frame offers that look plausibly attractive to potential partners; recipients decide based on the concrete numeric gains to themselves. Over three rounds, we expect zebra to steadily accumulate green by selling red, while whale and swan accumulate red, with most trades occurring when zebra still holds redeemable red. Late in the game, once zebra's red stock is exhausted, proposals to buy red will be declined and trading will stall.

rounds.round_explanation: At the start everyone has 10 red and 10 green. Early opportunistic trades are likely: whale and swan will attempt to buy red by offering green; zebra will happily convert red to green. Proposers will pick small, clearly profitable exchange rates (e.g., 1G for 1R or 2R for 1G) that likely attract zebra's acceptance. Multiple acceptances are possible when offers are attractive to more than one player, producing random partner selection.

After round 0 zebra has already sold some red and holds more green; whale and swan increased their red holdings. Whale again proposes to buy red from the remaining red-holder (zebra) at favorable terms; zebra continues to accept beneficial conversions of low-value red for green. Zebra, when proposing, offers red for green and can attract at least one acceptor among the red-lovers. Swan continues to buy red from zebra while whale mostly declines when asked to give up red.

By round 2 zebra's red stock is nearly exhausted and holds a large green balance. Proposals to buy red are still attractive to zebra (since selling red is cheap for them) but unattractive to whale and swan when they would be asked to give up red. Once zebra's red is gone, offers to buy red will be declined by available partners and trading will stall. Zebra is forced to make some proposals even if unfavorable (selling green to buy red), but these will be declined by the red-preferers.

parsed_target.game_explanation: All three players start with identical counts of two chip types but different private valuations: every player values RED more than GREEN (turtle 1.00 vs 0.50, pig 0.90 vs 0.50, elephant 0.60 vs 0.50). This creates limited mutually beneficial trade opportunities: a proposer who wants extra RED must give up GREEN (which is worth less to them), but any prospective recipient values RED more than GREEN and so would typically decline unless compensated with multiple GREENs. As an analyst with access to true valuations, the most plausible accepted moves are those where proposers offer enough GREEN to make recipients strictly better off (e.g., 2 GREEN for 1 RED) — trades that often leave the proposer near break-even but benefit the recipient. Given simultaneous accept/decline by non-senders, proposals that make both non-senders better off will be accepted by both and the recipient chosen at random; proposals that would reduce the proposer’s own value are unlikely to be offered. Uncertainty from each individual’s private values and the random tie-breaking when multiple accept leads to some proposals that are borderline (break-even for the proposer) being used to probe others. Over three rounds, we therefore expect a small number of accepted, near break-even offers from the player who most strongly values RED (turtle) buying RED by selling multiple GREENs; most other proposals are declined because they cannot create mutual gains given monotonic private values.

rounds.round_explanation: Early in the game proposers test simple exchanges. Turtle (highest marginal value on RED) tries to convert GREEN into RED by offering 2 GREEN for 1 RED — that is enough to make both elephant and pig better off, so both accept and one is randomly chosen as recipient. Subsequent proposers (elephant, pig) propose 1 GREEN for 1 RED to try to get a marginal gain, but recipients would lose value on such trades, so those proposals are declined.

After seeing an accepted 2 GREEN → 1 RED trade, turtle repeats the same favorable-for-recipient offer. Both other players again find the deal beneficial and accept; this time pig becomes the realized recipient. Elephant again attempts a marginal 1 GREEN → 1 RED swap but faces rejection because giving up RED for a single GREEN lowers recipients' value; pig likewise proposes a similar marginal request and is rejected.

Turtle again repeats the 2 GREEN → 1 RED conversion; both non-senders would benefit and accept, and elephant is randomly selected as the recipient this time. The remaining proposers continue to attempt small 1-for-1 exchanges that would benefit only the proposer and thus are rejected by potential recipients.

parsed_target.game_explanation: All three players start with equal nominal holdings (10 red, 10 green) but different private values for red: kangaroo values red very low (0.10) and green 0.50, fox values red high (0.70) and green 0.50, gorilla values red moderately low (0.40) and green 0.50. Because green is universally 0.50, trades that move reds toward fox and greens toward kangaroo/gorilla create private gains for the participants. Proposers will typically offer chips they value less and request chips they value more (e.g., kangaroo/gorilla will offer reds to buy greens; fox will offer greens to buy reds). Responders accept if the trade raises their own total value given their private valuations. When both non-senders would accept a proposal, a random accepting player is chosen as recipient, introducing allocation uncertainty. Over three rounds this dynamic repeatedly shifts low-valued red away from kangaroo/gorilla toward fox in exchange for greens, with acceptance patterns driven by the sign and magnitude of the private gains per chip and occasional randomness when both non-senders accept.

rounds.round_explanation: Early-round incentives: kangaroo (low red value) and gorilla (moderately low red value) both prefer to trade reds for greens; fox prefers to acquire reds by offering greens. Kangaroo opens by buying green for red (appealing to fox), then fox attempts to buy red by offering green (both non-senders would accept), and gorilla tries to buy green for red — acceptance depends on which other player benefits.

With some redistribution completed, the pattern repeats. Kangaroo continues to prefer trading away red for green and targets fox as a likely accepting partner. Fox, still the highest red valuer, posts larger multi-chip offers to buy red; both kangaroo and gorilla benefit and will accept such proposals, but random selection can allocate the red to one of them. Gorilla again offers red for green expecting fox to accept.

Final round: players exploit the established pattern. Kangaroo keeps trading red for green to increase own value; fox continues to buy red, sometimes selling small amounts of green; gorilla again offers red for green expecting fox to accept. Because fox accumulates many reds, his pool of greens shrinks but he still accepts trades that raise his value. Random resolution when both non-senders accept continues to create allocation uncertainty.

strict_validation_errors: rounds[2].turns[2].recipient_id does not hold enough `RED` chips to satisfy the request.
repair_notes: rounds[2].turns[2]: downgraded accepted trade to decline because no feasible accepter remained.

parsed_target.game_explanation: Three players begin with equal counts of red and green chips but distinct private values: tiger values red very little (0.10) and green moderately (0.50), unicorn highly values red (0.80) and green at 0.50, and bat values both colors equally (0.50). Because green is common-value (0.50) for all, trades that convert low-valued red (for a given player) into green are attractive to that player but may also be attractive to others depending on their private red value. Proposers choose one-sided exchanges (request one color, offer the other). Given the asymmetry, tiger will repeatedly propose selling red for green (small quantities) to obtain positive net gains. Unicorn will repeatedly offer green for red (1-for-1) to acquire additional red that she values highly; she structures offers narrowly to avoid creating competition. Bat, whose marginal values are equal, can profit only by leveraging exchange rates (e.g., offering 1 green for 2 red) because obtaining more red than green can net him positive gains when the quantities are asymmetric. Responses are driven by each responder's private evaluation of the offered exchange (accept if the exchange strictly raises their own total chip value, decline if not). Uncertainty is present because each player observes only public history and their own values: they infer others' preferences from offers and tend to target offers that will be strictly profitable for a specific partner (to avoid multi-acceptance or being ignored). Over three rounds with the given proposer schedule, we expect a pattern where tiger and unicorn repeatedly trade 1-for-1 (unicorn buys red by selling green; tiger sells red for green), and bat occasionally executes asymmetric trades (offers 1 green for 2 red) that are strictly profitable for bat and for tiger as the recipient, but not for unicorn. All trades are small and sequential, holdings update immediately, and players focus solely on their individual payoff.

rounds.round_explanation: Early play: tiger wants to convert low-value red into green and therefore offers small 1-for-1 red->green trades to attract the player who strictly benefits (unicorn). Unicorn, who values red highly, counter-offers 1-for-1 green->red to target tiger. Bat, indifferent at 1-for-1, instead proposes an asymmetric deal (1 green for 2 red) that yields a strict gain for bat and also is attractive to tiger but not to unicorn.

Players continue the established pattern. Tiger continues small 1-for-1 red->green trades that are profitable for tiger and attractive to unicorn but not to bat. Unicorn again targets tiger with 1-for-1 green->red offers. Bat repeats an asymmetric 1 green for 2 red proposal that yields a strict gain for bat and is acceptable to tiger only.

Final round repeats the same incentives and patterns. Tiger continues to offload low-value red for green at 1-for-1; unicorn stays focused on acquiring additional red via 1-for-1 green->red offers targeted at tiger; bat again uses an asymmetric 1 green for 2 red trade that is profitable for bat and acceptable to tiger but not to unicorn. Sequentially updating holdings make these small, repeatable gains feasible until the last round.

parsed_target.game_explanation: Three players negotiate over two chip colors (RED and GREEN) across three rounds. GREEN has a common and known value (0.50) for everyone; RED is privately valued differently (dog=0.50, buffalo=0.90, cat=0.40) and only its public range (0.10–1.00) is common knowledge. This creates a clear pattern of mutually beneficial exchanges: buffalo strongly prefers RED and is willing to trade GREEN for RED; cat prefers GREEN over RED and is willing to trade RED for GREEN; dog is nearly indifferent between the two and thus is marginal to many trades. Proposers will request the chip color they privately value more and offer the color they value less, subject to anticipating whether recipients will gain privately from the swap. Recipients accept only when the trade increases their own private total value (or is acceptably neutral), and when more than one recipient would accept the proposer cannot choose among them — one accepting recipient is selected at random. Given these incentives and the public common value of GREEN, the most likely dynamic is repeated buffalo proposals that buy RED by offering GREEN (which buffalo prefers), and cat responses that accept modest one-for-one exchanges (getting GREEN > RED for her). Dog, being value-neutral, sometimes proposes symmetrical swaps or accepts neutral trades when it benefits (or is indifferent), producing a sequence where buffalo accumulates RED across accepted one-for-one GREEN-for-RED trades with cat as the frequent counterparty, while many cat-proposed RED-for-GREEN offers get declined because they are unattractive to buffalo and only marginal for dog.

rounds.round_explanation: Early round: players test the market. Dog (indifferent) tries a generous request to see if others will part with GREEN; buffalo, who values RED highly, proposes a small one-for-one GREEN→RED swap that is attractive to cat; cat tries to reciprocate but faces low acceptance because others lose from that swap.

Middle round: buffalo continues pursuing RED via one-for-one GREEN offers (these are strongly positive for buffalo and modestly positive for cat). Dog, occasionally indifferent, proposes neutral swaps that buffalo accepts when it benefits buffalo; cat keeps proposing RED→GREEN swaps but frequently fails to find willing partners except when terms directly help buffalo.

Final round: patterns crystallize. Buffalo repeatedly finds it profitable to buy RED with GREEN at roughly one-for-one terms and cat continues to be a willing counterparty when that trade benefits her. Dog remains a swing actor who sometimes accepts neutral swaps (helping buffalo) but often declines trades that don't improve its own value. Cat's own proposals remain rarely accepted because they typically require others to give up GREEN (which others value at least as much or more than the RED offered).

parsed_target.game_explanation: All three players have identical private valuations: GREEN = $0.50, RED = $0.40. Greens are strictly more valuable than reds for every player, so any trade that moves GREENs to a proposer from a recipient in exchange for REDs transfers value from the recipient to the proposer. Because players are selfish and know their own valuations, recipients will decline any trade that reduces their own total value. Proposers therefore face a tension: to acquire more GREENs they must offer enough REDs to make a counterparty better off, but offering enough REDs to compensate the recipient would usually leave the proposer worse off. There is also limited uncertainty during play: each player knows only their own values and the public ranges (RED: $0.10–$1.00, GREEN: $0.50 fixed), so proposers may attempt small, proposer-favorable swaps (e.g., 1 RED for 1 GREEN) hoping others have different private values; but given the actual (analyst) priors, such swaps are unprofitable for recipients and will be declined. The expected dynamic across three rounds is therefore repeated proposer attempts to swap lower-valued REDs for higher-valued GREENs with minimal offers, and unanimous declines by responders. No trade execution is expected in any turn.

rounds.round_explanation: Initial holdings are equal (10 RED, 10 GREEN each). Proposers will try straightforward swaps that improve their own value (buy 1 GREEN with 1 RED), but because recipients value GREEN more than RED, they will decline. Expect three declined proposal attempts in order: badger, goose, otter.

After all proposals in round 0 failed, proposers may repeat the same simple offers or modestly increase incentives. However, any offer that makes a recipient strictly better off typically makes the proposer worse off given the common valuations; thus proposers are unlikely to increase offers to the point of mutual benefit. Expect repeated 1-for-1 GREEN-for-RED attempts and declines.

In the final round proposers may make one last attempt. Given prior declines and symmetric valuations, any reasonable offer that benefits a responder will not leave the proposer better off. Anticipating this, responders will continue to decline. Expect final three declined proposals, completing the game with no executed trades.

parsed_target.game_explanation: This three-round, three-player micro-bargaining game features common-value GREEN chips (all players value GREEN at $0.50) and privately heterogeneous RED values (badger $0.20, peacock $1.00, otter $0.40). Incentives are asymmetric: peacock strongly prefers RED and is willing to pay GREEN for it; badger strongly prefers GREEN and is willing to sell RED for GREEN; otter is intermediate and will accept small favorable swaps either way. Proposers request one color and offer the other; because acceptances are simultaneous and a proposer cannot choose among multiple acceptors, proposers tend to make modest 1-for-1 offers that are likely to be profitable for at least one other player. Uncertainty for each player is which private values the others hold and who will accept; for proposers there is also the risk that multiple acceptances lead to a random partner. Over the three rounds we expect recurring 1-for-1 RED<->GREEN trades: badger and otter often swap RED for GREEN (beneficial to both badger and otter), while peacock repeatedly offers GREEN to acquire RED (beneficial to peacock and to sellers like badger/otter). Holdings update immediately, and players gradually concentrate RED with peacock and GREEN with badger/otter while each trade increases individual payoff for the accepting players given their private valuations.

rounds.round_explanation: At the start all players hold 10 RED and 10 GREEN. Badger (low RED value) leads and is likely to offer RED for GREEN to capture the higher-valued GREEN; otter (mid RED value) is the natural counterparty. Peacock will then try to buy RED by offering GREEN, a trade that both badger and otter prefer to accept; if both accept the recipient is random. Otter then has a turn and will again prefer GREEN, offering RED to a GREEN-holder (likely badger). Players do 1-for-1 swaps that are individually profitable given their private valuations.

After round 0 peacock has accumulated some RED while badger and otter hold more GREEN. Badger again prefers to convert RED into GREEN and will propose a 1-for-1 trade targeting otter; peacock will again try to buy RED by offering GREEN (both non-senders typically accept); otter's turn repeats the pattern of selling RED for GREEN to badger. Trades remain small (1-for-1) because those are reliably mutually beneficial for at least one counterparty and limit risk of being left with unfavorable holdings.

By round 2 peacock is the clearest aggregator of RED while badger and otter hold more GREEN. The same incentive pattern repeats: badger and otter continue profitable RED->GREEN swaps (badger selling RED to get GREEN; otter sometimes both sells and buys depending on sequencing), and peacock continues to offer GREEN to acquire RED. With repeated observations players can predict who will accept, so proposers stick to 1-for-1 trades that fairly reliably find at least one accepting partner. Final trades concentrate RED with peacock and GREEN with badger/otter.

parsed_target.game_explanation: All three players prefer RED to GREEN (private RED values: buffalo 0.90, camel 0.80, cat 0.70; GREEN common value 0.50). That creates a predictable pattern: proposers will generally try to trade away GREEN to acquire RED. Because two GREENs are worth 1.00 to any player (2 * 0.50), offering 2 GREEN in exchange for 1 RED is a trade that will be attractive to any counterparty whose RED value is less than 1.00 — which is true for all three players. From each individual player's viewpoint there is uncertainty about other players' exact RED valuations, but the public fact that GREEN = 0.50 and typical integer quantities means simple arithmetic guides accept/decline decisions. Proposers seek to design offers that make the recipient strictly better off (so the recipient will accept), while recipients accept any exchange that raises their own total value immediately. Given symmetric starting holdings (10 RED, 10 GREEN each) and repeated identical 2 GREEN for 1 RED offers, the game is likely to produce a sequence of accepted small exchanges that rotate REDs among players; when multiple recipients accept, the realized partner is chosen at random, so which counterparty loses a RED in any given accepted turn is unpredictable ex ante. Over three rounds with three turns each, the most likely pattern is repeated 2 GREEN for 1 RED proposals that are accepted by both potential recipients, with the realized recipient varying by random draw. Observationally this yields accepted trades on nearly every turn and holdings that oscillate but often return to similar configurations.

rounds.round_explanation: At the start, all players hold 10 RED and 10 GREEN. Proposers know their own RED preference but not others', yet the arithmetic of 2 GREEN (worth 1.00) versus 1 RED makes a 2-for-1 exchange attractive to any player whose RED value is below 1.00 (all players). Expect proposers to request 1 RED and offer 2 GREEN; both non-senders typically accept, and the realized recipient is chosen at random.

Players observe the prior accepted 2-for-1 trades and continue to face the same incentive structure. With holdings effectively back near their starting positions after the previous three accepted exchanges, proposers again can credibly offer 2 GREEN for 1 RED and expect both non-senders to accept. Random selection among acceptors determines who actually parts with a RED.

By the final round, players have seen repeated profitable 2-for-1 exchanges for recipients. There is no mechanism benefit to holding out (each accepted 2-for-1 is an immediate gain for the recipient and a calculated trade-off for the proposer), so proposers are likely to repeat the same type of offer. Expect the same pattern: 2 GREEN offered for 1 RED, both non-senders accept, and the realized recipient is randomly selected among acceptors.

parsed_target.game_explanation: All three players value green chips (common value $0.50) more than red chips (player-specific: peacock $0.40, owl $0.30, dog $0.20). That makes green the scarce/desired good and red the medium of exchange. Proposers who want greens must offer enough reds to make some recipient better off receiving reds and giving up greens. Whether a non-sender accepts depends only on their private red value: a recipient will accept an offer that gives them R reds for G greens if R*red_value - G*0.50 > 0. Proposers know their own valuations but not others', so they face uncertainty about who will accept and may have to overpay (offer extra reds) to induce at least one acceptor. Because non-senders respond simultaneously, offers that make more than one non-sender better off can result in multiple accepts and a random selection of the recipient. The forecast below uses those payoff comparisons as analyst priors to predict plausible, incentive-compatible offers (small integer quantities) and the likely accept/decline responses, producing a deterministic realized recipient when multiple players would accept.

rounds.round_explanation: Start holdings: everyone 10 RED, 10 GREEN. Peacock, Owl, and Dog all prefer GREEN to RED. To get a GREEN, a proposer must offer enough REDs so that some recipient gains value by swapping a GREEN for the offered REDs. Early in play proposers use modest premiums (2 RED for 1 GREEN) which makes the exchange attractive to mid/high red-value recipients (peacock and owl) but not to dog. These small, repeated 2-for-1 trades produce quick, local reallocation of chips and predictable acceptances.

Holdings entering round 1 reflect the pattern of earlier trades (peacock and owl have shifted some RED/ GREEN between them; dog has fewer REDs). Players repeat the successful 2-for-1 premium because it reliably entices at least one counterparty: 2 RED for 1 GREEN is attractive to peacock and/or owl (their red values 0.40 and 0.30 make 2*red - 0.50 positive) but still unattractive to dog (2*0.20 - 0.50 < 0). Proposers exploit that to secure a partner without offering larger premiums that would produce multi-accepts and randomness.

By the final round, holdings are more dispersed (some players accumulated REDs, others accumulated GREENs).
