Proposers continue to prefer small, targeted premiums (2 RED for 1 GREEN) because they remain sufficient to persuade at least one other player without creating multiple acceptances most of the time. Dog, with the lowest red value, still must offer similar premiums to attract partners; when its offers would attract multiple acceptors, the realized recipient is drawn from those who benefited (here chosen deterministically in the forecast).

parsed_target.game_explanation: All three players face the same public green value (0.50) while red has heterogeneous private values (ram 0.40, alligator 0.20, butterfly 0.50). This creates predictable incentives: players who value red less than green (alligator, ram) are willing to sell red for green; players who value red at or above green (butterfly) are less willing to sell red but may buy red if offered green. Proposers will typically request the color they value more and offer the color they value less, but they must also anticipate who will accept given private valuations. Because acceptances are simultaneous and the proposer cannot pick the partner if multiple accept, proposers sometimes aim for proposals that attract at least one accepting partner (e.g., offering green to buy red to attract alligator). Uncertainty stems from each player only knowing their own chip values (so early proposals function as signals about types) and from randomized resolution when multiple accept. Over three rounds, we expect repeated patterns: alligator reliably sells red for green, butterfly often trades when indifferent or to exploit alligator's weak valuation, and ram trades opportunistically for marginal gains on 1-for-1 exchanges.

rounds.round_explanation: Early signalling round. Ram, who prefers green to red, opens with a small 1-for-1 trade to buy green by offering red; this is only attractive to a player who values red at least as much as green (butterfly is indifferent), so butterfly accepts. Alligator then proposes a similar buy-green-for-red trade which butterfly again accepts. Butterfly, who is now slightly richer in red, proposes to buy red offering green; both ram and alligator find selling red for green profitable and accept, triggering a random selection of recipient (alligator chosen). These moves reveal alligator's willingness to sell red and butterfly's opportunistic responses.

By round start, butterfly has accumulated extra red and fewer greens; ram and alligator have shifted slightly. Ram tries again to buy green with a small 1-for-1 red offer but gets no takers (butterfly declines this time). Alligator repeats a buy-green-for-red offer and now secures a deal with ram, who gains the marginal 0.1 surplus. Butterfly then proposes to buy red for green; both other players accept (selling red for a higher-valued green), and a random recipient is selected (ram chosen). Trades continue to expose alligator as the primary red seller.

Final opportunity to reallocate. Ram again attempts a small red-for-green purchase but is rebuffed. Alligator makes one more buy-green-for-red offer and secures ram as a partner, capturing surplus. Butterfly finishes by buying red offering green; both ram and alligator accept (selling red for higher-valued green) and the randomly selected recipient is alligator. The pattern solidifies: alligator emerges as a net seller of red in exchange for green, ram opportunistically trades small amounts, and butterfly trades to exploit alligator's weak red valuation when multiple acceptances yield randomized partners.

parsed_target.game_explanation: Three-player, three-round alternating-proposer bargaining with two chip types. Greens are common-value (worth $0.50 to all). Reds are low-value to iguana (worth $0.10) but worth $0.50 to ram and dolphin. Iguana therefore strongly prefers to accumulate greens and is willing to trade away reds at steep discounts. Ram and dolphin value red and green equally, so they only accept trades that raise their own linear value (or are indifferent). Proposers request one color and offer another; because recipients respond simultaneously, proposers cannot pick among multiple acceptors. Uncertainty arises from private values (each player only knows their own red value) and from random selection when multiple accept. Given these priors, iguana will repeatedly propose trades that trade little red for multiple greens (which other players will typically decline because they lose value). Ram and dolphin will propose conversions that exchange a small number of greens for more reds (profitable to the proposer and attractive to iguana because iguana values greens much more than reds). Most accepted trades will be ones where iguana sells a small number of reds to acquire greens and where ram or dolphin offer a green for multiple reds; ram and dolphin rarely accept iguana's steep offers but accept proposals that give them net value.

rounds.round_explanation: Starting holdings: everyone 10 red / 10 green. Iguana strongly prefers green and will try to buy greens by offering cheap reds; such offers are unattractive to ram and dolphin and so are likely declined. Ram will look for profitable conversions (giving up a small number of greens to obtain more reds) and can entice dolphin to trade when dolphin benefits. Dolphin, when profitable, will subsequently try similar conversions and can often find iguana willing to accept because iguana values greens much more than reds.

Holdings after round 0: iguana ~8R/11G, ram ~13R/8G, dolphin ~9R/11G. Iguana continues to propose steep trades (few reds for multiple greens) that are unlikely to be accepted. Ram repeats profitable conversions (surrender a small number of greens to obtain more reds) and can extract gains by trading with iguana, who is willing to give up a small number of reds to get greens. Dolphin mirrors ram's successful pattern and again finds iguana a likely acceptor.

Holdings entering round 2: iguana ~4R/13G, ram ~15R/7G, dolphin ~11R/10G. Iguana's stock of reds is decreasing but she still values greens much more and will continue to propose aggressive buys (usually declined). Ram and dolphin continue to execute small-green-for-more-red conversions; iguana accepts when those conversions hand her an extra green for a small number of reds.

parsed_target.game_explanation: Three players repeatedly trade two chip types (RED and GREEN). Greens have the same objective market value (0.50) for everyone; RED is privately valued: iguana greatly prefers RED (0.80), owl moderately prefers RED (0.60), whale dislikes RED (0.10). Given these priors, the dominant trade pattern is simple: players who value RED more (iguana, owl) will propose to buy RED by offering GREEN, and the low-RED-value player (whale) will accept those offers to convert cheap RED into more valuable GREEN. Conversely, when whale proposes to buy GREEN by offering RED, both iguana and owl have an incentive to accept (they gain RED), so whale's proposals are often accepted by multiple partners and the recipient is determined randomly. Strategic uncertainty arises because each player only knows their own values and cannot condition offers on others' private payoffs; proposers must therefore propose small, plausibly mutually beneficial one-for-one swaps (1 RED for 1 GREEN) that are likely to attract at least one acceptor. Because the proposer cannot choose among multiple acceptors, whales risk giving up REDs to either iguana or owl when both accept. Over three rounds (nine turns) we forecast a steady flow of 1-for-1 RED↔GREEN trades: iguana and owl repeatedly buy RED from whale (selling GREEN), and whale repeatedly buys GREEN from whoever accepts (selling RED).

rounds.round_explanation: Early-round dynamics: proposers test simple 1-for-1 swaps. Iguana and Owl prefer RED over GREEN and will propose to buy 1 RED by offering 1 GREEN. Whale, who strongly prefers GREEN, will accept such swaps. When Whale proposes to buy GREEN by offering RED, both iguana and owl find it attractive to accept and a random accepting recipient is selected.

Mid-game: patterns reinforce prior beliefs. Iguana and Owl continue to buy RED one-for-one, and Whale continues to sell RED to acquire GREEN. Whale's purchase proposals again attract multiple acceptances; random selection delivers the GREEN to one accepting player.

End-game: incentives remain stable. Proposers stick to small fair-looking swaps (1-for-1). Whale still prefers GREEN and sells RED; iguana and owl still prefer RED and buy it. With limited rounds left, players continue to accept immediately profitable one-for-one trades rather than hold out for uncertain future gains.

parsed_target.game_explanation: Three players each start with 10 red and 10 green chips. Green chips have a common value (0.50) while red values differ by player (duck 0.10, gorilla 0.20, kangaroo 0.80). Duck and gorilla strictly prefer green to red; kangaroo strictly prefers red to green. This creates consistent opposing incentives: duck and gorilla will be willing to trade red for green, while kangaroo will be willing to trade green for red. Because proposals require the proposer to offer one color in exchange for another, trades that transfer red from a low-red-value player to kangaroo (who values red highly) or transfer green from kangaroo to duck/gorilla are mutually beneficial and thus likely to be accepted. Uncertainty arises when a proposal is attractive to more than one potential recipient (multiple acceptances lead to a random recipient), and from the fact that each player only knows their own red valuation during play; proposers must therefore make offers that are plausibly attractive to at least one partner given public common-value ranges. Over the three rounds, the forecast predicts repeated 1-for-1 (and one 2-for-2 / 2-for-1) exchanges that systematically move red toward kangaroo and green toward duck and gorilla, with occasional larger swaps when proposers exploit their bargaining position to convert multiple chips.

rounds.round_explanation: Early-round dynamics: duck and gorilla want green and will offer red; kangaroo wants red and will offer green. Proposers favor simple 1-for-1 exchanges that are minimally costly to them but attractive to at least one other player. When a proposer makes a 1R-for-1G offer, kangaroo (who values red highly) is the single likely accepter; when kangaroo offers 1G-for-1R, both duck and gorilla benefit and both are likely to accept, creating random allocation risk for the proposer.

With initial momentum favoring kangaroo accumulating red, duck continues to propose small red-for-green swaps that kangaroo accepts. Gorilla, observing that kangaroo still values additional green-to-red swaps, attempts a slightly larger swap (2 red for 2 green) to accelerate acquisition of green; kangaroo accepts. Kangaroo then exploits remaining green to trade 1 green for 2 red, an especially attractive deal for recipients, which leads to multiple acceptances and a random recipient selection.

Late-round play follows the established pattern: duck and gorilla continue to propose 1-for-1 swaps to pick up green; kangaroo, with diminishing green stock, continues to sell green for red. The final kangaroo proposal (1G for 1R) is attractive to both duck and gorilla; the random resolution awards the final red to one of them. Across rounds, red flows to kangaroo and green flows to duck and gorilla, consistent with private valuations.

parsed_target.game_explanation: This three-round, three-player bargaining game pits two players who value RED substantially above GREEN (duck: RED $1.00 vs GREEN $0.50; swan: RED $0.70 vs GREEN $0.50) against one player who values RED much less than GREEN (dolphin: RED $0.20 vs GREEN $0.50). Greens are common-value at $0.50. Rational, value-maximizing proposers will typically try to convert their lower-value chips into higher-value chips (swan and duck try to acquire RED by offering GREEN; dolphin prefers to exchange RED for GREEN). Responders accept trades only if the immediate chip-value change (using their private values) increases their own payoff. Proposers anticipate this: offers that move a GREEN to a high-RED-value player (or move RED to a low-RED-value player) are likely to be accepted. Because proposers cannot choose the recipient when multiple accept, they prefer terms that at least one likely partner will accept given public history (previous sellers/buyers are informative). Uncertainty comes from not knowing others' private RED valuations; players therefore rely on observed acceptances to infer who is willing to supply RED cheaply. Forecasts below treat each player as choosing proposals consistent with their private values and responders as accepting only strictly value-improving exchanges.

rounds.round_explanation: Opening round: dolphin is the most attractive seller of RED (low private RED value). Swan and Duck both prefer to buy RED by offering GREEN. Proposers will test 1-for-1 GREEN↔RED exchanges which are mutually profitable with dolphin but unattractive to the high-RED holder (duck or swan when asked to give RED). Dolphin will accept GREEN-for-RED offers and also try to buy GREEN by offering RED when profitable.

By now dolphin has sold some RED and holds more GREEN; he remains a likely seller of RED. Swan and Duck continue to prefer acquiring RED at 1-for-1 terms. Dolphin's offers to buy GREEN by selling RED are attractive to both buyers because they trade up from GREEN to RED. Multiple acceptances on dolphin's turn create random recipient selection.

Final round: swan and duck still gain from swapping GREEN→RED at 1-for-1; dolphin has fewer RED left but continues to sell RED for GREEN when it improves his own value. Given limited remaining rounds, proposers stick to simple 1-for-1 exchanges that have historically been accepted by dolphin, avoiding riskier multi-chip deals that could be declined.

parsed_target.game_explanation: Three players face repeated bilateral-proposal turns where proposers request one color and offer another. Bear and Pig privately value RED above GREEN (0.60 vs 0.50) and therefore prefer to acquire RED by offering GREEN; Hamster values RED very low (0.10) and GREEN at 0.50, so Hamster is the natural supplier of RED in exchange for GREEN. Because acceptances are simultaneous and proposers cannot choose among multiple acceptors, proposers will tend to craft trades that are strictly profitable for at least one counterparty. Observationally, this creates a steady pattern: Bear and Pig repeatedly propose to buy small amounts of RED in exchange for GREEN (which is mildly costly for them but very attractive for Hamster), and Hamster accepts those proposals because they raise Hamster's own value. Hamster's own proposals—to buy GREEN by selling RED—look beneficial from Hamster's private perspective but would be value-decreasing for Bear and Pig, so those proposals are likely to be declined. Uncertainty comes from private valuations and the random tie-break if multiple players accept; however given the asymmetry in RED values, multiple acceptances are unlikely (Bear and Pig typically decline trades that give away RED), so most executed trades are between the proposer (Bear or Pig) and Hamster.

rounds.round_explanation: Initial endowments are equal (10 red, 10 green each). Bear moves first and, valuing RED more than GREEN, offers GREEN for RED. Hamster (who values RED much less) finds such trades attractive and accepts; Pig declines. Pig then makes a similar proposal and likewise trades with Hamster. When Hamster proposes to buy GREEN by selling RED, Bear and Pig would lose value by accepting, so Hamster's proposal is declined.

With updated holdings (Bear and Pig a bit heavier on RED, Hamster heavier on GREEN), incentives remain the same. Bear again offers GREEN for RED; Hamster prefers the exchange and accepts. Pig repeats the same trade pattern and also trades with Hamster. Hamster's attempt to sell RED for GREEN is again unattractive to Bear and Pig and is declined.

The pattern repeats: Bear and Pig each make small offers trading GREEN for RED which remain attractive to Hamster and are accepted. Hamster's own proposal to sell RED for GREEN continues to be value-reducing for the other two and is declined. Over the three rounds, Bear and Pig gradually accumulate additional RED at the expense of Hamster increasing their GREEN holdings modestly, consistent with their private valuations.

parsed_target.game_explanation: This three-round, three-player bargaining game pits one low-red-value player (turtle-yellow-7601, RED=0.10, GREEN=0.50) against two high-red-value players (whale-green-1798 and swan-pink-1424, RED=0.90, GREEN=0.50). Greens have a common value (0.50) for everyone. Because each player cares only about their own total chip value, mutually beneficial exchanges trade low-valued chips for high-valued chips from each individual perspective: turtle benefits by selling RED to acquire GREEN; whale and swan benefit by selling GREEN to acquire RED. Proposers can only offer one color and request a different color; responders accept if the exchange raises their own value given their private value. Uncertainty arises because responders do not know others' private values and because when multiple responders accept the proposer’s partner is chosen at random. As a result, we expect a steady pattern across rounds: turtle will repeatedly propose to sell RED for GREEN and attract acceptance from whale and/or swan; whale and swan will typically propose selling GREEN for RED and attract acceptance from turtle. When both non-senders accept a proposal, the recipient is randomly chosen, which introduces allocation noise but not strategic deviation from these mutually beneficial 1-for-1 exchanges.

rounds.round_explanation: Start holdings: everyone begins with 10 RED and 10 GREEN. Turtle (low RED value) has incentive to sell RED for GREEN; whale and swan (high RED value) want RED and will sell GREEN. Early offers are small 1-for-1 exchanges because each 1-for-1 yields a 0.40 gain to both sides (turtle and a high-red player) or to the high-red player when selling GREEN for RED.

By round 1, roles are reinforced: turtle has fewer RED and more GREEN and continues to prefer selling RED for GREEN; whale and swan continue to prefer acquiring RED by offering GREEN. Small 1-for-1 trades remain attractive and likely to be accepted whenever the responder stands to gain (i.e., turtle will accept GREEN-for-RED offers; whale/swan will accept RED-for-GREEN offers). Random selection can determine which high-red player receives turtle's offers when both accept.

Final round: holdings have polarized further (turtle with many GREEN, others with many RED). Incentives remain the same, so similar 1-for-1 proposals occur. When both high-red players are asked to give GREEN for RED by turtle, both still benefit and may accept, leaving the recipient to random selection. Where a proposer requests RED in exchange for GREEN, turtle is typically the only acceptor because giving RED for GREEN is beneficial for turtle but not for the other high-red player.

parsed_target.game_explanation: This three-round, three-player bargaining game centers on one-for-one exchanges between red and green chips. Greens are common-value (0.50 for everyone). Reds are privately valued: hedgehog values red low (0.20) and thus prefers to accumulate greens; frog (0.70) and badger (0.60) prefer reds. Proposers will therefore mostly offer chips of the color they value less and ask for the color they value more (hedgehog: sell RED to buy GREEN; frog and badger: sell GREEN to buy RED). Responders decide based on their own private valuations: trades that increase their personal chip value (e.g., giving GREEN to receive RED for frog/badger) are accepted, while trades that reduce value are declined. Key uncertainties drive behavior: each player knows only their own values (so proposers cannot condition offers on others' exact preferences) and if multiple responders accept a proposal the actual recipient is chosen at random, creating risk for the proposer. Given these incentives and the provided valuations, most proposals are small 1-for-1 exchanges; hedgehog's offers to trade RED for GREEN attract acceptance from the other two players (both gain from swapping GREEN for RED), while frog's and badger's offers to trade GREEN for RED typically attract acceptance from hedgehog but are declined by the other red-preferrers. The forecast assumes modest, safe 1-for-1 offers and resolves multi-accept situations by a single random recipient (specified in the forecast).

rounds.round_explanation: Early in the game everyone still has many chips. Hedgehog (low red value) will propose selling RED to buy GREEN; both frog and badger (who prefer RED) benefit from giving GREEN to get RED and so are likely to accept such 1-for-1 offers. Frog and badger will propose the opposite (sell GREEN for RED); hedgehog benefits and will accept those, while the other red-preferrer will decline. Expect mostly single-chip 1-for-1 trades with occasional multiple-accept outcomes (random recipient).

Holdings have shifted but incentives remain: hedgehog still prefers greens and will keep offering 1 RED for 1 GREEN, often attracting both other players (so a random recipient). Frog continues to buy RED with GREEN and mostly gets hedgehog to accept; badger likewise offers GREEN for RED but only hedgehog typically accepts. Players avoid demanding unfavorable exchange rates because they know responders have unilateral accept/decline power.

Final round incentives mirror earlier rounds but players have fewer chips of their less-preferred color to trade. Hedghog remains willing to exchange RED for GREEN and still attracts acceptance from frog and badger; frog continues to buy RED using GREEN and hedgehog accepts those offers. Badger's attempts to buy RED with GREEN are again most likely accepted only by hedgehog. Expect more 1-for-1 exchanges and random recipient outcomes when both non-senders accept hedgehog's offers.

parsed_target.game_explanation: Three players repeatedly bargain by offering to swap chips of two colors. Green chips are common-value (0.50) while red chips have heterogeneous private values (mouse 0.30, tiger 1.00, unicorn 0.80). Each proposer will generally try to trade away chips they value less and acquire chips they value more, while responders accept only if the swap raises their own private value. Uncertainty comes from (1) not knowing opponents' private red valuations, only observing public history and holdings, and (2) the random resolution if multiple players accept a proposal. Expect tiger (high red value) to consistently try to buy reds by offering greens, mouse (low red value) to try to sell reds for greens, and unicorn to behave in between. Early offers that are very favorable to potential recipients can attract multiple acceptances and become randomly allocated; proposers anticipate this when setting terms but cannot pick the partner.

rounds.round_explanation: Opening round: proposers test others' willingness to swap. Mouse will attempt to offload red for green; tiger will place a generous buy of reds (offering little green) to capture reds from someone; unicorn will opportunistically buy reds from mouse. Early trades shift holdings so later proposers face updated inventories.

After initial reallocations, mouse continues to offer 1-for-1 swaps (red for green). Tiger, with increased red holdings, still prefers buying red and sets 1-for-1 terms that only the mid-valuing unicorn will accept. Unicorn repeatedly targets mouse's cheap red when possible. Acceptances reflect private value gains for each responder.

Endgame: players continue giving up relatively low-value chips for higher-value ones. Mouse repeatedly trades red for green, often attracting both other players; random tie-breaking allocates some of those gains to tiger. Tiger keeps buying red at 1-for-1 terms when it can extract surplus from mouse. Unicorn continues to pick up reds where mouse is willing to sell.

parsed_target.game_explanation: Three rounds of alternating proposals between parrot-blue-2249, fox-orange-371, and bear-orange-314 will center on the red/green value asymmetry. Green is a common-value chip worth $0.50 to everyone; red is worth $0.90 to fox but only $0.20 to parrot and bear. That makes mutually beneficial exchanges those where fox gives green to acquire red, and parrot/bear give red to acquire green. Parrot and bear both prefer green to red and therefore will propose trades that ask for green and offer red; fox prefers red and will propose trades asking for red and offering green. Because responders act simultaneously and the proposer cannot choose among multiple accepters, fox risks a random partner when both parrot and bear accept his offers. Uncertainty for each player comes from not observing others' private red valuations at the start; however the pattern of accepted trades quickly reveals that fox highly values red, producing repeated red-for-green transfers from parrot/bear toward fox across the three rounds. Each player acts to maximize their own chip-value gain per trade.

rounds.round_explanation: At the start all players hold 10 red and 10 green. Parrot and bear prefer green (0.50) over red (0.20) and will therefore request green offering red; fox prefers red (0.90) and will request red offering green. Early trades are expected to flow red -> fox and green -> parrot/bear. Parrot's opening proposal to buy green for red is likely accepted by fox (who values red highly). Fox's subsequent proposal to buy red (offering green) is attractive to both parrot and bear, generating multiple acceptances and a random recipient. Bear's closing proposal for the round will again be attractive to fox.

With public history showing fox acquiring reds in round 0, parrot and bear continue to request greens offering reds. Fox keeps accepting such offers to accumulate red. When fox proposes to buy red (offering green), both parrot and bear remain willing to sell red for green, so fox typically faces multiple accepters and a random recipient; this repeats the round structure and further concentrates red with fox.

By the final round fox has accumulated many reds and few greens; parrot and bear have more greens. Parrot and bear continue to propose red-for-green trades (buy green selling red) and fox continues to accept, while fox's proposals to buy red still attract acceptance from both parrot and bear. The pattern results in further red transfers to fox and green concentrates with parrot/bear.

parsed_target.game_explanation: Three players hold two chip types with fixed common green value (0.50) and heterogeneous red values (goose 0.90, buffalo 0.50, monkey 0.10). Greens are a stable medium; reds are privately valued and contested. Goose strictly prefers reds, monkey strictly prefers greens (dislikes reds), and buffalo is indifferent between the two. Proposers can request one color and offer another; recipients decide privately based on their own chip values and current holdings. Key dynamics: (1) Proposers who value red highly (goose) will repeatedly try to trade greens for reds; (2) the low-red-value player (monkey) is a natural counterparty, repeatedly willing to give up reds for greens; (3) buffalo’s indifference means it only accepts trades that are strictly beneficial to itself. Uncertainty arises from private valuations (players cannot observe others' red values), from limited holdings (a player may lack the requested chips and thus cannot accept), and from random selection when multiple recipients accept. Over three rounds, we expect repeated small exchanges where goose and buffalo attempt to buy reds by offering greens and monkey accepts until monkey runs low on reds; later proposals that would require recipients to give up reds become less likely to execute because monkey’s red stock is depleted and buffalo/goose will decline unattractive offers. Proposers who cannot create a strictly profitable offer will often propose trades that are unattractive to others (and therefore decline) to avoid harming themselves.

rounds.round_explanation: Early-round trades: all players start with ample reds and greens. Goose (high red value) will ask for reds and offer greens; monkey (low red value) is the natural acceptor. Buffalo may also try a similar buy when it looks profitable. Monkey can at first accept several offers to swap reds for greens. When multiple recipients find a deal attractive, random selection determines who trades.

Mid-game: monkey’s red stock has started to decline but can still accept profitable swaps. Goose continues to demand reds by offering greens; buffalo repeats successful buy patterns that benefited it previously when monkey still has reds. Monkey sometimes proposes trades that would be unfavorable to itself and thus frames offers that are unattractive to others (to avoid execution) when it would otherwise lose value.

Late-game: monkey has been depleted of reds and can no longer supply the common counterparty role. Proposals that request reds now cannot be accepted by monkey, and buffalo/goose will decline many offers that would make them worse off. Proposers who cannot secure strictly profitable exchanges tend to propose offers that are unlikely to be accepted (so trades are declined and holdings remain unchanged).

parsed_target.game_explanation: Three players each start with 10 red and 10 green chips. Cat and Rabbit value red more than green (Cat: RED=0.90, GREEN=0.50; Rabbit: RED=0.80, GREEN=0.50), while Dog values green more than red (Dog: RED=0.40, GREEN=0.50). Proposers request some quantity of one color and offer some quantity of the other; recipients decide privately based on their own chip values and current holdings. Because Dog prefers green, Dog is the most willing seller of red in exchanges that pay enough green; Cat and Rabbit only sell red if offered sufficiently more green to offset their higher red valuations. Conversely, Cat and Rabbit are good targets for Dog when Dog offers red for green. Uncertainty comes from (1) each player seeing only their private valuations, so they cannot be sure how generous an offer must be to induce another to accept, and (2) when both non-senders accept, the actual recipient is randomly chosen. Given these incentives, the likely pattern is repeated small exchanges where Cat and Rabbit repeatedly buy red from Dog by offering green, and Dog occasionally proposes but is often declined when buying red (since Dog would be worse off). Trades tend to be accepted when the recipient's gain (value received minus value given) is positive; otherwise they decline. The forecast below uses the players' private values as analyst priors to predict proposals, accept/decline choices, and how holdings evolve over the three rounds.

rounds.round_explanation: Early in the game all players have equal endowments. Cat and Rabbit prefer red; Dog prefers green. Cat and Rabbit will attempt to buy red by offering green; Dog is the natural seller of red. Small 2-for-2 or 1-for-1 swaps are sufficient to make Dog better off when selling red, but Cat and Rabbit would decline those unless they receive enough green.

Holdings have shifted so Dog holds more green and fewer red; Cat and Rabbit hold more red. Cat and Rabbit keep buying red from Dog. Dog continues to accept modest offers to sell red for green because it increases his value. When Dog proposes to buy green by offering red, both Cat and Rabbit will usually accept small swaps since they gain higher-value red.

By now Dog has run low on red and is rich in green. Cat continues to buy the remaining red from Dog while offering green. Rabbit may need to offer a slightly larger green payment to induce Cat to sell red because Cat holds more red and values it highly. Dog, now with few or no red, may attempt to buy red (by selling green) but such proposals are likely to be declined because recipients would lose higher-valued red for lower-valued green.

parsed_target.game_explanation: This three-round, three-proposer bargaining game centers on trade between two chip types: GREEN (common value $0.50 to everyone) and RED (private values vary by player). Otter and Squid both value RED substantially more than GREEN (Otter RED=0.90, Squid RED=0.80) so they prefer accumulating RED and selling GREEN. Ram values RED much less than GREEN (Ram RED=0.10), so he prefers selling RED to acquire GREEN. Because trades require the proposer to request one color and give the other, mutually beneficial 1-for-1 exchanges (one red for one green) create straightforward gains for both sides depending on who is asked to give which chip. Key incentives and dynamics: proposers who want RED will offer GREEN and target Ram (who gains by giving RED for GREEN); proposers who want GREEN (Ram) will offer RED and typically attract both Otter and Squid (both gain by giving GREEN for RED), creating competition and randomness over who receives the trade. Uncertainty arises because each player only knows their own private RED valuation; players accept or decline based on immediate self-gain from the proposed exchange, and proposers cannot choose which accepting partner is realized when multiple accept. Over repeated rounds, observed accept/decline behavior provides signals about others' preferences, but the immediate incentives make 1-for-1 exchanges the most likely focal offers throughout the game.

rounds.round_explanation: Round 0 establishes the pattern: Otter (high RED value) will propose buying RED by offering GREEN and is likely to attract Ram's acceptance; Ram (prefers GREEN) will propose buying GREEN by offering RED and will typically get acceptance from both Otter and Squid (leading to a random recipient); Squid will again buy RED offering GREEN and is likely to get Ram to accept. Proposers use small 1-for-1 exchanges that are immediately Pareto-improving for at least one counterparty given private values.

Round 1 repeats the established incentives. Otter again targets RED (offering GREEN) and Ram again prefers GREEN-for-RED trades; Ram's proposal to buy GREEN by offering RED draws both Otter and Squid, producing competition and a random recipient. Squid's buy-RED proposals continue to be attractive to Ram. Quantities remain small (1-for-1) because those trades clearly increase the acceptors' own chip-value given private valuations.

Round 2 continues the same steady pattern. Proposers stick to conservative, mutually beneficial 1-for-1 exchanges. Otter and Squid repeatedly try to buy RED by offering GREEN and primarily attract Ram. Ram's proposals to buy GREEN by offering RED again draw acceptance from both high-RED-value players, leading to random allocation when both accept. The repeated acceptances communicate consistent underlying preferences (even though players only directly know their own private RED value).

parsed_target.game_explanation: This three-round, three-player bargaining game features asymmetric private values: camel-pink-767 strongly prefers RED (0.80) over GREEN (0.50), while butterfly-yellow-2139 and alligator-orange-9612 prefer GREEN (0.50) over RED (0.20). That creates clear bilateral gains-from-trade opportunities: camel wants to acquire RED and is willing to give GREEN; the other two players are typically willing to give RED to receive GREEN. Proposers must request one color and offer another, and responders decide based on their private marginal values and current holdings. Uncertainty comes from (1) private valuations — each player only knows their own values and must infer others' preferences from observed trades — and (2) the random selection rule when multiple responders accept (the proposer cannot choose the recipient). As the game progresses, holdings shift and some trades that were mutually beneficial (e.g., giving RED to get GREEN) remain attractive to the low-RED-value players, while trades that require giving GREEN to get RED are usually rejected by those players. Camel will repeatedly propose to buy RED by offering GREEN; butterfly and alligator will typically propose the mirror trade (buy GREEN offering RED). When only one responder stands to gain, that responder will be the sole acceptor and the trade executes deterministically; when both stand to gain, both accept and a random acceptance resolves who receives the trade. The overall dynamic is repeated small exchanges of RED-for-GREEN that transfer low-value RED from butterfly/alligator to camel in exchange for GREEN, increasing camel's RED holdings over the three rounds.

rounds.round_explanation: Opening round: all players start symmetric in quantities (10 RED, 10 GREEN). Camel, who values RED much more, opens by offering GREEN for RED. Both butterfly and alligator benefit by giving RED to receive GREEN, so both accept; the recipient is selected randomly. Subsequent proposers (butterfly then alligator) mirror the same profitable RED→GREEN trades whenever it increases their own payoff; when both potential partners would gain, both accept and random selection picks the recipient; when only one would gain, only that player accepts and receives the trade.

Mid-game: holdings have shifted in favor of camel having more RED. Butterfly continues to propose RED→GREEN swaps (buy GREEN offering RED), which are profitable for camel and possibly alligator depending on holdings. Camel, who gains by receiving RED, often accepts other players' offers even though he is the frequent buyer. Camel's own proposals to buy RED by offering GREEN begin to face resistance because butterfly/alligator will not give GREEN for RED (they value GREEN more), so such proposals are likely to be declined unless the proposer offers more attractive terms.

Endgame: patterns are reinforced. Camel accumulates RED through multiple accepted offers from the other two players. Camel's own GREEN-for-RED proposals typically fail because butterfly and alligator prefer to keep GREEN. Butterfly and alligator continue to propose RED→GREEN swaps; when both non-senders would gain by accepting, both accept and random selection determines the recipient; when only one gains, that player alone accepts and receives the trade. The net result is further transfer of low-value RED to camel in exchange for GREEN.

parsed_target.game_explanation: Three players negotiate pairwise chip swaps over three rounds. Greens are common-value (all players value them at $0.50) while Reds have heterogeneous private values (owl low at $0.20, iguana high at $0.70, dolphin medium at $0.60). Because each proposer must request one color and offer the other, proposers will offer trades that increase their own value: owl will repeatedly offer red in exchange for green (sell RED, buy GREEN), while iguana and dolphin will offer green in exchange for red (sell GREEN, buy RED). Recipients decide privately based on their own valuations: a recipient will accept a 1-for-1 GREEN→RED trade if the red they would receive is worth more to them than the green they give up, and accept a RED→GREEN trade if green is worth more than the red they give up. Given the known private values used as analyst priors, owl's RED→GREEN proposals are attractive to both iguana and dolphin (both prefer receiving RED to giving GREEN), whereas iguana's and dolphin's GREEN→RED proposals are attractive to owl but not to the other high-red player (who would lose value by giving up red). With simultaneous acceptance and random selection when multiple accept, owl's offers are likely to draw multiple acceptances and be allocated randomly among the two high-red players; iguana's and dolphin's offers are likely to be accepted only by owl. Over the three rounds this drives reds toward the higher-value players (iguana and dolphin) and greens toward owl. The forecast below uses these priors to predict concrete 1-for-1 proposals and acceptance decisions (assuming minimal increments of 1 chip per trade).

rounds.round_explanation: Initial endowments are balanced (10/10 each). Owl prefers greens strongly over reds and will propose RED→GREEN swaps that both other players find profitable to accept. Iguana prefers reds strongly and will propose GREEN→RED swaps that owl (who dislikes red) will accept but dolphin will decline. Dolphin's GREEN→RED proposals similarly will be accepted by owl only. Expect owl's offers to draw both accepts and be randomly allocated, while iguana's and dolphin's offers go to owl.

After round 0 owl has begun accumulating greens while iguana and dolphin have accumulated some reds. The incentive pattern remains the same: owl's RED→GREEN proposals are attractive to both high-red players and will typically get multiple accepts and a random recipient; iguana's and dolphin's GREEN→RED proposals are attractive to owl only (since owl values red least), so those offers will execute with owl as recipient.

By round 3 the stock of reds has shifted toward iguana and dolphin while owl has accumulated many greens. Strategic incentives and accept/decline logic are unchanged: owl's offers attract both high-red players and may be randomly allocated between them; iguana's and dolphin's offers continue to be accepted primarily by owl. With 1-for-1 swaps each accepted trade further concentrates reds with the higher-value players and greens with owl.

parsed_target.game_explanation: Three players start with equal endowments but different private values: whale-orange prefers GREEN (0.50) to RED (0.30), zebra-pink slightly prefers RED (0.60) to GREEN (0.50), and swan-blue strongly prefers RED (1.00) to GREEN (0.50). GREEN has a common and known value (0.50) while RED is uncertain within a public range (0.10–1.00). Proposers can ask for one color and offer the other; because valuations are linear per chip, simple 1-for-1 exchanges are the clearest mutually beneficial moves. Whale will consistently try to trade away RED to accumulate GREEN; zebra and swan will try to trade away GREEN to accumulate RED. Recipients decide based only on their private marginal chip values: they accept if the incoming chip is worth more than the outgoing chip to them. That creates many trades that are mutually beneficial (both sides gain), so most 1-for-1 proposals where the proposer demands their preferred color will be accepted by at least one other player. When both non-senders would accept, the realized partner is chosen at random; this mainly matters when whale offers RED-for-GREEN (both zebra and swan benefit) or when swan offers GREEN-for-RED (both zebra and whale can benefit in different cases). Over the three rounds, we expect the whale to progressively accumulate GREEN by selling RED to others, while zebra and swan accumulate RED by selling GREEN; whale's RED holdings shrink while its GREEN holdings grow, and zebra/swan's GREEN holdings shrink while RED holdings increase. Uncertainty about other players' exact RED valuations only affects which non-sender accepts when both would benefit, resolved by random selection.

rounds.round_explanation: Early stage: all players have plenty of both colors. Whale will propose to exchange RED for GREEN (to capture its preferred GREEN); both zebra and swan prefer receiving RED for giving GREEN so they will accept whale's fair 1-for-1 offer. Zebra's proposal to buy RED with GREEN will be acceptable to whale (who prefers GREEN) but not to swan (who highly values RED), so zebra's trade should execute with whale. Swan's similar proposal will be accepted by whale but not zebra, so swan's trade also executes with whale. These moves start shifting RED toward zebra/swan and GREEN toward whale.

With updated holdings, the same incentives persist. Whale continues to offer RED for GREEN; both zebra and swan still benefit from receiving RED and thus will accept when offered fair 1-for-1 terms (leading to occasional randomization when both accept). Zebra's buy-RED offers are attractive to whale (who prefers GREEN) but unattractive to swan, so zebra's proposals will again execute with whale. Swan's buy-RED proposals will typically find whale accepting (zebra will usually decline). The pattern accelerates the transfer of RED to zebra/swan and GREEN to whale.

Late stage: holdings are now noticeably shifted (whale has more GREEN, others more RED). The same single-chip 1-for-1 exchanges remain mutually profitable in the same directions: whale sells remaining RED to acquire GREEN; zebra and swan continue selling GREEN to obtain RED. When both non-senders would accept (e.g., whale offers RED-for-GREEN), a random acceptor is selected — this is the only remaining source of allocation uncertainty. By the end, whale has few RED and many GREEN; zebra and swan hold most RED.

parsed_target.game_explanation: All three players start with the same physical endowment (10 red, 10 green) but different private values for red: pig and elephant value red at $0.40 and green at $0.50, while turtle values red much lower at $0.10 and green at $0.50. This creates a persistent trading opportunity for the player who treats red as cheap (turtle): by offering a small number of red chips in exchange for green chips, turtle can increase their own total value while giving a net gain to any counterparty who values red at $0.40. Pig and elephant both prefer green to red, so they will not give up green cheaply; they will decline one-for-one swaps (lose 0.50 to gain 0.40) but will accept multi-red offers that more than compensate them. Because the proposer cannot choose among multiple acceptors, turtle faces some partner-selection risk (random assignment if multiple accept). Pig and elephant have little prospect of profitable proposals that other players will accept: any proposal by pig or elephant that buys green by offering more than one red per green would hurt the proposer (since their red is relatively valuable to themselves), and one-for-one proposals will typically be declined. Observational uncertainty arises because each player only knows their own chip values, so accept/decline decisions are taken privately and proposers must infer others' valuations from past behavior. Over three rounds, we therefore expect turtle to repeatedly propose trades that sell a few reds for one green (profitable to turtle and attractive to pig/elephant), while pig and elephant's proposals are likely to be declined.

rounds.round_explanation: Early in the game all players hold equal quantities. Pig and elephant both prefer green to red but cannot credibly buy green from others without overpaying for themselves. Turtle can profitably buy green by selling multiple reds (because red is cheap for turtle). Turtle's best trade is to offer 2 reds for 1 green: it increases turtle's value and creates a positive surplus for any counterparty who values red at $0.40, so pig and elephant are likely to accept. The proposer-selection rule (random recipient if multiple accept) creates some partner risk for turtle.

After the first round turtle has converted some low-value reds into greens and remains motivated to repeat the same profitable swap. Pig and elephant have both seen that accepting 2R->1G is beneficial ex post (they receive higher-valued red chips), so when turtle proposes the same deal again it will attract acceptances from both potential partners and be executed with a random recipient. Pig and elephant still cannot make offers that will be accepted without hurting themselves, so their proposals tend to be declined.

Turtle continues exploiting the arbitrage: selling a couple of low-valued reds for a green remains strictly profitable for turtle and still attractive to pig and elephant. The proposer risk (random assignment among multiple acceptors) persists, so turtle accepts randomness in exchange for repeated gains. Pig and elephant continue to lack mutually acceptable profitable proposals and therefore their turns largely produce declined offers.

parsed_target.game_explanation: All three players start with 10 red and 10 green chips. Greens are common-valued at $0.50 for everyone; reds are private-valued and higher than green for every player (fox 0.60, kangaroo 1.00, gorilla 0.90). Because every player values red more than green, each player's own incentive as a proposer is to acquire more red by offering green (a 1-for-1 green->red trade benefits any proposer whose red value exceeds $0.50). However, any recipient who would have to give up red in exchange for green loses value unless compensated at a rate of roughly 2 greens per red (because 2*0.50 >= 1*red_value for red_value up to 1.00). That creates a structural conflict: proposers want 1-for-1 (or similar) deals that increase their own value, but such deals are value-decreasing for potential partners, so they will typically decline. Players know only their own private red value during play, so early proposals are plausibly optimistic (e.g., 1 green for 1 red), but public rejections provide information that others also value red and will not sell it cheaply. Given the analyst-side priors (the actual private values), the predicted path is that each proposer will attempt simple green-for-red offers that improve their own payoff (most likely 1 green for 1 red) and all non-senders will decline, producing no executed trades across the three rounds and nine proposal turns.

rounds.round_explanation: At the start players have no extra information about others' red valuations beyond public ranges. Each proposer is motivated to buy red by offering green (a 1-for-1 exchange increases the proposer's value given their private red value), so early offers are likely simple 1 GREEN -> 1 RED proposals. Potential recipients decline because surrendering red for a single green reduces their own value.

After seeing the first-round rejections, proposers update beliefs that others value red relatively highly. Still, each proposer individually prefers acquiring red (their own red value exceeds green), so they are likely to repeat straightforward offers (again 1 GREEN for 1 RED). Given observed rejections and private incentives to refuse, non-senders continue to decline, so no trades execute.

By the final round, public history (multiple declines) reinforces that trading partners demand much better compensation to give up red. Proposers still prefer additional red individually, so may attempt one more 1-for-1 or slightly larger offers, but absent a proposer willing to make an offer that leaves them worse off (e.g., 2 greens for 1 red would hurt fox and gorilla given their own red values), recipients continue to decline. The analyst forecast is zero executed trades by the end of the game.

parsed_target.game_explanation: Three players each start with 10 red and 10 green chips. Green has a common value of $0.50 to everyone; red is privately valued higher than green for every player (tiger $0.80, bat $0.90, unicorn $0.60). Each proposer can request one color and offer the other; acceptors exchange the requested color for the offered color. A trade benefits the proposer if they obtain a chip type they value more than the one they give away; it benefits an acceptor if the offered chips they receive are worth more to them than the chips they give up. Given the private values, no mutually beneficial 1-for-1 swap exists: everyone values red strictly above green, so an acceptor giving red and receiving one green would lose value. To induce acceptance a proposer would need to offer multiple greens per red, but that would cost the proposer more in value than the red is worth to them (two greens equal $1.00, exceeding any player's red value). Therefore, absent off-equilibrium dynamics or mistakes, proposers who try to buy red by offering green will increase their own payoff on paper but will anticipate rejection by others. The dominant forecast for this single standalone game is that all straightforward proposals (e.g., buying RED for 1 GREEN) will be declined; uncertainty remains only about whether any player will propose a loss-making deal to themselves to incentivize a partner, but such moves are unlikely for self-interested players. The public history and holdings will update only when trades are accepted; here we forecast no accepted trades across the three rounds.

rounds.round_explanation: Early stage: each player prefers red to green. Proposers will try simple profitable-for-them trades (request RED, offer GREEN) but expect others to decline because recipients would lose value by swapping a higher-valued red for a lower-valued green. Anticipated behavior: proposers present 1-for-1 RED-for-GREEN offers and both non-senders decline.

After a round of declined 1-for-1 proposals, proposers face the same incentives and public history: red remains more valuable to all players than green, so acceptors still would not trade red for single greens. Proposers could attempt to offer larger quantities of green to entice acceptance, but any multi-green offer sufficient to compensate acceptors would cost proposers more than the red is worth to them. Forecast: the same pattern repeats — 1-for-1 RED-for-GREEN offers and declines.

Final round: incentives remain unchanged. Any proposer who tries to make a trade that would be attractive enough for a recipient (e.g., offering 2 greens for 1 red) would hurt themselves because two greens ($1.00) exceed any player's red value. Given self-interest and the observed rejections so far, players will likely not propose loss-making packages, and non-senders will continue to decline 1-for-1 RED-for-GREEN offers. Forecast: all proposals declined; no trades executed across the game.

parsed_target.game_explanation: Three players start with equal supplies of red, blue, and green chips but have different private values for red and blue (green is common-value). Each proposer can offer one color in exchange for another; recipients decide privately whether the trade increases their own total value. Expect proposers to offer chips they value relatively less in order to acquire chips they value more, and to target partners who value the requested color least (so they are most likely to give it up). Because proposers cannot choose the recipient when multiple players accept, offers that are attractive to exactly one counterparty are safer. Uncertainty arises from (a) each player only knowing their own valuations (so proposers form beliefs about who will accept), and (b) the random resolution when multiple players accept. Over three rounds with the given values, the plausible dynamic is repeated small exchanges: players who highly value blue (bear, buffalo) try to acquire it by offering greens or combinations that leave sellers better off; players who value green equally may accept trades that convert low-value blue/red into higher-value green; bear, who values both blue and red relatively highly, participates when red-for-green swaps or green-for-red swaps are slightly advantageous. Traders repeatedly convert low-value chips into higher-value ones for themselves while the other players accept only when trades strictly improve their own holdings.

rounds.round_explanation: Early in the game proposers try simple one-for-one swaps. Buffalo prefers blue over green and will offer green to buy blue, expecting the player who values blue least (goose) to accept. Bear can profitably trade a single blue for two reds (or vice versa), and will structure offers to attract buffalo. Goose has the weakest blue/red values and will accept conversions that increase green holdings; when goose proposes, bear (who values red > green) is the natural counterparty.

With updated holdings, players repeat straightforward profitable swaps. Buffalo again trades a green for a blue, targeting goose. Bear follows the same pattern, using green to buy blue from goose. Goose continues to convert reds into greens by offering red for green; bear, who values red slightly more than green, accepts those offers.

Final round behavior mirrors earlier rounds: buffalo and bear continue acquiring blue by offering greens (which they value less than blue), and goose repeatedly converts low-value chips into greens. Because these one-for-one swaps are strictly beneficial for the accepting counterparty or for the proposer (and at least weakly beneficial for one recipient), they all succeed without contested acceptances.

parsed_target.game_explanation: Three players start with identical holdings but different private values for blue chips (otter low, peacock medium, cat high); red is uniformly most valuable and green is a common-value intermediate. Proposers will try to trade away chips they value relatively less (typically blue for otter) to acquire chips they value more (red or green). Acceptance decisions depend on the change in the responder's own value: a responder accepts if the value of the chip they would receive exceeds the value of the chip they give up (indifference tends to produce declines in our forecast). Uncertainty for each player comes from not knowing others' private blue valuations, so proposers typically offer just enough of a low-valued chip to make one likely partner strictly better off while not overpaying. Randomization can matter if multiple responders strictly prefer the deal, but in these forecasts proposers size offers to attract one specific partner (the high-blue cat or the blue-averse otter) so trades execute cleanly.

rounds.round_explanation: Early stage: proposers try low-risk, small conversions. Otter seeks to convert low-value blue into slightly higher-value green. Peacock will try to buy red by offering extra blue (targeting the player who values blue most). Cat looks for ways to accumulate blue (highest blue valuation) by offering greens.

Momentum: players repeat successful conversions. Otter continues converting blue into red when a favorable exchange partner (the high-blue cat) is likely to accept; peacock executes low-cost swaps that are neutral for them but attractive to otter; cat continues accumulating blue by swapping greens for blues when otter is the likely acceptor.

Final bargaining: peacock attempts another red-for-blue bundle aimed at the high-blue acceptor (cat). Otter repeats a profitable small blue->red conversion when cat is likely to accept. Cat finishes by turning an inexpensive green into one more blue with otter as the accepting partner.

parsed_target.game_explanation: Three players repeatedly bargain over three chip colors with private per-chip values (red/blue differ by player; green common-value). Proposers will try to exchange chips they value relatively less for chips they value more; recipients decide simultaneously based only on their private values and current holdings. Because greens are middling (0.50) and players disagree on red vs. blue, typical patterns are: players who dislike red (squid) will sell red to acquire blue or green; players who dislike blue (camel) will sell blue for red or green; badger, who values red and blue equally and values green less, will try to trade green away to pick up red/blue. Uncertainty arises because proposers do not know others' private valuations, so they tend to propose trades that are likely to be attractive to at least one counterpart (e.g., offering green to buy blue from camel or offering green to buy red from squid). The realized game tends to feature many small (1-chip) exchanges where the recipient is the single player who strictly benefits from the swap.

rounds.round_explanation: Opening round: proposers attempt straightforward 1-for-1 swaps that convert lower-valued chips into higher-valued ones. Badger opens by offering green to buy a blue (attractive to camel), then camel targets squid by offering green to buy a red, and squid then offers red to buy a blue (attractive to camel). These simple mutually beneficial swaps are likely to be accepted by the single player who gains value.

After initial small reallocations, players continue the same profitable pattern. Badger again trades green for blue (camel is the marginal donor), camel trades green for red (squid benefits), and squid trades red for blue (camel benefits). Each proposer targets the partner who, by private valuation, is the likely beneficiary.

Endgame: players make one last set of low-friction swaps. Badger targets squid to buy a red by selling green (squid values red least), camel again sources red from squid by selling green, and squid uses low-value red to obtain an extra blue from camel. The pattern yields several 1-chip transfers that reflect static private-value incentives.

parsed_target.game_explanation: Three players start symmetric holdings (10 of each chip) but have asymmetric private values for RED and BLUE (GREEN common-value at $0.50). Each proposer will try to trade away chips they value relatively less to acquire chips they value more. Because responses are private and simultaneous, proposers face uncertainty about which partner (if any) will accept; offering very attractive terms can induce multiple acceptances and leave the actual partner to chance. Responders accept only when the trade raises their own total value. Over three rounds (nine proposals) we expect repeated patterns: dog repeatedly offers low-value BLUE to buy high-value RED; butterfly repeatedly offers chips (RED or GREEN) to acquire high-value BLUE; ram trades away BLUE (his lowest value) to acquire RED or GREEN. Trades that give a clear, positive one-shot gain to a responder are accepted; trades that would make a responder worse off are declined. Given these incentives, most proposals that exchange low-value for higher-value chips for both sides (e.g., dog: BLUE for RED with butterfly; butterfly: GREEN for BLUE with dog) are executed, sometimes with multiple acceptances when more than one responder benefits, in which case a random accepting partner is realized.

rounds.round_explanation: Opening round: players test straightforward, single-chip swaps that immediately increase both proposer and at least one responder's value. Dog targets RED (highest value for him) by offering BLUE; butterfly targets BLUE by offering RED; ram uses his low-value BLUE to buy RED. Responders evaluate only their own private values and accept if they gain.

Players have updated holdings. Dog continues to trade away BLUE for RED (low personal value BLUE for high-value RED). Butterfly leverages GREEN to buy more BLUE (GREEN and RED are closer in value for her, but BLUE is best). Ram continues to swap BLUE for GREEN when GREEN is more valuable for him. Offers that give a positive payoff to multiple responders produce multiple acceptances and a random matching; offers that benefit only one responder are accepted by that responder alone.

Final round: players consolidate desirable chips. Dog continues offering BLUE for RED; butterfly continues converting scarce RED or GREEN into BLUE; ram uses BLUE to secure additional GREEN or RED depending on where he can capture small positive gains. With private valuations unchanged, responders continue to accept only when the swap increases their own value.

parsed_target.game_explanation: Three players start with equal nominal holdings (10 of each color) but different private values for red and blue; green is common-value (0.50). Alligator and Lion both value blue highest (0.60 and 1.00) and red lowest, so they will repeatedly try to convert red into blue. Iguana values red highest (0.80) and blue lowest (0.40), so Iguana is a natural supplier of blue (or demander of red) and will often accept offers that exchange blue for red. Because players only know their own private values, proposers must make offers that are likely to be individually attractive to at least one opponent. The main dynamic is repeated, small one-for-one trades where Alligator and Lion offer red to acquire blue, and Iguana responds by selling blue (or using green to buy additional red). When Iguana proposes, it can often buy red by offering green; both Alligator and Lion benefit slightly from swapping red for green and may both accept, with a random accepting recipient realized. Uncertainty comes from not knowing which opponent will accept (if both do) and how many blue chips Iguana is willing to sell before its marginal value of blue changes; nevertheless, given these static private values, we forecast a sequence of mutually beneficial 1-for-1 exchanges executed each turn.

rounds.round_explanation: Early play: Alligator and Lion both try to trade away low-valued red for higher-valued blue. Iguana, who prefers red, is the likely trading partner and will accept exchanges that give it red in return for blue. When Iguana proposes, it prefers to buy red by offering green, which both Alligator and Lion find mildly attractive; both can accept, with a random recipient chosen.

Players continue the same pattern: Alligator and Lion keep attempting one-for-one red->blue swaps; Iguana remains willing to supply blue in exchange for red. Iguana uses green to buy additional red when it proposes, and both other players find red->green swaps slightly favorable and often accept. Because multiple acceptances can occur on Iguana's proposals, recipients are resolved randomly in forecasting.

Late-stage play repeats the established incentives. Alligator and Lion still prefer to convert red into blue and continue to have a willing partner in Iguana; Iguana keeps accumulating red and occasionally buys more red from whichever opponent accepts its green-for-red offers. Acceptance patterns remain stable: Alligator and Lion decline giving up blue to each other, while Iguana consistently accepts blue->red trades.

parsed_target.game_explanation: This three-round, three-player bargaining game is driven by asymmetric private valuations over red and blue chips (green chips have the same public value for everyone). Whale and Duck both place the highest value on red (1.00) and substantially less on blue (0.40 and 0.60 respectively), while Dolphin prefers blue (0.80) over red (0.60). Because each proposer must offer one color in exchange for another, profitable trades typically arise when the recipient gives up a chip they value less than the chip they receive. That creates a predictable pattern: players who value blue more (Dolphin) will be willing to trade red for blue, and players who value red more (Whale and Duck) will be willing to trade blue for red. Green (0.50) is close to middle value and so is rarely attractive as a trading currency. Uncertainty enters mainly through two channels: (1) accept/reject decisions are private and based on private values, so proposers face strategic uncertainty about who will accept; (2) when multiple non-senders accept, the actual recipient is chosen at random, introducing allocation risk. Over repeated turns, proposers exploit these complementarities by offering minimal quantities of their low-value chip for the counterparty's high-value chip; recipients accept only when the exchange increases their own total value. The forecast below draws on those incentives to predict a sequence of small (1-for-1) exchanges, with Dolphin repeatedly trading red for blue and Whale and Duck repeatedly trading blue for red, and with randomization resolving multi-accept situations where it occurs.

rounds.round_explanation: With symmetric starting endowments (10 of each color) and the known valuation asymmetries, the round is likely to open with Whale trying to buy red by selling blue (whale values red most, blue least). Dolphin, who prefers blue, will accept red-for-blue swaps; Duck (high red value) will not. Dolphin will then propose symmetrical exchanges (sell red for blue) that both other players find attractive; multiple acceptances can occur, resolved by random selection. Duck will also buy red by selling blue when doing so benefits Dolphin. Trades are small (single-chip exchanges) to minimize cost while extracting favorable chips.

After the opening exchanges, holdings diverge: Dolphin accumulates blues, Whale accumulates reds; Duck trades opportunistically. The same incentives persist, so Whale again offers blue for red (targeting Dolphin), Dolphin continues to trade red for blue (often attracting acceptances from both Whale and Duck), and Duck will buy red from Dolphin by offering blue when it is individually profitable for Dolphin. Multi-accept outcomes remain possible and create randomness in who becomes the trading partner.

In the final round, patterns are entrenched: Dolphin holds many blues and few reds and will continue to accept red-for-blue exchanges; Whale and Duck continue to try to buy red by selling blue. Because Dolphin's marginal preference for blue remains strong, they frequently accept offers to give up red for blue. Multi-accept situations still occur when a proposer offers red in exchange for blue (both Whale and Duck sometimes prefer the opposite swap), producing random assignment when both accept.

parsed_target.game_explanation: This three-round, three-player bargaining game features repeated small, one-for-one chip trades. Each player begins with identical counts (10 of each color) but private marginal values differ for red and blue (green is common-value at 0.50). Players propose exchanges that trade a color they offer for a different color they request; non-senders accept only if the trade raises their own private total value. Dynamics will therefore be driven by (1) proposers offering colors that are relatively cheap to them to acquire colors they value more, (2) targeted small-quantity offers (single-chip trades) that create positive net gains for at least one counterparty, and (3) occasional competition (multiple acceptances) which makes the realized partner random. Uncertainty: proposers do not know opponents' private valuations, only holdings and public history, so they bias proposals toward trades that are likely to appeal to at least one plausible accepter (e.g., offering green to buy blue appeals to players who value blue substantially more than green). Because each player maximizes their own value, many proposals are selfish (small net gains for proposer) and responders accept only when their private gain is nonnegative (strict gains preferred). Over three rounds we expect repeated patterns: turtle (who values blue most) will frequently try to buy blue, gorilla (who values red most) will repeatedly try to acquire red, and zebra (with moderate red preference) will attempt small profitable red buys; many proposals will be accepted by the one player who benefits most, and occasionally by multiple players leading to a random recipient.

rounds.round_explanation: Opening round with symmetric endowments. Turtle (BLUE=1.00) will try to buy blue by offering a cheaper color (green or red). Gorilla (RED=1.00) will try to buy red back using blue as an offer. Zebra's first attempt to buy red by offering green is unlikely to attract accepts because both other players value red more than green.

After the first round there is modest reshuffling: turtle has accumulated some blue and gorilla some red/green. Turtle continues to target blue, often offering red (which still has high value but is slightly cheaper to trade than blue for some partners), producing offers that can attract multiple accepters. Gorilla persists in targeting red, typically offering blue. Zebra continues to make small, positive-margin proposals for red when possible.

With holdings now polarized (turtle with many blues, gorilla accumulating reds, zebra modestly shifted), proposers keep making small one-for-one trades that yield small positive private gains. Turtle continues to extract blue by offering red (often attracting both gorilla and zebra), gorilla continues to buy red by offering blue (typically accepted by turtle), and zebra keeps attempting profitable red-for-blue trades that turtle will accept.

parsed_target.game_explanation: Three players start with equal chip counts but different private values for RED and BLUE (GREEN is common-value). Proposers will try to exchange chips they value relatively less for chips they value more, but must make offers attractive enough for at least one other player to accept. Because GREEN has identical value (0.50) to all, it is a convenient low-friction currency for trades that transfer RED (valued differently across players). zebra strongly prefers BLUE, iguana and cat prefer RED, and iguana dislikes BLUE the most. This creates a repeated pattern: cat and iguana repeatedly try to acquire RED by offering chips others value more weakly (often GREEN or BLUE), while zebra repeatedly tries to convert its low-valued RED into BLUE. Acceptance decisions depend only on private values: a recipient accepts if receiving the offered chips increases their own total value given the chips they give up. With these incentives, most proposals are small 1-for-1 exchanges that benefit both proposer and at least one recipient, and multi-accept situations (both non-senders prefer the proposed exchange) lead to a random matching among acceptors.

rounds.round_explanation: Early-round dynamics: proposers test mutually beneficial 1-for-1 swaps. cat will try to obtain RED by offering GREEN (cat values RED >> GREEN), iguana will try to obtain RED by offering BLUE (iguana values RED >> BLUE), and zebra will try to obtain BLUE by offering RED (zebra values BLUE >> RED). Because zebra's RED is the cheapest for zebra, others are willing to accept RED-for-BLUE or RED-for-GREEN trades; sometimes both non-senders prefer the swap, producing randomized partner selection.

Given the updated holdings, the same incentive pattern repeats. cat continues to offer GREEN for RED (profitable for both cat and zebra), iguana continues offering BLUE for RED (zebra prefers RED->BLUE), and zebra continues offering RED for BLUE (both cat and iguana can benefit by swapping). Multi-acceptances remain possible when both non-senders gain from the swap, so realized partners are again randomly selected among acceptors.

By the final round players continue the same bilateral patterns because relative private values are unchanged. Proposals are again small 1-for-1 exchanges that are typically mutually beneficial for proposer and at least one potential recipient. When both non-senders prefer the exchange, the partner is determined randomly, so the proposer secures a trade but not a chosen counterparty.

parsed_target.game_explanation: All three players start with identical holdings (10 of each color) but different private values for red and blue; green is universally worth 0.50. Each player prefers higher-valued colors and will only accept a proposed exchange if, using their private values, the chips they receive are worth at least as much as (and generally more than) the chips they give up. Proposers therefore try to convert low-value green (0.50) into red/blue (0.80 or 0.70) or engineer one-for-one red/blue swaps that attract a particular counterparty who values the incoming color more. Because acceptances are private and multiple acceptances are resolved randomly, proposers cannot pick partners and must craft offers that make at least one other player strictly better off. Early offers that ask for a valuable color in exchange for green are attractive to the proposer but unattractive to recipients; more successful proposals tend to be swaps where a recipient benefits (e.g., trading the recipient's less-valued blue for a more-valued red). Uncertainty stems from private valuations (players do not know others' precise values) and the randomness if more than one non-sender accepts. Over three rounds, I forecast a pattern of initial declined, then targeted one-for-one swaps that concentrate reds with the player who values them and blues with the player who values them, produced by proposers offering colors that make a specific recipient strictly better off.

rounds.round_explanation: Round 0: proposers try simple green→red/blue offers because green is the weakest for everyone. Those offers benefit the proposer but are unattractive to recipients, so anticipate declines across the three opening turns.

Round 1: proposers shift to one-for-one red/blue swaps that can make a particular non-sender strictly better off (even if the proposer is indifferent or only slightly better off). These targeted swaps are likely to produce accepted trades when a counterparty values the incoming color more than the outgoing one.

Round 2: with holdings already moved toward preferred colors, proposers continue targeted one-for-one red/blue swaps. These swaps tend to be accepted by the player who benefits (trading away a less-valued color for a more-valued one), further concentrating reds with the player who favors red and blues with those who favor blue.

parsed_target.game_explanation: Three self-interested players start with equal counts of red, blue, and green chips but different private values for red and blue (green is common value). Monkey and camel strongly prefer blue (0.90 and 0.80) while gorilla values green most (0.50) and red/blue less (0.30 each). Trades therefore tend to move blue from gorilla and camel toward monkey and camel, and move lower-value red (for monkey) or green (for camel) toward gorilla. Proposers will offer quantities that make a trade individually profitable for at least one responder given that responders only accept if their private value increases. Because proposals are simple one-color-for-one-color bundles, proposers often need to overpay (offer multiple low-value chips) to lure higher-value chips from others. Uncertainty arises because each responder only knows their own valuations and must infer whether an offered bundle is a genuine surplus; when both non-senders would benefit, multiple acceptances lead to a random recipient. The forecast below uses the full-set private values as analyst priors to simulate plausible offers, acceptance decisions, and holdings updates across the three rounds.

rounds.round_explanation: Early play: gorilla wants more green (highest private value) and can pay with red (equally low for gorilla). Camel and monkey have different incentives: camel is willing to trade green or red for extra red/blue in some ratios (neutral between red and green), while monkey strongly prefers blue and will generally only give up red. Expect gorilla to buy green by overpaying red to entice camel; camel will then seek blue by offering green, and monkey will offer low-value red in bulk to obtain blue, generating opportunities where both others benefit and a random recipient is chosen.

With some reallocation of chips after round 0, gorilla still prefers green and continues to buy green by overpaying in red; camel continues to convert green into blue when profitable for gorilla to give up blue; monkey repeats a profitable pattern of offering red in exchange for blue, a trade that benefits at least one responder (often both), so multiple acceptances are again possible.

Late-game incentives stay consistent: camel continues to acquire blue by offering red; gorilla continues to buy green by offering red; monkey persistently trades low-value red for high-value blue, and because both other players can occasionally benefit from accepting, some offers again generate multiple acceptances resolved by random selection.

parsed_target.game_explanation: Three players repeatedly trade single-color bundles over three rounds. Each player privately values red, blue, and the common green (0.50) differently, so sensible proposals will try to exchange a chip the proposer cares for less for one they care for more. Alligator (prefers RED > GREEN > BLUE) will tend to sell BLUE to buy RED (or occasionally GREEN). Otter (prefers BLUE = GREEN > RED) will tend to sell RED to buy BLUE or GREEN. Tiger (prefers RED > GREEN > BLUE) will also typically sell BLUE to buy RED.
