Because recipients decide based only on their private valuations, proposers make offers that create strictly positive immediate gains for at least one counterparty; when an offer benefits both non-senders both will accept and a recipient is randomly chosen. Uncertainty comes from: proposers not knowing others' private valuations (so they must choose safe, attractive terms), simultaneous private responses (which may yield multiple acceptances and a random recipient), and the evolving holdings (which constrain later offers). The forecast below uses these incentives and the specific private values to predict proposals, who accepts, and the realized recipients. All trades are small (1–2 chips) to ensure feasibility and clear immediate incentives.

rounds.round_explanation: Early round: proposers aim to convert low-valued chips into higher-valued ones. Alligator and Tiger will offer BLUE to get RED; Otter will offer RED to acquire BLUE. Proposers choose small, attractive deals that yield immediate positive gains for at least one counterparty given their private values.

Mid game: holdings have shifted so players repeat similar profitable swaps. Alligator continues buying RED with BLUE; Otter sells RED to acquire GREEN (or BLUE) when beneficial; Tiger still trades BLUE for RED when it creates a gain for a counterparty (typically Otter). When proposals benefit both non-senders a recipient is randomly selected.

Final round: players make small, high-probability trades to convert remaining low-value chips into preferred chips. Otter, now low on RED, continues selling RED for BLUE; Alligator keeps buying RED; Tiger continues selling BLUE for RED when Otter can supply RED profitably.

parsed_target.game_explanation: Three players have cyclic preferences over the three chip colors: dolphin strongly prefers BLUE, frog strongly prefers RED, and pig prefers GREEN (green is common-value at 0.50). All start with 10 of each color. Proposers will tend to request their favorite color and offer a less-preferred color. Responders accept a proposal when the chips they would receive are worth at least as much as the chips they would give up according to their private values. Because players only know their own values, acceptance is driven by local, myopic gains: dolphin repeatedly offers low-valued RED to obtain high-valued BLUE; frog does the mirror image, offering BLUE to obtain RED; pig trades lower-valued red/blue for extra GREEN when it is profitable to the responder. Uncertainty arises from (1) responders not knowing other players' valuations (so they judge each offer only on their own payoff), (2) potential conflicts when more than one responder would benefit (in which case a random accepting partner is selected), and (3) the gradual change of holdings across rounds altering who is willing to trade. Given the valuations, we forecast a pattern of repeated 1-for-1 exchanges: dolphin buys BLUE for RED (accepted mainly by frog), frog buys RED for BLUE (accepted mainly by dolphin), and pig buys GREEN for RED (accepted by frog because frog values RED highly). This pattern increases concentration of BLUE for dolphin, RED for frog, and GREEN for pig over the three rounds.

rounds.round_explanation: Start: everyone has 10 of each color. Dolphin (BLUE=1.00, RED=0.20) will try to trade RED for BLUE. Frog (RED=1.00, BLUE=0.30) will trade BLUE for RED. Pig (GREEN=0.50, RED/BLUE=0.40) will try to buy GREEN by offering a lower-valued color to a player who finds that favorable (frog values RED highly). Early trades are small 1-for-1 proposals; responders accept if the incoming chip is worth more to them than the outgoing chip.

Holdings have shifted: dolphin has more BLUE, frog has accumulated more RED, pig has started accumulating GREEN. The same local incentives persist: dolphin keeps offering RED for BLUE (profitable for dolphin and attractive to frog), frog continues offering BLUE for RED (profitable for frog and attractive to dolphin), and pig continues offering RED for GREEN when frog finds the exchange profitable. Trades remain 1-for-1 and are accepted by the player who gains locally.

By the final round, patterns are entrenched: dolphin continues to concentrate BLUE, frog concentrates RED, and pig accumulates additional GREEN when frog finds it beneficial to swap. Because frog values RED highly, frog frequently accepts exchanges that transfer RED to them in return for BLUE or accepts selling GREEN for RED when offered. Dolphin and frog continue their reciprocal 1-for-1 exchanges; pig's offers for GREEN are accepted primarily by frog.

parsed_target.game_explanation: This three-round bargaining game pits three players with different private values for red, blue, and a common-valued green chip. Iguana highly values blue, owl highly values red, and squid values blue and green equally while disliking red. Expect proposers to try to swap chips they value little for chips they value more (e.g., iguana and squid offering red to acquire blue; owl offering blue to acquire red or green). Responders accept only when the proposed exchange increases their own private value; because acceptance is simultaneous and a proposer cannot pick the partner, proposers tend to structure offers that make at least one other player better off. Strategic uncertainty arises because each player sees only their own valuations; they infer others’ likely responses from public trade history and current holdings. Trades that advantage owl (who values red strongly) often get accepted by owl in exchange for their relatively low-valued blue; trades offering red can attract owl quickly. Trades that ask blue from iguana are less likely to be accepted by iguana (since iguana values blue highly), but iguana will accept offers that give them blue in return for low-value red. Random selection when multiple acceptors exist introduces risk for proposers and can affect how generous offers must be to attract one partner.

rounds.round_explanation: Opening round: proposers try straightforward value-improving swaps. Iguana (blue-lover) offers cheap red to acquire blue; owl can profit from trading blue away for red; squid prefers blue/green and will accept only exchanges that increase its value. Early trades reveal who is willing to give up blue.

Middle round: holdings have shifted, making owl richer in red and iguana richer in blue. Iguana continues to trade red for blue. Owl uses its low-valued blue to buy green (marginally valuable) or otherwise swap to consolidate red. Squid targets blue by offering green or red where it expects owl to accept.

Final round: proposers make last attempts to optimize holdings. Iguana continues converting red into blue. Owl uses its remaining blue to acquire additional red (very valuable to it); when multiple partners would gain from giving red, random selection determines the recipient. Squid makes a final small red-for-blue swap with owl, further consolidating blue.

parsed_target.game_explanation: Three players with distinct private values create repeated opportunities for mutually beneficial bilateral trades. Gorilla (low BLUE=0.10, mid RED=0.40, GREEN=0.50) benefits from trading away RED for GREEN; both Llama and Whale (high BLUE=0.90, RED=0.60, GREEN=0.50) benefit from acquiring RED or BLUE relative to GREEN. Proposers aim to increase their own total value by swapping lower-valued chips for higher-valued ones given their private values; responders accept when the offered exchange raises their own value. Uncertainty arises because each player knows only their own values and must infer others' likely acceptances from the public history. As a result, Gorilla repeatedly offers RED for GREEN (profitable for Gorilla and for others who prefer RED) and Llama/Whale repeatedly offer RED for BLUE (profitable for them and attractive to Gorilla who values RED more than BLUE). When both non-senders would gain, multiple acceptances occur and a recipient is chosen at random; otherwise only the individually benefiting responder accepts. Holdings update immediately, which changes future feasibility and attractiveness (especially Gorillas' diminishing BLUE stock and Llama/Whale declining GREEN stock).

rounds.round_explanation: Opening round: Gorilla exploits its preference for GREEN over RED by offering RED to buy GREEN — both Llama and Whale prefer RED to GREEN and thus accept. Llama targets BLUE (high value for her) by offering RED; Gorilla, who values RED more than BLUE, accepts while Whale declines. Whale then tries to convert GREEN into BLUE; Gorilla (who dislikes BLUE) is willing to give BLUE for GREEN, so Gorilla accepts.

With public history showing profitable RED↔GREEN and RED↔BLUE swaps, Gorilla repeats selling RED for GREEN in larger quantity; both Llama and Whale still prefer RED to GREEN and accept. Llama then again buys BLUE by offering RED; Gorilla accepts. Whale also purchases BLUE by selling RED: Gorilla (who has little BLUE but still values giving it up for RED) accepts that trade as well.

Endgame: Gorilla continues to convert RED into GREEN in larger chunks while Llama and Whale, having accumulated BLUE, have less GREEN to trade. Llama presses for additional BLUE by selling RED and still finds Gorilla as a willing supplier. Whale finishes by buying a small amount of BLUE (feasible given Gorilla's remaining BLUE), keeping the pattern of Gorilla accepting BLUE in exchange for RED.

parsed_target.game_explanation: Three players with heterogeneous private values for red and blue and a common value for green bargain over three rounds. Players propose bilateral-style chip swaps (request one color, offer another); non-senders decide simultaneously whether the swap improves their own private value and whether they can supply the requested chips. The main dynamic: players who value blue very highly (tiger) are natural buyers of blue and sellers of lower-valued chips (green or red), while players with low blue values (duck, lion) prefer to sell blue in exchange for red or green. Because acceptance is determined by each responder's private valuation and inventory, proposals that offer chips that are much more valuable to at least one responder (e.g., blue to tiger) tend to be accepted quickly, often by a single best-fit partner. Uncertainty for each proposer comes from not knowing others' private valuations and from multiple-acceptance tie risk (recipient is then random). Over the three rounds observers expect aggressive blue-seeking by tiger and opportunistic blue-selling by duck and lion, with most trades executed when the proposer sells low-valued chips to someone who values them more.

rounds.round_explanation: Early bargaining: duck and lion attempt small upgrades by selling low-value blue to acquire green (higher for them), and tiger—who values blue strongly—accepts such offers. Proposers aim for clear value improvements; responders accept when the incoming chips are worth more than those they give up.

After initial trades inventories shift: tiger accumulates blue and becomes an even more attractive trade partner for blue sellers. Duck and lion continue selling blue to acquire red/green when profitable. Proposals that give a clear value gain to at least one responder are accepted; some proposals draw multiple acceptances and the recipient is chosen at random among acceptors.

Endgame moves: inventories are uneven (tiger concentrated in blue). Duck has few/zero blues and so cannot accept blue-asking proposals, limiting some matches; proposals that ask for chips a responder actually holds and that create a positive private value delta for that responder are accepted. Tiger continues to convert lower-value chips to blue when possible.

parsed_target.game_explanation: Three players begin with 10 of each chip. Reds and blues are privately heterogeneous in value; greens are common-value (0.50). Proposers can trade one color for another; recipients privately decide to accept if the trade raises their own value. Because bear and goose both value red at 1.00 while ram values blue at 1.00, a recurring profitable pattern emerges: players with relatively high blue value (ram) are willing to trade reds for blues, and players who value red highly (bear and goose) propose to acquire red by offering blue. Goose repeatedly trades low-value blue for green (gain +0.30 for goose) and ram accepts because he values blue highly (net +0.50 for ram). When both non-senders would gain from a proposal, both accept and the realized partner is chosen at random, introducing uncertainty about who actually trades with the proposer. Over three rounds, this yields a sequence of small one-for-one exchanges: bear repeatedly buys red for blue from ram; goose repeatedly converts blue into green with ram; ram repeatedly tries to buy blue by selling red and sometimes matches with bear or goose depending on the random resolution when both accept. Throughout, each accepted trade is individually value-improving for proposer and chosen recipient given their private valuations; players decline trades that reduce their own value. The main strategic considerations are (1) propose exchanges that are clearly positive for you, (2) anticipate which counterparty values the offered/requested color more, and (3) account for randomness when both accept.

rounds.round_explanation: Initial holdings are equal. Bear and goose both highly value red; ram highly values blue. Expect bear to offer blue for red (ram willing to sell red for blue), goose to swap blue for green (ram gains blue), and ram to try buying blue by selling red (both others benefit and may both accept). Early trades establish the pattern and update holdings that make repeated similar trades attractive.

Holdings shifted: bear and goose accumulated red or green, ram accumulated blue. The same profitable one-for-one motifs repeat. Bear continues buying red with blue (ram still prefers blue), goose repeats blue->green trades (ram benefits by gaining blue), and ram again offers red for blue; when both others benefit, random selection determines the counterparty.

By the final round, players' adjusted holdings reinforce incentives to repeat the earlier exchanges. Bear again buys red for blue from ram; goose again converts blue into green with ram; ram makes one last red-for-blue offer and both others prefer the deal, so the recipient is determined by chance.

parsed_target.game_explanation: This three-round, three-player bargaining game pits private valuations for RED and BLUE chips against a common-known value for GREEN. Each player starts with ample holdings (10 of each color) and proposes one-for-one or asymmetric chip exchanges by offering chips they hold in return for chips held by others. Incentives: players propose trades that increase their own total value (trade away lower-valued chips to obtain higher-valued ones). Responders decide privately whether the offered exchange raises their own value (they lose the requested chip(s) and gain the offered chip(s)). Uncertainty arises because responders do not observe other players' private values and cannot coordinate; if multiple responders accept, one is chosen uniformly at random to realize the trade, so proposers sometimes must make offers attractive enough to induce acceptance while still benefiting themselves. Given the provided private values, octopus (high value for BLUE and RED) will repeatedly attempt to convert low-value GREEN into RED; ram (low value for RED) will accept such offers and also try to swap low-value RED for higher-value GREEN or BLUE when profitable. Lion prioritizes BLUE over RED/GREEN but faces opponents who value BLUE strongly, so simple one-for-one requests for BLUE will often be declined unless the proposer makes the offer sufficiently lucrative to the potential giver. The forecast below treats each player's proposal choice as aiming for a positive net gain for the proposer and each responder as accepting only if their own private-value change is positive (ties treated as declines).

rounds.round_explanation: Early bargaining. Lion tries to acquire BLUE (their highest-valued chip) by offering a single lower-valued chip; octopus exploits ram's low RED value to trade GREEN for RED; ram attempts to acquire BLUE by offering RED but is rebuffed because others value BLUE more.

With holdings adjusted (octopus converted green->red; ram gained green), players repeat profitable swaps. Lion again unsuccessfully requests BLUE for GREEN. Octopus again trades GREEN for RED, which ram accepts (ram values GREEN more than RED). Ram then opportunistically buys GREEN from octopus with RED; octopus accepts because RED is more valuable to them.

Final round: lion again fails to secure BLUE at a favorable exchange rate. Octopus continues to convert GREEN into RED (profitable for them) and ram accepts. Ram closes by buying more GREEN with RED when it is profitable; octopus accepts that swap as well, continuing the pattern of octopus exchanging low-valued GREEN for high-valued RED and ram using low-valued RED to accumulate GREEN.

parsed_target.game_explanation: Three players repeatedly trade single-color chips across three rounds. Buffalo values blue very little and prefers to sell blue to acquire red (positive gain for Buffalo). Cat values red and blue equally and values both substantially more than green; Cat is therefore a flexible counterparty who is often indifferent between receiving red or blue and frequently accepts trades that are value-neutral for them. Duck values red highest and blue second, so Duck prefers to convert blue into red when possible. Greens have identical common value (0.50) for everyone and are often used by Cat as a cheap currency to buy reds. Strategic uncertainty arises because proposals are accepted privately by others who only know their own values; when a proposal is attractive to more than one responder, the actual recipient is chosen at random. That randomization, plus Cat's frequent indifference between red and blue, makes it possible that both Buffalo and Duck sometimes accept the same proposal. Given these incentives, expect Buffalo to repeatedly offer blue for red, Cat to repeatedly offer green for red, and Duck to repeatedly offer blue for red; most proposals will be accepted by Cat (neutral) or by the player who gains positive value (Buffalo or Duck), with ties resolved by random selection.

rounds.round_explanation: Opening round: Buffalo uses its low valuation of blue to try to buy red by offering blue; Cat is likely to accept neutral swaps of red for blue. Cat will try to convert cheap green into high-value red; both Buffalo and Duck gain by accepting that, so multiple acceptance is possible and the recipient will be randomly chosen. Duck prefers converting blue into red and will offer blue for red, which Cat (neutral) often accepts.

By round 1 players have slightly adjusted holdings but incentives remain the same. Buffalo continues selling blue to accumulate red. Cat continues to spend green to acquire red (high personal value) and typically finds it acceptable to receive green-to-red trades; when both Buffalo and Duck would accept Cat's green-for-red proposal, random selection determines who trades. Duck again offers blue for red to increase red holdings; Cat's neutrality on red vs blue makes Cat the frequent acceptor of Duck's offers.

Final round: players intensify straightforward, individually profitable swaps. Buffalo persistently sells blue to buy red. Cat continues buying red with green when profitable and accepts many blue-for-red offers as a neutral partner. Duck continues to convert blue into red when doing so yields positive marginal gain. Random selection again decides outcomes when multiple responders would accept.

parsed_target.game_explanation: Three players start with equal endowments (10 of each color) but very different private values for red and blue; green is common-value at $0.50. Each proposer will try to trade away chips they value relatively less for chips they value more. Because players only know their own values, offers are simple: request a high-value color and offer a low-value color. Given the specific private values (butterfly and squid value red very highly; kangaroo values red low and green moderately), the analyst expects repeated 1-for-1 exchanges where butterfly and squid repeatedly try to acquire red by offering green, and kangaroo repeatedly tries to acquire green by offering low-value blue. Acceptance decisions follow obvious private-payoff comparisons (accept if the incoming chip is worth more to the responder than the chip they must give up). When two responders would both gain, both will accept and the recipient will be selected at random; when only one responder gains, that single player accepts. Uncertainty for proposers comes from not knowing which responder values the offered/requested color how much; the simple public ranges and past trade behavior reduce that uncertainty over time, producing repeated mutually beneficial 1-for-1 trades concentrated between kangaroo (who is the cheap-red supplier / green demander) and the others (who value red more).

rounds.round_explanation: Opening round: butterfly moves first and aims to trade green (low for them) for red (high for them). Kangaroo values red relatively low and is likely to accept such offers; squid values red highly and will decline. Kangaroo's turn aims to swap low-value blue for green; both other players value blue more than green, so both will accept, producing a randomized recipient. Squid then tries to acquire red by offering green, again appealing to kangaroo.

After the first round, holdings reveal kangaroo as a recurring net supplier of red and buyer of green. Butterfly repeats the green-for-red approach and targets kangaroo. Kangaroo again offers blue for green; both others still benefit from accepting, so both accept and the recipient is randomized. Squid again requests red for green and kangaroo, still better off giving red for green, accepts.

Patterns persist into the final round: butterfly again offers green for red and kangaroo accepts; kangaroo offers blue for green and both others accept, yielding another randomized recipient; squid makes a final green-for-red proposal that kangaroo accepts. Over the game kangaroo accumulates green and gives up red, consistent with their valuations.

parsed_target.game_explanation: This three-player chip bargaining game centers on asymmetric private values for RED and BLUE chips and a common value for GREEN (0.50 for all). BAT values RED and BLUE relatively highly (0.80 each), FOX strongly prefers BLUE (0.90) and dislikes RED (0.40), and FROG values BLUE the most (1.00) with intermediate/low valuations for RED (0.50) and GREEN (0.50). Given these priors the dominant pattern is repeated small exchanges where proposers try to shed chips they value less (often GREEN or RED for FOX) to acquire chips they value more (RED or BLUE for BAT and FROG). Because acceptors evaluate trades against their own private values, a trade that is profitable for the proposer is accepted only if it also raises (or at least does not reduce) the recipient's private value. Uncertainty for actual players arises because each only knows their own values; as an observer using the provided priors we forecast that BAT will frequently offer GREEN for RED (exploiting FOX's low RED value), FOX will reciprocate by offering RED for GREEN, and FROG (who highly values BLUE) will sometimes overpay with multiple GREEN or RED to entice a BLUE seller. When multiple responders find an offer profitable, one accepting partner is randomly chosen; otherwise a single acceptor executes the trade. The forecast below simulates three rounds (each with three proposers in the prescribed order), showing likely proposals, which players accept, and how holdings update.

rounds.round_explanation: Early in the game players still hold symmetric endowments (10 each). BAT sees GREEN as the weakest chip and will try to convert GREEN into RED (or BLUE), targeting FOX because FOX has the lowest private value on RED and thus is most likely to accept GREEN-for-RED swaps. FOX reciprocates by buying GREEN with RED when it benefits him. FROG, who values BLUE most, is likely to attempt to acquire BLUE by offering multiple GREENs (overpaying slightly) to attract either BAT or FOX as acceptors.

After the first round holdings become asymmetric (BAT accumulates RED, FOX accumulates GREEN, FROG has acquired some BLUE). BAT continues to exploit FOX's low RED value by trading GREEN for RED. FOX repeats the GREEN-for-RED proposal when it yields more GREEN to him. FROG again tries to buy BLUE by offering multiple GREENs to attract a seller; with both BAT and FOX likely to accept such an overpay, a random accepting partner is selected.

Endgame incentives push BAT and FOX to continue the same profitable micro-trades (GREEN → RED for BAT, RED → GREEN for FOX) until holdings reflect those preferences strongly. FROG makes a last attempt to accumulate BLUE; to secure a partner FROG offers two REDs for one BLUE (a larger package) so that at least BAT—who is relatively indifferent between RED and BLUE—finds it profitable to accept. The resulting trades further concentrate RED with BAT and GREEN with FOX while FROG increases BLUE holdings.

parsed_target.game_explanation: This three-round, three-player bargaining game centers on heterogeneous private values for RED and BLUE chips and a common value for GREEN. Dog values BLUE much less than RED/GREEN, while Parrot and Turtle value BLUE highest. That creates a clear pattern: Dog is the natural seller of BLUE (cheap to him) and will propose trading BLUE for RED or GREEN; Parrot and Turtle are natural buyers of BLUE and will accept trades that exchange their lower-value chips (typically RED or GREEN) for BLUE. Because players only know their own values, proposers cannot perfectly target the partner but will offer exchanges that are plausibly attractive to at least one counterparty (single-unit, mutually profitable swaps). Recipients decide based on their private valuations: they accept if receiving a chip they value more than the one they give up. When both non-senders accept, the realized recipient is chosen at random; otherwise a single acceptor executes the trade. Over the three rounds we therefore forecast repeated 1-for-1 swaps of BLUE (sold by Dog) for RED (bought by Dog) and of BLUE (bought by Parrot) for GREEN (sold by Parrot) or RED (bought by Turtle) for BLUE, producing incremental transfers of chips toward each player's preferred composition while respecting feasibility constraints and private-information acceptance criteria.

rounds.round_explanation: Opening offers are simple, small, mutually beneficial swaps. Dog (who values BLUE lowest) offers BLUE for RED to improve his composition; both Parrot and Turtle would gain by accepting but only one becomes recipient by randomization. Parrot, valuing BLUE highly, then offers to buy BLUE by selling GREEN (a trade that Dog will accept since he prefers GREEN to BLUE). Turtle finishes the round by buying BLUE with RED from Dog, again accepted by Dog.

Players continue the same profitable pattern. Dog again posts a BLUE-for-RED swap; both other players would benefit as recipients, producing another accepted trade (we model Turtle as the realized recipient this time). Parrot repeats the GREEN-for-BLUE offer and Dog accepts. Turtle again proposes RED-for-BLUE and Dog accepts.

By the final round the same incentives persist and players continue to execute small mutually beneficial swaps. Dog again sells BLUE for RED (we model Parrot as the recipient this round). Parrot again buys BLUE by selling GREEN and Dog accepts. Turtle finishes with another RED-for-BLUE exchange accepted by Dog.

parsed_target.game_explanation: All three players seek to maximize their own total chip value given their private valuations. Badger and Unicorn strongly prefer BLUE (0.90 and 1.00 respectively), Peacock strongly prefers RED (1.00) and dislikes BLUE (0.10). GREEN is common-value (0.50) for everyone and so is useful as a neutral currency to shape who will accept. Because responses are private and a proposer cannot choose among multiple acceptors, proposers tend to craft offers that attract exactly one likely partner (by offering a chip the undesired player values little or offering a chip only the desired partner values favorably). Uncertainty comes from private valuations: proposers must infer acceptance sets from observable holdings and the known public ranges, but each responder knows only their own values. Trades that give a low-value chip (for that responder) and receive a high-value chip to that responder are very likely to be accepted; trades that would make a responder worse off are declined. Given these priors, we forecast a sequence of small 1-for-1 exchanges where proposers ask for BLUE and offer RED (to attract Peacock, who gives up low-value BLUE for high-value RED) or ask for RED and offer GREEN (to attract Unicorn, who gains or loses slightly on that swap), producing nearly deterministic single-acceptor outcomes each turn.

rounds.round_explanation: Early signaling and low-friction trades: proposers target the counterparty who most benefits from the exchange. Badger and Unicorn both value BLUE high and will try to secure BLUE by offering RED; Peacock dislikes BLUE and will readily give it up for RED. Peacock values RED highly and will offer GREEN to buy RED when that isolates Unicorn as the sole acceptor. Expect 1-for-1 swaps that trade low-valued chips for high-valued chips from the recipient's perspective, with proposers designing offers to elicit a single acceptor.

With a small public history, players reinforce profitable exchange patterns. Badger continues to request BLUE offering GREEN to attract Peacock (who prefers GREEN to giving up BLUE). Peacock keeps leveraging GREEN as bait to acquire extra RED from Unicorn. Unicorn persistently requests BLUE and offers RED, again drawing Peacock because Peacock's marginal value for BLUE remains low. Proposers aim for offers that produce a single accepting counterparty to avoid randomization.

Endgame incentives push players to extract remaining gains. Badger again requests BLUE offering RED to peel off Peacock's low-value BLUE. Peacock continues the pattern of converting GREEN into RED by dealing with Unicorn, and Unicorn continues to acquire BLUE by offering RED, typically transacting with Peacock. Because these offers target the player who gets a clear positive surplus, acceptance remains concentrated and deterministic in our forecast.

parsed_target.game_explanation: Three players start with identical chip endowments but different private values for red and blue (green is common-value). Expected dynamics: dog (values: BLUE 0.30, RED 0.50, GREEN 0.50) will try to trade away low-value blue to acquire green or red. Mouse (BLUE 0.40, RED 0.40, GREEN 0.50) prioritizes green and will similarly trade blue for green when profitable. Owl (BLUE 0.70, RED 0.50, GREEN 0.50) strongly prefers blue and will sell green or red to accumulate blue. Proposers offer one color and request another; responders accept only when their private valuation implies a strictly positive gain. Because owl values blue much higher, owl tends to accept trades that exchange green for blue; dog tends to accept exchanges that convert blue into red; mouse is intermediate but consistently trades blue for green when it yields a positive payoff. Uncertainty arises because responders only know their own values, not others', and when multiple players could accept a proposal the actual recipient is determined randomly; proposers therefore favor trades likely to produce at least one accepting partner. Forecasted equilibrium behavior is repeated small one-for-one exchanges: dog and mouse repeatedly offer blue to buy green (accepted by owl), and owl repeatedly offers red to buy blue (accepted by dog).

rounds.round_explanation: Early-move dynamics: dog opens by exchanging low-value blue for green; owl prefers blue and will accept green->blue trades. Mouse follows by trying to acquire green with blue, and owl again is the natural acceptor. Owl then offers red for blue to accumulate blue; dog, who dislikes blue, accepts.

Mid-game: patterns repeat as players exploit favorable one-for-one gains. Dog again trades blue for green (profitable to dog, attractive to owl). Mouse repeats the blue->green trade. Owl again purchases blue with red and dog, who prefers red to blue, accepts.

End-game: players continue small-value-improving swaps. Dog and mouse keep converting blue into green when owl is willing to sell green for blue; owl keeps converting red into additional blue via trades with dog. No player has strong incentive to deviate from these straightforward, positive-payoff one-for-one trades.

parsed_target.game_explanation: Three players each start with equal counts of red, blue, and green chips but differ slightly in how they value red and blue (green is a common-value chip at $0.50). Alligator and Buffalo place the highest value on red ($1.00), then blue ($0.70); Rabbit values red a bit less ($0.90) and blue slightly less ($0.60). Proposers request one color and offer a different color. Because each player only accepts trades that raise (or at least do not lower, if indifferent) their own expected chip-value based on private valuations, most offers that transfer higher-valued chips (red) will be accepted only if the proposer compensates with enough quantity of the offered color. Proposers who would lose private value are unlikely to make those proposals, so accepted trades tend to involve (1) proposers offering multiple lower-value chips to buy a higher-value chip from someone who values it relatively less (e.g., Rabbit selling red to someone offering >0.90 in perceived value), or (2) trades that leave recipients weakly better off (indifferent recipients sometimes accept). Uncertainty arises from private valuations (each player knows only their own chip values) and from multi-accept situations: if multiple recipients accept, one is chosen at random, so proposers cannot pick the partner. The forecasted game below uses these incentives and the private valuations to predict which proposals are made and which are accepted, updating holdings after each accepted trade.

rounds.round_explanation: Opening moves: proposers test the market for red by offering blue-for-red swaps. Because red is the most valuable chip for Alligator and Buffalo, small blue offers to buy red are declined; Buffalo counters with a more generous blue bundle that makes recipients better off, producing the first executed trade. Rabbit experiments with a small green-for-blue proposal but it is declined.

After the initial exchange Alligator has more blue and Buffalo has more red. Alligator uses some green to try and top up red holdings (offering a small green bundle to buy red); Rabbit, who values red slightly less than the others, accepts. Buffalo makes a conservative attempt to buy red with green but offers too little perceived compensation and is declined. Rabbit's follow-up offer to convert green into blue is also declined because recipients would lose value.

Late-stage proposals try to reallocate reds toward players who value them more. Buffalo posts an offer to buy red by offering two greens; Rabbit (who values red slightly less) gains from that exchange and accepts. Some smaller one-for-one proposals fail because recipients would lose private value.

parsed_target.game_explanation: This three-round, nine-turn bargaining game centers on asymmetries in private valuations for red and blue chips while green chips are common-value. Goose highly values red (0.90) and will try to buy reds by offering lower-valued chips (blue or green). Buffalo also values red relatively highly (0.80) and values blue very little (0.20), so as a responder buffalo is the natural supplier of red buyers (they will frequently accept offers that give them red in exchange for blue). Bear values red, blue, and green equally (0.50 each) and is effectively indifferent among colors; as a result bear often acts as a liquidity provider who will accept trades that leave their own value unchanged. Proposers can only offer chips they hold, and acceptances are private and simultaneous. Uncertainty for each player concerns the private values of others and whether indifferent players (especially bear) will accept. Observationally we expect a pattern: goose repeatedly attempts to buy red by offering medium-value green or blue; bear often accepts such trades (indifferent), transferring red to goose; bear also routinely offers red to acquire blue, which buffalo accepts eagerly (swapping buffalo's blue for red). Buffalo accumulates red by repeatedly accepting blue-for-red offers, while bear and goose are the main sellers of red when indifferent or compensated with equal-value chips.

rounds.round_explanation: Opening moves. Goose, who most values red, makes a conservative request for a single red in exchange for a blue. Bear (indifferent) and buffalo (who values blue very low) must decide whether to give up red; buffalo will not, bear is indifferent and may decline. Bear then exploits buffalo's low blue valuation by offering red to get blue; buffalo accepts, gaining red. Buffalo's turn attempts to convert blue into additional red but goose and bear decline.

With buffalo having shown willingness to trade blue for red, goose again targets red, this time offering a green (equal-value to many) to buy a red; bear, being indifferent, sells one red to goose. Bear then repeats the profitable-for-buffalo move of offering red for blue; buffalo accepts again. Buffalo uses its turn to buy another red by offering blue, and bear—indifferent and holding some reds—accepts, allowing buffalo to further accumulate red.

Final round: goose again targets red by offering green; bear, still indifferent and having fewer reds, sells another red to goose. Bear then offers red to buy blue and buffalo, who consistently prefers red to blue, accepts. On the last turn buffalo converts another blue into red by offering blue for red; bear again accepts, leaving buffalo with the largest red holding and bear acting repeatedly as a liquidity provider.

parsed_target.game_explanation: All three players start with identical holdings (10 of each color) but different private values for RED and BLUE; GREEN is common-value (0.50). Players maximize their own total chip value, so feasible trades must make the proposer strictly better off and persuade at least one counterparty that they are strictly better off (or at least weakly so, in cases of indifference). Because RED is relatively valuable to Peacock (0.80) and Otter (0.70) but only moderately valuable to Cat (0.50), acquiring RED requires offering enough value to overcome other players' attachment to RED. BLUE is the lowest-value chip for Cat (0.30) and Otter (0.20), so multi-unit BLUE→GREEN exchanges (e.g., selling two BLUE for one GREEN) can be Pareto-improving: the BLUE seller gains by turning very-low-value BLUE into 0.50-valued GREEN, while the GREEN-giver receives multiple BLUE units worth more to them. This creates a pattern: early proposals attempting straightforward 1-for-1 RED trades often fail, while trades where low-value BLUE is exchanged in quantity for GREEN are likely to be accepted by at least one other player. Uncertainty arises from: (1) proposers not knowing others' private valuations, (2) recipients breaking indifference differently (some accept weakly beneficial / indifferent offers, some decline), and (3) the random selection rule when multiple recipients accept. My forecast assumes rational, value-seeking behavior, occasional acceptance of indifferent offers by Peacock in bilateral situations, and that when both non-senders strictly benefit from an offer they both accept, producing a random recipient realization.

rounds.round_explanation: Peacock opens and will try for high-value RED but faces resistance; Cat tries to convert low-value BLUE into GREEN but cannot yet persuade others; Otter, who disfavours BLUE most strongly, can profitably buy GREEN by offering two BLUE, producing a clear Pareto-improving trade that both other players are willing to accept.

After Otter's successful 2 BLUE → 1 GREEN trade, Peacock has more BLUE and fewer GREEN; Cat sees that GREEN is obtainable by trading BLUE and proposes modest 1-for-1 exchanges that benefit Cat and are weakly acceptable to Peacock. Otter repeats the profitable two-BLUE bid for GREEN; both non-senders benefit and accept, producing another executed trade.

Peacock again tries to acquire RED but faces resistance; Cat continues to convert surplus BLUE into GREEN via 1-for-1 trades with Peacock; Otter finishes by repeating the 2 BLUE → 1 GREEN offer, which again benefits Otter and at least one other player and is accepted.

parsed_target.game_explanation: Three self-interested players repeatedly propose bilateral one-color-for-one-color trades over three rounds. Each proposer prefers to give away chip types they value least and request chips they value most; recipients accept only when the value they receive is at least as large as the value they give up. Uncertainty comes from two sources: (1) proposers do not know other players' private valuations (only public common ranges), so they must target trades that are likely to be acceptable to at least one counterparty given plausible valuations; (2) when multiple non-senders accept, a random selection decides the partner, so proposers cannot pick which accepter will be realized. Given the privately known values (squid: BLUE=1.00, GREEN=0.50, RED=0.10; badger: BLUE=1.00, GREEN=0.50, RED=1.00; camel: BLUE=0.80, GREEN=0.50, RED=0.40), predictable patterns emerge: squid repeatedly trades away low-value RED to acquire high-value BLUE; camel similarly tries to convert RED into BLUE but faces better resistance from squid; badger, who values RED and BLUE equally and GREEN less, acts as the market maker accepting many red-for-blue or green-for-red trades when they are neutral or beneficial for him. The forecast below uses these incentives and simple acceptance thresholds (accept if value received >= value given) to reconstruct a likely history of offers, responses, and holdings updates.

rounds.round_explanation: Early bargaining: all players start with 10 of each color. Squid, who greatly prefers BLUE over RED, opens by offering RED for BLUE. Badger (who values RED and BLUE equally) is willing to accept neutral red-for-blue swaps; camel (who values BLUE > RED) declines. Badger then sells cheap GREEN to request RED (beneficial to squid and camel), and camel finishes the round by offering RED for BLUE, which badger accepts as a neutral swap.

With updated holdings, squid continues converting low-value RED into BLUE in larger blocks. Badger, who places equal value on RED and BLUE and lower value on GREEN, uses GREEN as a bargaining chip to extract extra RED from others (both squid and camel find selling RED for GREEN weakly beneficial). Camel continues attempts to swap RED for BLUE; badger again is the most likely counterparty to accept neutral red-for-blue swaps.

Endgame incentives: squid continues smaller red-for-blue swaps while badger, running low on BLUE, tries (unsuccessfully) to buy BLUE using cheap GREEN. Camel makes one last red-for-blue attempt but cannot find an accepting partner. Declined proposals arise when the proposed exchange would make all potential recipients worse off from their private valuation perspective or when potential recipients lack the requested chips.

parsed_target.game_explanation: This three-round, three-player bargaining game pits three different private valuations against a common holding structure. Ram values RED highest, then BLUE, then GREEN; Dog values GREEN highest and both colored chips much less; Butterfly values RED highest, with BLUE and GREEN equal and intermediate. Proposers request one color and offer another; recipients decide based only on whether, if they were the recipient, the incoming chips would increase their own private value net of what they give up. Because RED is generally the most valuable for Ram and Butterfly, proposals that transfer RED to proposers by offering BLUE often find acceptance from recipients who prefer RED to BLUE (they receive BLUE and give RED, so only accept if they personally value BLUE more than RED — the opposite of the proposer's direction). Dog, who most values GREEN, has a harder time getting trades accepted because other players typically value GREEN no more than what they would have to give up. Strategic uncertainty arises because when both non-senders would benefit from accepting, the proposer cannot choose the recipient; a random selection determines who executes the trade. Overall, expect frequent small 1-for-1 trades converting lower-valued chips into higher-valued ones for the proposer (particularly BLUE -> RED trades by Ram and Butterfly), while Dog's attempts to buy GREEN will often fail unless the terms compensate recipients who would otherwise lose value.

rounds.round_explanation: Opening moves. Ram and Butterfly both prefer RED over BLUE and will try small 1-for-1 BLUE->RED swaps. Dog will attempt to acquire GREEN but other players typically won't accept trades that give up RED or BLUE in exchange for GREEN, so Dog's GREEN-seeking proposals are likely to fail early.

Proposers continue converting BLUE into RED when profitable. Ram and Butterfly both can repeatedly propose BLUE->RED swaps that other players (who would receive BLUE and give RED) find acceptable because they value RED more than BLUE when acting as recipients. Dog again tries to buy GREEN but faces resistance because recipients typically prefer RED or BLUE to GREEN.

Final round. Ram and Butterfly continue to exploit RED>BLUE differentials with more 1-for-1 swaps. Dog's efforts to acquire GREEN remain mostly blocked unless a recipient is indifferent or gains; as a result, Dog often finishes with fewer successful trades. The random selection rule matters when both non-senders accept — it determines who actually provides the requested chips.

parsed_target.game_explanation: Three players repeatedly propose single-color-for-single-color swaps over three rounds. Each player privately values RED, BLUE, and a common-value GREEN; Greens are universally valuable (0.50) while RED/BLUE vary by player (Lion: RED=0.20, BLUE=0.20; Iguana: RED=0.50, BLUE=0.70; Alligator: RED=0.30, BLUE=0.70). Proposers will try to trade away chips they value relatively less to acquire chips they value more. Recipients decide based on whether the offered chip(s) exceed the value of the chip(s) they must give up. Because proposals are anonymous with respect to partner choice (if multiple accept, a recipient is chosen at random), proposers tend to make offers that create acceptance by at least one counterparty even if that requires a small value concession. Uncertainty comes from (1) private valuations (each player only knows their own values), (2) how many and which opponents will accept a given offer, and (3) random selection if multiple players accept. Given the value structure, we expect Lion to repeatedly pay a small premium in RED to acquire GREEN (Lion values GREEN much more than RED/BLUE), Iguana to trade GREEN for BLUE (BLUE is Iguana's highest-valued chip), and Alligator to try to buy BLUE by offering GREEN (or sometimes RED) because Alligator also values BLUE highly. Many trades will be accepted by Lion when he is offered GREEN in exchange for BLUE or by other players when they are paid enough RED to give up GREEN/BLUE.

rounds.round_explanation: Early in the game Lion will try to convert low-value RED into high-value GREEN; Iguana will try to convert GREEN into BLUE; Alligator will try to acquire BLUE by offering GREEN. Lion's offers must be attractive enough to overcome recipients' preference for GREEN (so Lion offers extra RED). Iguana targets Lion for BLUE by offering GREEN, since Lion undervalues BLUE. Alligator similarly offers GREEN to Lion for BLUE.

Players see the immediate effect of round 0 trades on holdings. Lion continues to prefer GREEN and is willing to pay two RED for one GREEN to ensure acceptance; Iguana continues to trade GREEN for BLUE targeting Lion who undervalues BLUE; Alligator again offers GREEN for BLUE and expects Lion to accept. Offers are small (one or two chips) to keep trades profitable for the proposer while attractive to at least one counterparty.

Late-game incentives are similar but holdings have shifted: Lion has accumulated GREEN and is an obvious partner when others offer GREEN for BLUE, while Iguana keeps converting GREEN into BLUE and Lion remains the likely counterparty. Alligator continues to buy BLUE cheaply by offering GREEN. Random selection can determine which recipient completes multi-accept offers, but proposers structure offers to ensure at least one acceptance.

parsed_target.game_explanation: Three players repeatedly propose one-for-one (or multi-for-one) chip swaps over three rounds. Each player privately values red, blue, and green differently: green is a common 0.50 to everyone, red is most valuable to whale and moderately valuable to dolphin but low for duck, while blue is the weakest asset for dolphin and duck and middling for whale. Given these priors, proposals will typically use blue as a low-value 'currency' to buy reds or greens from others. Proposers will offer chips they personally value less and request chips they value more; responders accept only when the trade increases their own private value. Uncertainty comes from not knowing others' private valuations and from random resolution when multiple responders accept: the proposer cannot choose the partner. Strategic considerations include crafting offers that create positive gains for at least one other player while minimizing the chance of creating multiple acceptors (because that introduces randomness). Over the three rounds we expect repeated small exchanges where whale accumulates reds, duck accumulates greens, and dolphin trades away blue to acquire reds/greens, with acceptances determined by individual gains given private values.

rounds.round_explanation: Opening round: proposers will use low-value blue to purchase higher-value greens and reds. Dolphin (who dislikes blue) will try to buy green by offering blue; whale (who highly values red) will try to acquire red by offering blue; duck (who values green strongly) will try to buy green in exchange for low-valued red. Responders will accept only if the swap raises their own value.

Mid-game: players update expectations based on public holdings. Dolphin continues converting blue into red (preferring red/green over blue). Whale continues to favor red and may sell some green to pick up extra red. Duck, who values green highly and red/blue low, opportunistically trades away red or blue to collect greens. Many proposals are designed to be attractive to at least one counterparty (often duck or whale).

Closing round: proposers pursue remaining gains. Dolphin may make larger blue-for-red offers to get guaranteed improvements; whale continues to try to accumulate red by offering blue; duck uses blue to buy green. Where offers are attractive to multiple responders, a random acceptance determines the trading partner, adding endgame uncertainty.

parsed_target.game_explanation: Three-player alternating-proposal bargaining where each player holds identical quantities (10 each) of red, blue, and green chips but has different private values for red and blue (green is common-value at $0.50). Proposers request one color and offer a different color; they cannot pick the accepting partner and so face uncertainty when an offer appeals to multiple recipients. Incentives: each player will try to trade away chips they value relatively less to obtain chips they value more. Because green is moderately valued (0.50) by everyone, it serves as a cheap way for a player who highly values red or blue to induce acceptance from players who prefer green. Given private values, zebra consistently prefers red (0.80) and dislikes blue (0.30), gorilla prefers green and blue over red (blue 0.40, red 0.20), and turtle strongly prefers blue (0.70) and dislikes red (0.10). Consequently, zebra will repeatedly propose trading green (or blue) for red; gorilla will propose trading red for blue; turtle will propose trading red for blue. Non-senders decide solely on their private values and current observed holdings: they accept if the offered chip is worth more to them than the chip they must give up. Because zebra's green-for-red offers look profitable from the recipients' perspectives (they gain green and give up red which they value less), those proposals attract multiple acceptances and are realized by a random accepting partner. Offers to swap red for blue by gorilla and turtle appeal primarily to zebra (who values red much more than blue), so zebra accepts those, yielding repeated one-for-one exchanges. The main uncertainties are (1) when proposers offer a type that multiple players prefer to the requested type, which partner will be randomly selected, and (2) whether proposers will adjust quantities in later rounds; given single-unit profitable trades, simple 1-for-1 proposals are the most likely path.

rounds.round_explanation: Opening round with symmetric starting holdings. Zebra (who values red most) will try to buy red by offering green; both gorilla and turtle prefer green to red so they will both be willing to accept, creating a random-recipient outcome. Gorilla and turtle will then seek blue (which they value more than red) by offering red; such offers appeal to zebra (who values red highly and blue less) and are accepted by zebra. Expect mainly 1-for-1 trades that transfer red to zebra and blue/green to others.

Holdings shifted: zebra has accumulated red and reduced blue/green modestly; gorilla and turtle hold more blue/green. Zebra repeats profitable green-for-red offers; again both others prefer green to the red they'd give up and will accept, producing another random-recipient execution. Gorilla continues to seek blue by offering red; zebra accepts such exchanges because zebra values red more than blue. Turtle similarly offers red for blue and zebra accepts. The pattern of zebra accumulating red and others accumulating blue/green continues.

Final short round follows the established pattern. Zebra again offers green for red, attracting both non-senders; one is randomly chosen as recipient. Gorilla and turtle each make one-for-one red-for-blue proposals; zebra continues to accept those because they increase zebra's total value. By the end, zebra has accumulated additional red chips while gorilla and turtle have increased blue/green holdings, consistent with each player's private valuations.

parsed_target.game_explanation: Three players start with identical chip endowments but different private valuations for red and blue chips (green is common-value). Each proposer will try to exchange chips they value less for chips they value more; responders accept if the trade raises their own private chip value. Because iguana values red highly (0.80) and blue poorly (0.30), they are frequently willing to give up blue to acquire red; cat and zebra both strongly prefer blue (0.70 and 0.80 respectively) and are willing to give red for blue. Strategic uncertainty arises because proposers cannot pick the accepting partner when multiple players would gain; random resolution of multiple acceptances and privately known valuations mean proposers infer acceptance probabilities rather than certainty. Over three rounds with three proposals per round, the dynamics favor repeated red-for-blue swaps: iguana accumulates reds while cat and zebra accumulate blues. Most proposals exchange 1-for-1 chips because small, clearly profitable swaps are low-risk and easy to accept given the private valuations.

rounds.round_explanation: Early in the game players exploit clear mutually beneficial 1-for-1 exchanges: cat and zebra seek blues and are willing to give reds; iguana seeks reds and is willing to give blues. Proposers will propose simple 1 red for 1 blue trades that are profitable for at least one other player. When both non-senders benefit from a proposal, both will accept and the recipient is randomly chosen.

With early trades having moved chips toward preferred holders (iguana accumulating reds, cat and zebra accumulating blues), proposers repeat the same profitable 1-for-1 red↔blue offers. Iguana continues to buy reds by offering blues and often finds multiple acceptors; cat repeatedly buys blues from iguana. These micro-trades continue to shift holdings in predictable directions.

By the final round most profitable 1-for-1 trades remain available because valuations differ strongly across players. Proposers continue the same pattern; when both non-senders benefit the recipient is randomly selected. Given the accumulated imbalance in holdings, iguana ends the game with many reds and few blues while cat and zebra have relatively more blues.

parsed_target.game_explanation: Three players with differing private values negotiate bilateral one-for-one (or small-quantity) chip exchanges over three rounds. Greens have identical common value (0.50) so they mainly serve as a medium for redistributing value between players who disagree about red vs. blue. Bear values blue the most (BLUE=1.00) and will repeatedly try to acquire blue by offering lower-valued chips (often red or green). Peacock values red above blue and green (RED=0.60, BLUE=0.40, GREEN=0.50) and therefore is willing to trade away blue to obtain red; peacock is a repeat acceptor of offers that give them red in exchange for blue. Swan also strongly prefers blue (BLUE=0.90) and will accept trades that exchange their lower-valued red (0.50) for blue, but will decline trades that force them to give up blue for lower-value chips. Because acceptances are private and multiple acceptances lead to a random selection of recipient, proposers craft offers that are strictly profitable for themselves while anticipating which opponents are likely to accept based on typical value rankings. Uncertainty comes from (1) proposers not knowing opponents' exact private valuations (though they can infer likely preferences), (2) random resolution when multiple opponents accept, and (3) changes in holdings over the game which alter who can afford or is willing to accept a given exchange. Over the three rounds we expect repeated small 1-for-1 trades: bear repeatedly offers lower-value chips to obtain blue; peacock repeatedly exchanges blue to obtain red; swan intermittently trades red for blue when it is cost-effective for them, and peacock often acts as the accepting counterparty because giving up blue is cheap for peacock while receiving red or green is beneficial.

rounds.round_explanation: Initial holdings are symmetric (10 of each chip). Bear, who values blue the most, opens by trying to buy a single blue by offering a green — that is attractive to peacock (who prefers green over blue) but not to swan. Peacock then uses blue (their weakest chip) to buy red, an exchange attractive to both bear and swan; with both accepting, random selection determines the recipient. Swan then attempts to buy blue by offering red; that is attractive to peacock (who values red > blue) and peacock accepts.

Holdings have shifted in favor of players who successfully traded. Bear continues to try to accumulate blue by offering red (now that bear has fewer greens), targeting peacock as the likely acceptor. Peacock reciprocates by again offering blue to buy red (profitable to peacock and attractive to both other players), and the random resolution selects one recipient. Swan repeats the pattern of buying a blue by offering red; peacock again finds it attractive to accept.

By the final round bear remains focused on acquiring blue via small red-for-blue offers, and peacock continues to capitalize on exchanging their relatively cheap blue for red. Both bear and swan occasionally accept when red-to-blue swaps are clearly profitable; peacock is the frequent acceptor when a proposal asks for blue because giving up blue is least costly for peacock. Several trades in this round follow the established pattern and are executed.

parsed_target.game_explanation: This three-round, three-player bargaining game is driven by heterogeneous private values over the red and blue chips and a common value for green. Gorilla (G) strongly prefers red (1.00) and dislikes blue (0.10); Camel (C) prefers blue (0.60) and dislikes red (0.30); Monkey (M) values red (1.00) and blue (0.90) highly and green is middling (0.50) for all. Early trades are likely to move chips from players who value them less (Camel's red, Gorilla's blue) to players who value them more, with proposers offering away low-value chips to request high-value chips. Because proposers cannot pick the recipient, they craft proposals that are attractive to at least one plausible counterparty; when both non-senders benefit both typically accept and a recipient is chosen at random. Over time holdings change and some mutually profitable trades become infeasible (because the cheap supplier runs out of the requested color) or unattractive, so later rounds contain more declined proposals. Uncertainty centers on which non-sender(s) will accept when a proposal benefits more than one player and whether proposers must overpay to secure a desired chip from a reluctant supplier.

rounds.round_explanation: At the start everyone holds 10 of each color. Gorilla will try to trade away low-value blue to acquire red (which Gorilla values most). Camel, who values red least, is a natural trading partner for red-heavy requests; Monkey also sometimes finds blue attractive enough to accept. Early offers are generous enough that both non-senders can accept on some turns, leading to random recipient selection.

Holdings have shifted: Gorilla accumulated red but has few blues, Camel has many blues and few/no red, and Monkey has increased red. Gorilla can cheaply trade blue for green (both non-senders may accept because green is worth more than blue to them), while Camel can make small, targeted swaps to get a bit more blue from Gorilla. Monkey attempts a balanced swap but finds no profitable counterparty and is declined.

By the final round many of the mutually beneficial exchanges are exhausted or infeasible (some players lack the requested color). Proposals that would benefit the sender are often unattractive to the only potential suppliers given current holdings, so several late proposals are declined.

parsed_target.game_explanation: Three players start with equal holdings of red, blue, and green chips but different private valuations for red and blue (green is common-value). Proposers can request one color and offer another; each responder privately accepts only if the swap raises their own total value given their private values. Because alligator values red very little and blue moderately, she will repeatedly try to sell red for blue. Tiger values both red and blue highly but red slightly less than blue, so he is willing to give up a blue if compensated with multiple reds (or otherwise receive red for green) — meaning many of alligator's red-for-blue offers will be attractive to tiger but not to otter. Otter strongly values blue (highest), so he rarely gives up blue for anything except unusually generous terms; he will accept some deals that convert his lower-valued chips (like red) into green. Because players only know their own values, proposers optimistically offer small profitable trades for themselves that are also likely to appeal to one opponent; responders accept purely on private payoff comparisons, so we expect a pattern where alligator repeatedly trades small bundles of her low-value red for blue and otter occasionally trades red for green, with tiger frequently acting as the recipient. Some proposals that are beneficial only to the proposer but harmful to both opponents are declined. Uncertainty for each player concerns whether a proposal will be attractive enough for at least one other player to accept and whether a competing acceptor might be randomly selected when multiple accept. The overall dynamic favors repeated small exchanges that exploit asymmetries (alligator's cheap red, otter's expensive blue, tiger's appetite for red quantity) and leads to several accepted one-to-one or two-for-one exchanges, with a few declined offers when terms are unfavorable to all responders.

rounds.round_explanation: Early in the game all players still hold many chips. Alligator, who devalues red, will try to trade red for blue in small bundles to gain value. Otter may try a modest red->green swap to slightly increase his value. Tiger, who benefits from receiving extra red (and sometimes green), is the most likely recipient of these targeted offers. Proposals that require responders to give up high-valued blue for little return will be declined.

With a public history showing tiger frequently accepting red-for-other trades, alligator repeats a profitable red-for-blue offer. Otter again proposes a small red->green conversion (a slight private gain) expecting tiger to find it attractive. Tiger's own proposals tend to be ones that increase his blue share, but such proposals generally harm both opponents and are declined.

Patterns solidify: alligator continues converting low-valued red into blue when tiger is available as a recipient; otter repeats the small red->green trade which tiger prefers; tiger proposes sensible value-increasing trades but these often require opponents to give up higher-valued blue and thus are declined. The result is several small, repeated accepted trades benefiting the proposer and tiger as recipient, and some declined attempts when terms are unfavorable to both responders.

parsed_target.game_explanation: Three players start with equal holdings (10 of each color) but have different private valuations for red and blue; green is common-value and highest for all (0.50). Incentives push players to acquire greens and, for frog, to accumulate reds (frog values red at 0.90). Proposers can exploit asymmetric valuations by offering low-value chips (for them) to buy higher-value chips (for them). Responders accept if the exchange raises their own private total value given the exact quantities on offer. Uncertainty arises because proposers cannot target the accepting partner (if multiple accept, one accepting partner is chosen at random) and because each player only knows their own private values while observing public holdings and past trades. Early rounds will feature aggressive offers for green by dolphin (who values red/blue very little) and targeted attempts by frog to acquire red; pig tends to trade moderately (prefers green > red > blue) and will accept offers that give small positive gains.

rounds.round_explanation: Initial bargaining with symmetric holdings. Dolphin wants greens (high personal value) and is likely to offer low-value red/blue. Frog prefers red and may try to buy red cheaply. Pig is middling and will accept only beneficial trades. Expect initial probing offers; some will be declined if they hurt likely responders.

After a successful early trade (frog gained a red from pig), frog has more red; dolphin still values green highly and can buy green cheaply by offering extra blues. Proposers may intentionally overpay slightly (in terms of others' valuations) to induce acceptance; responders accept only if their own value increases.

Players have diverged in holdings: frog accumulated reds, pig and dolphin moved chips around. Dolphin continues to buy green using cheap blues; frog still seeks red and will trade greens for reds when profitable for responders. Pig continues to transact when it produces a small positive gain.

parsed_target.game_explanation: Three self-interested players each start with 10 chips of each color. Green is a common-value chip ($0.50) while red and blue have player-specific private values: iguana values green most, then red, then blue; owl values red most, then blue, then green; squid values red most, with blue and green equal. Because each player only knows their own red/blue/private preferences, proposals are driven by each sender trying to swap chips they value less for chips they value more. Responders accept only when the proposed exchange strictly increases their personal total value (or at least is not negative); this creates predictable patterns: players with high red-values will sell green/blue to obtain red, and players who under-value blue will offer blue in exchange for green. Uncertainty arises from two sources: (1) private valuations — proposers cannot perfectly predict which counterparty will accept, so some proposals risk multiple acceptances and random partner selection; (2) random selection when multiple responders accept. The likely dynamics are sequential targeted trades where proposers choose offers that are modestly profitable to them but attractive to at least one other player, producing several one-to-one accepted swaps that reallocate green toward the player who values it most (iguana) and red toward those who value red most (owl and squid).

rounds.round_explanation: Early play: proposers attempt clearly advantageous swaps. Iguana (who values green highest) will try to trade low-value blue for green; owl will try to accumulate red by offering green; squid will likewise try to convert green into additional red. Responders accept whenever they personally gain value, which sometimes leads to multiple acceptances and a random recipient.

With updated holdings, players continue making small profitable swaps. Proposers prefer offers that will be accepted by at least one other player (targeting the likely beneficiary). Because iguana strongly prefers green, several proposals offering red or blue for green are attractive to iguana; owl repeatedly trades some green for red; squid seeks additional red by giving green.

Final round: remaining mutually profitable one-for-one swaps occur. Proposers make modest exchanges that leave them better off while enticing a single counterparty to accept. Because iguana consistently values green most, iguana continues to receive green in exchange for lower-value chips; owl and squid acquire additional red when that is profitable.

parsed_target.game_explanation: This 3-round, 3-player chip bargaining game centers on asymmetric private values over red vs. blue chips and a common (identical) valuation for green chips. Greens are neutral (0.50 for everyone) so they are unlikely to be traded in directed exchanges; bargains will focus on moving red and blue toward players who privately value them more. Gorilla (RED=0.80, BLUE=0.50) strongly prefers red and is willing to give blue; Llama (BLUE=0.80, RED=0.60) strongly prefers blue and is willing to give red; Whale (RED=0.60, BLUE=0.30) prefers red moderately and dislikes blue. Proposers will therefore typically request the color they value more and offer the color they value less (usually 1-for-1 trades here). Because each player only knows their own valuations, proposers must infer which counterparty is likely to accept based on public holdings and common ranges; when both non-senders would benefit the trade, both will accept and a random accepting partner is selected. Over repeated turns we expect Llama to accumulate blue chips (because many other players get positive gains by giving red to obtain blue), Gorilla and Whale to accumulate red, and greens to remain largely unchanged. The forecast below uses these analyst-side priors about private values and executes plausible 1-for-1 proposals and deterministic accept/decline responses consistent with value-improving behavior for each recipient (accept iff the recipient's total chip value increases).

rounds.round_explanation: Opening round: proposers make small, conservative 1-for-1 offers. Gorilla targets red by offering blue (appeals to Llama who values blue highly). Llama targets blue by offering red (both others would gain by exchanging a red for a blue), and Whale likewise tries to buy red by offering blue (primarily attractive to Llama). Expect most proposals to succeed, often with Llama accepting many offers and occasionally a random selection when both accept.

Mid-game: patterns from round 0 reinforce themselves. Gorilla continues to buy red with blue; Llama keeps buying blue with red (often attracting both potential partners and leaving the random draw to determine who trades); Whale repeats attempts to convert blue into red but typically only Llama finds it profitable to accept. Trades remain 1-for-1 and all proposals that create positive net value for at least one counterparty are accepted.

Endgame: players consolidate holdings consistent with revealed tendencies. Gorilla keeps acquiring red by giving blue (usually to Llama). Llama, having repeatedly gained blue at a favorable price, continues to accept trades that increase her total value. Whale still tries to buy red with blue but typically trades with Llama. Greens remain untouched throughout.

parsed_target.game_explanation: Three players (duck, lion, tiger) repeatedly propose 1-for-1 or multi-for-1 chip trades across three rounds. Each player knows only their own chip values and seeks to maximize their personal total value. Greens are middling (0.50) and common; ducks value red and blue highly (1.00 each), lion moderately prefers red (0.90) over blue (0.50), and tiger strongly prefers red (1.00) but devalues blue (0.30). These private preferences make green a convenient currency to move value between players and make multi-blue offers attractive to extract red from players who underprice blue. Uncertainty arises because proposers cannot choose among multiple acceptors and acceptors decide simultaneously and privately; proposers therefore balance attractiveness to potential recipients against their own costs. Forecasted play features proposers offering quantities that make the trade net-positive (or at least non-negative) for plausible acceptors given their private values, leading to several accepted exchanges where low-valued chips (for the proposer or recipient) are used to obtain high-valued red chips.

rounds.round_explanation: Early bargaining: duck leverages green (0.5) to buy blue (valuable to duck) and attracts tiger (who prefers green over blue). Lion attempts a small red-for-green swap but faces resistance because red is worth more to others; tiger then uses under-valued blue to buy red by offering two blues, which is attractive to both duck and lion and results in a random recipient.

After initial reallocations, lion pursues small, low-risk trades to pick up blue (selling green), which appeals to tiger because tiger devalues blue. Tiger again leverages low-valued blue to try to acquire red by offering two blues, an offer attractive enough for both other players, yielding another accepted exchange.

Final round: players make targeted trades that exploit remaining mismatches. Lion successfully trades a red for two blues by appealing to tiger (who values red highly and blue less), and tiger finishes by swapping a low-valued blue for a green with duck — a small, mutually beneficial exchange.

parsed_target.game_explanation: Three players repeatedly propose one-for-one (or one-for-two) chip exchanges to improve their individual portfolio value given private valuations. Bear highly values BLUE and dislikes RED, so as proposer Bear will repeatedly try to buy BLUE by offering RED. Goose values RED and BLUE equally and values GREEN less, so Goose will opportunistically trade GREEN for RED/BLUE when it increases her value; she is also willing to accept large RED bundles for BLUE if profitable. Ram values BLUE > RED and will propose trades to acquire BLUE using RED, but will only get deals accepted if the price compensates the recipient. Greens have a common value (0.50) and often serve as a low-cost offer for Goose to extract higher-value REDs. Key strategic features: proposers cannot choose among multiple acceptors, so they must weigh the expected probability-weighted benefit of offers that might attract more than one acceptor; responders decide only on private gains from the single trade they would execute. Uncertainty stems from (1) not knowing other players' private valuations, (2) random selection when multiple players accept, and (3) how future proposers will react to the updated holdings. Behavior forecast: Bear will repeatedly offer REDs to buy BLUEs at a favorable exchange rate (e.g., 2 REDs for 1 BLUE) because that increases Bear's value; Goose will accept exchanges that convert GREEN into RED (GAIN) or accept lucrative RED bundles for BLUE when profitable; Ram will try to buy BLUE with RED but often faces declines unless he posts a sufficiently generous package (which may not be in his own interest).

rounds.round_explanation: Early-round dynamics: proposers try straightforward, high-likelihood gains. Bear (highly preferring BLUE) offers cheap REDs for BLUEs to capture surplus. Goose uses GREEN as a cheap currency to buy REDs because her valuations favor RED/B LUE over GREEN. Ram tests buying BLUE by offering a single RED but faces declines unless he makes the offer more generous.

Middle-round adjustments: holdings have shifted so Bear can continue offering RED bundles for BLUE and may attract multiple acceptors. Goose continues converting GREEN into RED when it's personally profitable. Ram repeats smaller offers to buy BLUE but often finds them unattractive to others unless he posts a much larger (and potentially unprofitable) package.

Endgame: players solidify positions. Bear again posts the same favorable RED-for-BLUE exchange and attracts acceptances; Goose continues profitable GREEN->RED swaps with Bear. Ram continues to propose small RED-for-BLUE trades but is usually declined unless he posts a larger offer that would reduce his own gain.

parsed_target.game_explanation: This three-round, three-player chip-bargaining game centers on asymmetric private values for RED and BLUE chips and a common-value GREEN. Two players (lion-blue-472 and ram-purple-2824) strongly prefer BLUE (0.60 and 1.00 respectively), while octopus-green-4361 values BLUE very little (0.10) and prefers GREEN (0.50). GREEN is uniformly valued at 0.50 by all players. Given these priors, octopus is the natural ``liquidity provider'': he is willing to trade away BLUE for RED or GREEN because those return higher private value to him, while lion and ram will aggressively acquire BLUE by offering lower-valued RED or GREEN. Proposers will request a chip color they privately value more than the chip they offer, and recipients will accept when the incoming chip is worth more to them than the chip they must give up. Uncertainty appears in two places: (1) proposers cannot target a specific partner if multiple players accept — the partner is chosen at random — and (2) players only know their own values, so they infer others' likely responses from public history and typical incentives. Overall we expect repeated small 1-for-1 trades where octopus sells BLUE in exchange for RED (or sometimes GREEN -> he swaps his low-value BLUE for higher-value chips), and lion and ram alternate in buying BLUE. Trades that exchange low-valued chips for higher-valued ones for the recipient will be accepted; trades that require a recipient to give up a chip they value more than the offer will be declined.
