rounds.round_explanation: Opening round: players start symmetric in holdings (10 each). Lion moves first and, valuing BLUE more than RED/GREEN, will try to buy BLUE by offering RED; octopus (who values RED > BLUE) is likely to accept while ram will decline. Octopus then uses his low BLUE value to trade BLUE for GREEN (he offers BLUE to get GREEN); both lion and ram would prefer BLUE to GREEN, so both may accept and a random selection determines the recipient — here ram is chosen. Ram finishes the round by buying BLUE from octopus (offering RED), which octopus accepts.

By round 1 octopus has become a net seller of BLUE and accumulated RED/GREEN. Lion and ram continue to target BLUE using RED as the offer; octopus, preferring RED/GREEN to BLUE, accepts these exchanges. When octopus proposes again to swap BLUE for GREEN, both lion and ram have an incentive to accept (they value BLUE more than GREEN), so the recipient will be randomly chosen — here ram is chosen again. Expect continued small 1-for-1 exchanges with octopus as the primary BLUE supplier.

Final round: patterns from earlier rounds persist. Lion and ram continue purchasing BLUE by offering RED; octopus, with depleted BLUE holdings and increased RED/GREEN, keeps accepting as these trades raise his private value. Octopus's proposals to swap BLUE for GREEN remain attractive to both buyers, but random selection determines who supplies GREEN. The outcome is additional 1-for-1 shifts of BLUE toward lion/ram and RED/GREEN toward octopus.

parsed_target.game_explanation: Three self-interested players begin with identical endowments (10 of each color) but different private valuations for red and blue; green is common-value (0.50). The proposer on each turn offers one color in exchange for another. Strategic logic centers on trading away chips you value relatively little to obtain chips you value more. Buffalo highly values red (0.90) and disfavors blue (0.10), so he will repeatedly try to sell blue to buy red. Duck highly values blue (0.90) and disfavors red (0.40), so he will sell red to buy blue or accept offers that trade away red for blue. Cat has intermediate/low values (red 0.40, blue 0.20, green 0.50) and will seek greens by trading away the color she values least (blue or red) when it yields a gain. Because acceptors only compare the private value they give up to the private/common value they receive, many proposals are attractive to exactly one other player (creating single accepts) while proposals that trade green for blue or red for blue often attract multiple accepters and thus lead to randomized recipients. Uncertainty arises primarily from (1) proposers not controlling partner selection when multiple players accept and (2) incomplete information about opponents' private red/blue valuations; observers can predict responses because private values are known to us, but players act only on their own values and the public history. Over three rounds we expect repeated, relatively small 1-for-1 swaps: buffalo repeatedly sells blue for red (accepted by duck), cat repeatedly trades away blue or red to get green (accepted by duck or buffalo depending on the price), and duck alternates between selling red for blue or selling green for blue to accumulate blue—sometimes causing multi-accept situations and random assignment of the counterparty.

rounds.round_explanation: Opening round: proposers test which partners will accept simple 1-for-1 swaps. Buffalo will try to convert low-value blue into high-value red and expect duck (who values blue highly) to accept. Cat will try to buy green by selling blue (she gains), which is attractive to duck (who values blue highly). Duck will favor proposals that trade away green (0.50) to get additional blue (0.90) so that both buffalo and cat (who have low blue values) may accept, producing a contested acceptance resolved at random.

Players continue with profitable 1-for-1 swaps. Buffalo repeats selling blue to buy red (duck consistently accepts). Cat repeats buying green by selling blue; duck remains the marginal accepter. Duck, seeking more blue, attempts to buy blue by offering red; both others benefit from giving blue to receive red in that deal, so multiple accepts create a random recipient.

Endgame round: Buffalo and Cat make last-effort gains by converting low-value colors into higher-value colors. Buffalo again sells blue to buy red (duck accepts). Cat shifts to buying green by selling red when that trade attracts buffalo as a counterparty. Duck finishes by selling red to buy blue; when both other players accept, the recipient is chosen at random.

parsed_target.game_explanation: This three-round, three-player bargaining game centers on asymmetric private valuations for red and blue chips and a common-value green chip. Each player starts with equal endowments but different private preferences: squid strongly prefers blue, kangaroo strongly prefers red, and butterfly has intermediate preferences favoring green. Because proposals must trade one color for a different color, proposers will typically offer chips they value least and request chips they value most. Strategic considerations: proposers try to craft one-for-one or small-quantity swaps that target a single counterparty (by offering a color that one non-sender values more than the requested color while the other values it less) to avoid random assignment among multiple accepters. Recipients accept if the trade increases their private total chip value; this creates predictable accept/decline patterns given the known valuations. Uncertainty remains because acceptances are private and, when both non-senders would accept, the actual recipient is chosen at random — so proposers risk losing the identity of the partner. The forecast below uses these incentives and the private-value priors to predict plausible proposals and which non-senders accept each turn.

rounds.round_explanation: Early-round dynamics: proposers try low-cost conversions. Butterfly will likely sell low-valued blue to buy green; kangaroo will try to buy red by selling blue (targeting squid who values blue highly); squid will convert green into blue by offering green (cheap to squid relative to blue they'd get) hoping at least one other accepts.

With holdings shifted, players repeat similar conversions. Butterfly continues to trade away blue to obtain red or green; kangaroo concentrates on acquiring more red by offering blue; squid continues to leverage their high blue valuation to extract red or convert low-valued red/green into more blue. When both non-senders would gain from a swap, the recipient is determined randomly (we select a plausible realized recipient).

Final-round play focuses on last-minute value adjustments. Butterfly and kangaroo again offer their weaker-valued blues to buy higher-valued greens or reds respectively, and squid continues to turn lower-valued reds/greens into additional blues. Because accept/decline decisions are driven purely by private gains, squid is frequently the unique accepter when offered blue-for-red or blue-for-green swaps, but random assignment can still allocate trades to either non-sender when both benefit.

parsed_target.game_explanation: This three-round, three-proposer bargaining game centers on asymmetric private values for red and blue chips and a common public value for green chips. All players start with equal endowments (10 of each color), but their incentives diverge: bat values blue most (0.60) and red least (0.20), fox highly values blue (1.00) and moderately values red (0.60), while frog strongly prefers red (0.70) and places low value on blue (0.30). Because proposers cannot choose which acceptor gets a trade if multiple players accept, proposers tend to craft offers that are attractive to at least one other player who values what the proposer is offering more than the requested chips. Given these private values, frog is the most frequent marginal seller of blue (frog values blue the least), and is therefore the natural counterparty for repeated trades where proposers offer red (low value to bat, moderate to fox) in exchange for blue. Greens are common-value (0.50) and thus harder to trade for profitable unilateral gains for all players; trades involving green produce smaller surpluses and are less central to the dynamics. Uncertainty arises because each player only knows their own valuations and because when multiple players accept a proposal the realized recipient is chosen at random; proposers must therefore balance the size of their personal gain with the likelihood of attracting at least one acceptor.

rounds.round_explanation: In round 0 players probe profitable bilateral swaps. Bat and fox both seek additional blue chips (their highest-valued color) and target frog—who values blue the least—to supply them by offering red. Frog prefers red strongly, so it will accept exchanges that trade one blue away for one red. Frog, when proposing, tries to buy more red by offering blue, which is attractive to both bat and fox. Expect simple 1-for-1 red↔blue swaps that benefit both the proposer and at least one other player.

Having observed that frog is willing to sell blue for red, proposers continue to propose 1-for-1 red↔blue trades. Bat and fox again try to buy blue by offering red; frog often accepts because the exchange increases frog's own value. When frog proposes to buy red with blue, both bat and fox find that beneficial and may both accept, creating random assignment of the recipient.

In the final round proposals follow the established pattern: bat and fox continue to target frog to buy blue with red, and frog seeks more red by offering blue. Because frog consistently values blue less than the others, these 1-for-1 red↔blue trades remain mutually beneficial. With multiple acceptances possible when frog proposes, the realized recipient is randomly chosen among acceptors.

parsed_target.game_explanation: Three players repeatedly trade single-color chips for single-color chips. Each player privately values red, blue and green differently (blue is most valuable to dog and turtle at $1.00; parrot values red highest at $0.60; green is common $0.50 for all). Proposers will typically request the color they value most and offer a color they value less. Acceptors decide based on their private values: they accept only if the chips they receive are worth at least as much as the chips they give up, so many profitable trades for the proposer are attractive to the low-value holder of the requested chip (parrot is the frequent seller of blue because parrot values blue only $0.30). When multiple responders would gain, both accept and a recipient is chosen at random; when only one responder stands to gain, that responder alone accepts and the trade executes deterministically. Uncertainty arises because each player only knows their own valuations and must infer others' preferences from past trades and current holdings; repeated rounds allow some learning, but each decision is still driven by private payoff-maximization rather than maximizing group surplus.

rounds.round_explanation: Early-round incentives: dog and turtle strongly prefer blue and will offer lower-valued red to acquire blue. Parrot has low blue value, so is likely to sell blue for better chips (red or green). Proposers will offer minimal one-for-one exchanges that are profitable for themselves and likely profitable for at least one other player; multiple acceptances are possible when trading low-value blue for higher-value red/green.

Holdings have shifted to give blue-rich players slightly different endowments, but incentives remain: dog and turtle continue to target blue; parrot still finds selling blue attractive when receiving red or green. Parrot's proposals to buy red by offering blue tend to be accepted by both dog and turtle (who value blue highly), so recipient selection becomes random when both gain.

Late-round play continues the pattern: dog repeatedly buys blue by offering red and typically finds parrot willing to accept; parrot keeps using scarce blue to acquire red (or green) when profitable, often creating two-person competition among the blue-loving players and thus random assignment of the recipient. Turtle similarly buys blue from parrot when parrot's blue is cheap relative to red.

parsed_target.game_explanation: Three players each start with 10 red, 10 blue, 10 green chips. Greens are highest-value common chips (0.50 for everyone); blues are lowest (0.10). Badger and Peacock value red at 0.30 while Unicorn values red at 0.50. Proposers try to increase their own total chip value by offering some chips and requesting others. Because green is universally the most valuable chip, proposals that ask others to give up green in exchange for lower-value chips (e.g., blue) are unlikely to be accepted. Similarly, asking for red in exchange for blue is also unattractive to potential givers. The one feasible bilateral pattern is when a proposer offers green in exchange for red: recipients who value green more than red (badger and peacock do) gain by accepting, while a proposer who values red at least as much as green (Unicorn values red = green) may be willing to make an essentially neutral exchange. Uncertainty enters because acceptances are private and, when multiple players would accept, the recipient is chosen randomly. Anticipating others' private valuations is limited (players only know their own values), so proposers tend to test simple 1-for-1 swaps. Given the specific values, most low-cost buy attempts (requesting green or red while offering blue) are predicted to be declined; the plausible accepted trades are 1-for-1 GREEN-for-RED proposals made by Unicorn (who is indifferent between red and green) and accepted by Badger or Peacock (who strictly prefer green to red).

rounds.round_explanation: Initial exploratory round. Proposers will try simple 1-for-1 proposals. Because green is valuable to everyone, offers that request green while only offering blue or red are expected to be declined. Early proposals are likely tests and mostly rejected.

Players see no holdings change from round 0. Badger and Peacock continue to test straightforward trades (likely declined). Unicorn recognizes that offering GREEN to get RED is acceptable to Badger and Peacock (they prefer green to red) while Unicorn is indifferent, so Unicorn proposes a GREEN-for-RED swap that both others prefer; both accept and a random accepting recipient is chosen.

After Unicorn successfully converts one green into one red (recipient Peacock), holdings slightly shift: Unicorn has an extra red, Peacock has an extra green. Subsequent simple requests for green or red in exchange for blue remain unattractive and are declined. Unicorn again can offer GREEN for RED; both other players prefer green to red and will accept, leading to another 1-for-1 GREEN-for-RED swap. This time the random selection yields Badger as the recipient.

parsed_target.game_explanation: Three risk-neutral, myopic players each start with 10 of each color. Greens are common-value (0.50) and are intermediate for all players; blue is highly valued by dog and owl (1.00) and moderately by mouse (0.60); red is valued heterogeneously (dog 0.80, owl 0.30, mouse 0.20). Proposers must offer one color and request a different color. Trades will only execute when a responder expects a private-value gain by exchanging the requested chips for the offered chips. Because players only accept trades that increase their own total chip value, profitable proposals for the sender require offering chips that responders value more than the chips requested. This generates two characteristic dynamics in the observed play: (1) players who value a chip highly (dog and owl for blue; dog for red) have trouble acquiring more of it one-for-one because others value it at least as highly; they instead successfully purchase preferred chips by offering bundles of lower-value chips that make responders better off in aggregate, and (2) players with low value for red (mouse) can cheaply buy greens by offering multiple reds (or sell reds profitably), producing several multi-unit exchanges where recipients are compensated with several low-value chips. Uncertainty in partner selection (when multiple responders accept, the recipient is randomly selected) leads proposers to sometimes propose offers attractive to multiple opponents, accepting randomness in who becomes partner. The forecast below uses the full private-value profile to predict which proposals are profitable for the sender and acceptable to responders, and simulates random resolution when multiple responders accept.

rounds.round_explanation: Early stage: all players start balanced. Dog (high red and blue values) can cheaply convert greens into reds because both other players prefer receiving green to giving up red. Mouse, with low red value, can then use accumulated greens/red positions to conduct further multi-unit swaps. Owl aims to acquire blue by selling red bundles, which is attractive to dog who values red highly. Expect several accepted exchanges where proposers buy the chip they value more by offering quantities of a chip others value relatively more.

After initial reallocations dog accumulates red and has fewer greens; he repeats green-for-red swaps because they increase his private value and are attractive to others. Mouse, having traded away many reds but accumulated greens, again uses small bundles of reds to buy greens (profitable for him and for recipients). Owl continues to convert red into blue by offering multi-red bundles; dog, who highly values red, is a frequent acceptor of blue-for-red offers.

Endgame incentives: dog continues to prefer red over green and purchases an additional red for green when feasible; mouse repeats the low-cost red->green swaps while he still has some reds; owl again purchases blue by offering red bundles that make dog (the high-red valuer) strictly better off. Because proposers cannot choose which accepting partner is realized, many proposals are designed to be attractive to multiple responders; randomness in partner selection is resolved whenever both other players accept.

parsed_target.game_explanation: Three risk-free rounds of sequential proposals between three players with heterogeneous private values. Greens are common-valued (0.5) while reds and blues differ: Alligator moderately prefers red (0.6) > blue/green (0.5), Buffalo is indifferent between red and green (0.5) but dislikes blue (0.2), and Rabbit strongly prefers red (1.0) > green (0.5) > blue (0.3). Proposers will try to get chips they value more by offering chips the recipients value less. Because each player only knows their own values, proposers target trades that are attractive to particular opponents (e.g., offering blue to Alligator is attractive because Alligator values blue highly, offering green to Buffalo is attractive because Buffalo values green as much as red). Acceptors decide only on their private gains; indifferent players (Buffalo vs. red/green swaps) may accept or reject, creating some unpredictability. Multiple acceptances create a random-selection risk for the proposer, so some proposals aim for a single likely acceptor (by tailoring compensation), while others that appeal broadly can produce multiple acceptances. Over three rounds, trade opportunities that leave a clear positive private gain for one counterparty tend to be accepted and executed; proposers avoid trades that leave them with a clear negative private return.

rounds.round_explanation: At the start everyone has 10 of each color. Early proposals test the market for red, the most contested chip (especially prized by Rabbit). Alligator initially tries a low-cost attempt to get red but is turned down because recipients would lose value. Buffalo then offers blue to buy red in a way that appeals to Alligator (who values blue), producing a bilateral trade. Rabbit then opportunistically buys a red from Buffalo by offering a green; Buffalo is indifferent between red and green and accepts.

With updated holdings, players repeat profitable, targeted offers. Alligator proposes a low-friction green-for-blue swap that is mildly attractive to both others (both gain privately from exchanging low-valued blue for green or vice versa), producing multiple acceptances; one counterparty is randomly selected as the trading partner. Buffalo then again tries to buy red by offering blue; Alligator is an appealing partner and accepts. Rabbit finishes the round by buying another red from Buffalo with a green, where Buffalo again accepts because the swap is roughly value-neutral for them.

Endgame behavior focuses on securing marginal gains in red holdings. Alligator obtains another red from Buffalo by offering a green (Buffalo is indifferent and accepts). Buffalo then buys a red from Alligator by offering blue, selecting the partner (Alligator) who stands to gain from the blue. Finally Rabbit pays with blue to secure one more red from Alligator; Alligator accepts because the blue payment compensates them.

parsed_target.game_explanation: Three-player, three-round bargaining with unilateral single-item proposals per turn. Each proposer requests one color and offers a different color (one unit each here). Players are private-value maximizers (each knows only their own valuations) and will accept a proposal precisely when the chip(s) they would receive are worth at least as much as the chip(s) they must give, with strict gains driving acceptance in our forecast. Green is common-value (0.50 for all); other colors differ: badger prefers BLUE > GREEN > RED > PURPLE, cat strongly prefers PURPLE, dog strongly prefers RED. This creates repeated opportunities where a proposer offers a low-value chip to them (from the proposer's perspective) to buy a chip another player values more. Uncertainty exists about which non-sender will accept when multiple players would gain — the protocol chooses a random accepting recipient. Anticipation of others' private preferences shapes proposals: proposers usually offer a color they themselves rank low (to minimize personal cost) and request a color they personally value highly; they also choose trades likely to be accepted by at least one counterparty based on observable trading history and typical preference patterns. Over three rounds we expect repeated low-cost-for-proposer trades (e.g., PURPLE -> BLUE for badger; RED -> PURPLE for cat; BLUE -> RED for dog), with accepts by players for whom the swap is value-improving. Because each accepted trade updates holdings, later proposals reflect both updated endowments and unchanged private valuations.

rounds.round_explanation: Early play: proposers exploit obvious cross-player complementarities. Badger will try to trade away cheap PURPLE to obtain BLUE. Cat will leverage others' lower PURPLE valuations to acquire more PURPLE by offering low-value RED. Dog will seek more RED by offering PURPLE. Acceptances are driven by simple value comparisons given private valuations; when multiple players would gain, a random accepting recipient is realized.

With updated holdings, proposers repeat profitable patterns. Badger continues swapping his low-value PURPLE for BLUE. Cat looks for small, safe improvements (buying GREEN by selling RED) that are attractive to dog but not to badger. Dog continues to accumulate RED by offering BLUE, a trade that benefits either non-sender and therefore risks a random partner.

Final-round incentives: proposers still try low-cost-for-them swaps to pick up their preferred colors. Cat repeats small safe GREEN-for-RED trades attractive to dog; dog continues to buy RED using BLUE and may again face multiple acceptors. Because players are myopic value-maximizers, the pattern of PURPLE-for-BLUE, RED-for-PURPLE, and BLUE-for-RED trades persists.

parsed_target.game_explanation: Three players with distinct private values for red/blue/purple and a common-value green engage in three rounds of bilateral-proposal trades. Each proposer requests one color and offers another; recipients accept only if the trade increases their private total value. Buffalo strongly prefers blue, goose strongly prefers red, and bear mildly prefers green; this creates complementary trading incentives: buffalo will offer cheap reds/purples to acquire blues, goose will offer greens or blues to get reds, and bear will offer low-value blues/purples to obtain greens. Because players do not know others' private valuations, proposers favor trades that are profitable for themselves while likely attractive to at least one counterparty (i.e., offering a chip that is cheap to the proposer but relatively valuable to likely recipients). Simultaneous responses by non-senders can produce multiple acceptances; a random selection among acceptors determines the recipient, so proposers sometimes obtain a different partner than intended. Public holdings evolve immediately after accepted trades, providing additional information for subsequent turns and shifting bargaining power (players who accumulate their preferred color become less willing to trade it away). The forecasted sequence reflects these incentives and the acceptance rule based on private value gains.

rounds.round_explanation: Opening moves trade obvious mismatches: buffalo targets blue by offering low-value red (profitable for both potential recipients), goose reciprocates by buying red when buffalo has extra blue, and bear trades off low-value purple to acquire green. Early trades both reveal preferences and reallocate attractive chips toward the high-valuing players.

With updated holdings, proposers push similarly advantageous swaps. Buffalo again converts a low-valued red into green (profitable for a green-deficit player), goose trades a red for a blue where a low-valuing blue holder will accept, and bear purchases green with red where a red-loving recipient is willing to sell green.

Final-round proposals exploit remaining favorable margins. Buffalo again targets blue by offering red (bear is the likely acceptor), goose attempts a more aggressive swap (buying red by selling green) but is turned down because both others value red more than green, and bear finishes by exchanging a low-value blue for a green that both others would find attractive—resulting in another accepted green acquisition.

parsed_target.game_explanation: This three-round, three-player bargaining game centers on pairwise one-for-one (or small integer) chip swaps where each proposer tries to increase their own total chip value by trading away chips they value relatively less for chips they value more. Key incentives: dog-orange values RED and BLUE highest (0.7) and GREEN lowest (0.5), buffalo-green most dislikes PURPLE (0.1) and values RED/BLUE at 0.6, and bear-red values GREEN highest (0.5) while valuing RED lowest (0.3). Because players only know their own private valuations, proposers attempt offers that look profitable to them and hope at least one other player benefits from the swap. Recipients decide accept/decline purely by comparing the value they would receive versus the value they give up (using their private values). Uncertainty enters because proposers do not know opponents' valuations (so they may propose offers that are declined), and if multiple players accept the same proposal the actual recipient is chosen at random. Given these values, a recurring profitable pattern is: dog sells GREEN (its weakest) to buy RED/BLUE; buffalo sells PURPLE (its weakest) to buy RED/BLUE; bear, who values GREEN relatively highly, is the natural acceptor in many offers where GREEN or PURPLE is given in exchange for RED. We forecast a plausible path where dog and buffalo repeatedly propose swaps that bear accepts, while bear's own proposals to buy GREEN with RED are typically declined because both opponents prefer RED to GREEN.

rounds.round_explanation: Early bargaining: proposers test straightforward value-improving swaps. Dog will try swapping a low-value GREEN for a high-value RED. Buffalo will try to offload low-value PURPLE for RED. Bear prefers GREEN and will accept trades that exchange his low-value RED for GREEN or PURPLE. Bear's own attempts to buy GREEN with RED are unlikely to attract accepts from dog or buffalo.

Patterns solidify: dog repeats the profitable GREEN->RED swap and buffalo again proposes PURPLE->RED. Bear, who gains from receiving GREEN or PURPLE in exchange for RED, is the consistent acceptor. Bear's own attempt to trade RED for GREEN remains unattractive to the other two players and is declined.

Late-stage repetition: proposers continue offering the same value-improving 1-for-1 swaps. Bear continues to accept exchanges that replace his low-value RED with GREEN or PURPLE. Bear's proposals to buy GREEN with RED again fail because both dog and buffalo prefer RED to GREEN.

strict_validation_errors: rounds[2].turns[2].recipient_id does not hold enough `BLUE` chips to satisfy the request.
repair_notes: rounds[2].turns[2]: downgraded accepted trade to decline because no feasible accepter remained.

parsed_target.game_explanation: Three players begin with identical endowments but heterogeneous private valuations over red/blue/purple (green is common-value). Proposers will try to trade chips they value relatively less for chips they value more; responders decide solely on whether the trade would increase their own total value (taking into account the possibility of being chosen if multiple players accept). Because valuations differ, many simple one-for-one swaps (e.g., a chip type that proposer values more for one they value less) are mutually beneficial to some but not all counterparts. Uncertainty enters through (a) private valuations (each player observes only their own values), (b) how many others will accept a proposal (if multiple accept, one is picked at random), and (c) changing holdings across turns that constrain future offers. Expect frequent small-quantity, targeted swaps (1–3 chips) that exploit opponents' relative preferences; green (common-value) is less attractive as a currency for getting higher private-value chips because it gives symmetric gains/losses to all. Proposers therefore often use colors that are relatively cheap to them but valuable to at least one counterpart to structure trades that are likely to be accepted.

rounds.round_explanation: Early-move dynamics: proposers try low-risk one-for-one exchanges to convert slightly lower-valued holdings into marginally higher-valued ones. Responders accept if the immediate swap increases their private value. Because everyone starts with large endowments, proposers can offer small quantities (1–2 chips) to tempt at least one partner; once a trade executes, holdings update and subsequent proposers adjust accordingly.

With holdings updated, middle-round proposals become slightly larger or repeat profitable patterns. Players who accumulated chips others value use multi-for-one offers to extract high-value chips for themselves. Acceptances often occur when the offered bundle yields a clear positive gain to a responder.

Late-round behavior: players convert accumulated inventories into their most-preferred chips where possible. Proposers may offer larger bundles to secure high-value targets; responders weigh both the direct gain and the randomization risk when multiple players accept. Because this is the final round, players are more willing to accept trades that yield definite end-of-game gains.

parsed_target.game_explanation: Three players start with identical holdings of four chip colors but very different private per-chip values for red/blue/purple (green is common-valued). Bargaining centers on using chips the proposer values relatively little to buy chips they value highly from others. Because purple is nearly worthless to bear but very valuable to dog, and red is valuable to otter/bear but less so to dog, a repeating pattern is likely: bear repeatedly offers low-value purple to buy red from dog; dog repeatedly offers red to buy purple from others. Proposers must balance improving their own portfolio against making offers attractive enough that at least one other player strictly benefits. Uncertainty enters because responses are private and if multiple players accept, one recipient is chosen at random; proposers may deliberately craft offers that produce a single likely acceptor (targeted terms) or that attract multiple acceptors (risking random assignment). Over three rounds, we expect several small asymmetric trades where bear accumulates extra red by paying purple, and dog accumulates purple by giving up red, while otter experiments but mostly fails to find mutually profitable terms.

rounds.round_explanation: Early round of experimentation. Otter (who values red most) tests the market with an offer that is not attractive to others and gets declined. Bear exploits his low value for purple to buy red from dog, who values purple highly. Dog then uses some red to buy more purple back from bear (both otter and bear would find this attractive but bear is chosen). The dynamic is bear selling almost-worthless purple to gain high-value red; dog converts red into purple.

Pattern intensifies. Otter again experiments with a small trade and is declined. Bear repeats the profitable strategy of offering purple for red; dog, who values purple highly, accepts. Dog then proposes trading red for purple; both other players would gain from giving up purple for red, so multiple acceptances occur and a single recipient is resolved randomly (bear is realized here). The bargaining shows clear specialization: bear accumulates red by selling purple; dog accumulates purple by selling red.

Final round continues the established trades. Otter again makes a non-attractive proposal and is declined. Bear again purchases red using cheap purple from dog, and dog again trades red for purple with multiple potential acceptors; one recipient (otter) is randomly selected. By the end, bear has increased red holdings, dog has accumulated purple, and otter has not managed a mutually profitable swap.

parsed_target.game_explanation: Three players repeatedly propose one-for-one chip trades (one color requested, a different color offered). Each player seeks to maximize their own total chip value using only their private values for red/blue/purple (green is common-value). Proposers will typically offer chips they value relatively low in exchange for chips they value relatively high; responders accept only when the trade increases their own value. Uncertainty arises because proposers do not know the other players' private valuations and therefore must propose plausible exchanges that create positive expected surplus for at least one potential partner. Because acceptance when multiple partners agree is resolved at random, proposers sometimes offer terms attractive to several players, which risks losing a preferred trading partner but increases the chance the trade executes. Over three rounds, we expect a sequence of small, beneficial one-for-one swaps: high-valued players (e.g., cat for RED/BLUE) will buy those chips by offering lower-valued chips (e.g., GREEN or PURPLE), while low-valued holders of a chip (e.g., badger for RED) will sell cheaply. Trades will update holdings immediately and that updated distribution will shape subsequent offers.

rounds.round_explanation: With symmetric starting holdings (10 of each color) and private values known only to each player, initial offers will target obvious asymmetric values. Cat highly values RED and BLUE and will try to acquire RED by offering lower-valued GREEN; otter (who values BLUE and PURPLE low) will try to trade BLUE to obtain RED; badger (who values RED very low) will sell RED and use the low-value RED to acquire higher-value PURPLE or GREEN. Early trades are likely to be accepted by the player who most dislikes the chip being sold.

After the first round holdings have shifted: cat and otter have accumulated extra REDs while badger has given up some REDs and gained GREEN/PURPLE/BLUE. In round 1, cat continues to target RED by paying GREEN (still a low-cost way for cat), otter repeats a profitable BLUE-for-RED exchange (selling low-value BLUE to someone who values RED more), and badger uses low-valued RED to purchase GREEN from cat (cat sometimes benefits from swapping GREEN for extra RED). Trades remain one-for-one; responders accept only if they gain positive value.

With holdings further adjusted (cat holding many REDs, badger holding more GREEN/BLUE/PURPLE, otter accumulating RED), proposers shift to exchanging mid-value chips. Cat can profitably swap PURPLE for BLUE (both highly valued by cat but BLUE slightly more), offering terms attractive to both other players; otter again uses low-value BLUE to pick up another RED from badger; badger finishes by trading BLUE for PURPLE with cat, who finds the swap mildly beneficial. Because some offers appeal to multiple partners, random selection determines the actual recipient when multiple accept.

parsed_target.game_explanation: Three players share identical starting holdings but have different private valuations for red, blue, and purple chips; green chips have a common known value. Camel and Otter both strongly prefer purple (0.8 and 0.9), while Badger values colored chips much less (red 0.2, blue 0.1, purple 0.1) and thus gains from selling purple for any of the other colors. This creates a persistent pattern: Camel and Otter will repeatedly propose trading away mid-value chips (red/blue) to acquire purple, and Badger will often accept those offers because they raise Badger's own payoff. Because accept/decline decisions are private to responders and multiple acceptances lead to a random recipient, proposers face uncertainty about which partner will be chosen when more than one would benefit. The bargaining dynamics therefore combine repeated targeted offers for purple, frequent acceptances by Badger (and sometimes by the other high-purple player), and occasional random allocation of deals when both high-value players accept. Over three rounds (nine proposal opportunities), we expect a sequence of small 1-for-1 swaps (red for purple) that concentrates purple holdings with Camel and Otter and builds up red holdings with Badger; greens and blues remain largely unchanged in this forecast.

rounds.round_explanation: Opening round: all players start with 10 of each chip. Camel and Otter highly value purple, Badger values purple very little. Camel goes first and is likely to offer a red for a purple (an exchange appealing to Badger). Badger, seeing an easy gain from selling purple, will accept. Badger then uses their newly acquired red to continue trading, offering purple for red to accumulate more red; that offer is attractive to both Camel and Otter (who value purple), producing competing acceptances and a random recipient. Otter then proposes a red-for-purple trade, which Badger accepts. The pattern is Camel targeting Badger for purple, Badger trying to turn purple into red, and Otter competing for purple as well.

By the second round Badger has accumulated more reds and fewer purples. Camel and Otter continue to prefer acquiring additional purple chips and repeatedly offer red-for-purple. Badger will repeatedly accept such offers because selling purple for red increases Badger's private value. When both Camel and Otter find a proposed deal beneficial, they may both accept and the recipient is chosen at random; proposers cannot pick the recipient. The incentives still favor sequences of 1-for-1 red↔purple swaps, concentrating purple with Camel and Otter while Badger accumulates red.

Final round: holdings have become more skewed—Badger holds many reds and few purples, while Camel and Otter hold more purples. The same incentives persist: Camel and Otter continue to propose red-for-purple trades and Badger typically accepts as it increases Badger's own payoff. When both high-purple players would benefit, acceptances are correlated and the recipient is determined by chance. Over the three rounds these incremental trades concentrate purple with Camel and Otter and red with Badger; blue and green remain largely untouched in this forecast.

parsed_target.game_explanation: This three-round, three-player bargaining game centers on asymmetric private valuations for the colored chips. Each player starts with equal nominal holdings, but their incentives differ: lion highly values RED and BLUE, butterfly highly values PURPLE, and camel favors BLUE; GREEN is common-value for everyone. Proposers will typically attempt to trade away chips they personally value least (lion: PURPLE; butterfly: BLUE or RED; camel: PURPLE or RED) in exchange for chips they value more. Responders decide to accept or decline based only on their private values and current holdings: they accept if the immediate exchange raises their own total value, decline if it lowers it, and are indifferent when it nets zero (often declining). Because responses are simultaneous and a proposer cannot choose among multiple acceptors, proposers sometimes propose trades that attract multiple acceptances and leave the partner selection to chance. Uncertainty arises from private valuations (proposers do not know which responder values the requested color more), the discrete nature of small-quantity trades, and the random resolution when multiple players accept. Over three rounds we expect a few small mutually beneficial exchanges (typically trading low-valued PURPLE for higher-valued BLUE/RED or trading modest-value GREEN for high-value PURPLE), some declined proposals where responders would lose value, and occasional rounds where multiple acceptances lead to a random recipient.

rounds.round_explanation: Early in the game players still hold many chips. Lion, who dislikes PURPLE, will try to convert PURPLE into BLUE or RED. Butterfly prefers PURPLE, so she will resist giving it up. Camel values BLUE and has weak value for PURPLE and RED, making him a likely counterparty when a proposer offers PURPLE for BLUE. Expect a mix of accepted small trades (where the responder gains) and declines when responders would be hurt.

After the first accepted trade, holdings shift slightly: lion has fewer PURPLE and more BLUE, camel has more PURPLE. Lion will again try to trade PURPLE for high-value RED or BLUE. Butterfly, who values PURPLE highly, will try to convert moderate-value BLUE or GREEN into more PURPLE. Camel continues to prefer BLUE and will propose favorable one-for-one swaps when it improves his own value. Some proposals attract multiple acceptances (both non-senders can gain), creating random partner selection.

By the final round players have adjusted inventories and try to capture remaining easy gains. Lion, short on PURPLE, will still trade small amounts of PURPLE for BLUE/RED when beneficial. Butterfly, with many PURPLE chips, will occasionally trade GREEN or BLUE for additional PURPLE only when it benefits her private value. Camel will attempt one-last improvements toward BLUE. Several proposals are small one-for-one or one-for-two trades; some attract multiple acceptances and get resolved randomly, others are declined when they would harm responders.

parsed_target.game_explanation: Three selfish players repeatedly propose one-for-one chip trades over three rounds. Each proposer requests one color and offers a different color; non-senders respond privately. Because chip values are heterogeneous (red/blue/purple differ by player, green is common-value at 0.50), trades that transfer chips toward a player's high-valued colors in exchange for low-valued colors are individually attractive. Lion strongly values red and blue (0.90 each) and moderately values purple (0.60); squid values blue and green (0.50) and dislikes red/purple (0.10); camel values blue (0.60), purple (0.50) and green (0.50) and moderately values red (0.40). Given these priors, proposers will tend to offer chips they value relatively less to acquire chips they value more, and prospective recipients will accept only when the offered chip is worth more to them than the chip they must give up. Uncertainty during play comes from private values (each player only knows their own valuations) and from random resolution if multiple responders accept; observers infer likely acceptances by comparing private-value gains for each potential responder. The forecasted path emphasizes repeated, small mutually beneficial swaps where low-value chips (for the proposer) are offered to lion in exchange for green or where lion gains high-value red in exchange for green or purple, yielding a sequence of accepted exchanges benefiting proposers and lion, and other turns declining when no responder gains.

rounds.round_explanation: Early stage: proposers seek quick, profitable one-for-one swaps. Squid and Camel can profitably acquire greens or purples by offering chips they value less; Lion is willing to give green or purple in exchange for higher-valued red or purple. Proposals that would make any responder worse off are declined.

After initial exchanges lion has accumulated some reds/purples while squid and camel gained greens/purples. Proposers again try swaps that convert their lower-valued chips into higher-valued greens; lion repeatedly accepts trades that give him red (high value) in exchange for green (lower value). Proposals that would require responders to give up high-value blues are rejected.

Late stage: proposers repeat straightforward profitable swaps (red -> green) while lion continues to accept exchanges that convert green into red. Lion's proposals to acquire additional blue by offering purple fail because potential partners would lose too much value by giving up blue.

parsed_target.game_explanation: Three players begin with identical holdings but different private values for red, blue, and purple chips (green is common-value). Proposers will tend to offer chips they value relatively less in exchange for chips they value more. Recipients accept only when the bilateral exchange increases their own total chip value. Uncertainty comes from (1) proposers not being able to choose among multiple acceptors (random selection if more than one accept), and (2) players not knowing others' private values, so proposers craft offers to attract a likely partner based on observable holdings and common knowledge of ranges. Early offers try to convert low-value purple into higher-value blue or red for those players who prefer them; mid-game holdings shifts create opportunities for others (e.g., a player with many purple becomes a target to sell purple). Throughout, each player acts selfishly to maximize their own private-valued sum, so many trades are asymmetric and targeted to partners who gain from the offered chip and hence will accept.

rounds.round_explanation: At the start everyone has 10 of each color. Squid (who values blue most) will try to acquire blue by offering purple; peacock (who values blue and purple highly) will try to trade away low-value red to get purple; alligator (who values red and blue highly and purple very little) looks for opportunities to swap purple for high-value chips. Early proposals test which partners will accept given holdings.

Holdings have shifted: squid has more purple from the previous seller, peacock gained purple as well, and alligator increased red/blue. Squid targets blue by selling purple; peacock opportunistically trades small red for green (an accepted, low-friction deal with alligator); alligator continues converting very low-value purple into higher-value red.

By the final round peacock has accumulated a large purple stock, squid has increased blue holdings, and alligator holds an elevated red position while having little purple left. Squid uses small, low-cost exchanges to convert remaining purple into green (an acceptable deal for peacock). Peacock repeats a conservative green-for-red swap that alligator accepts. Alligator finishes by trading leftover purple for an extra red.

parsed_target.game_explanation: Three players have heterogeneous private values for RED, BLUE, and PURPLE while GREEN is a common-value chip. Owl values BLUE very little and PURPLE/RED relatively highly, Alligator strongly prefers RED and BLUE and dislikes PURPLE, and Ram most strongly values RED and is neutral among the others. Given these asymmetries, the likely bargaining pattern is repeated small one-for-one or two-for-one swaps where: Owl monetizes low-valued BLUE to acquire RED or additional PURPLE; Alligator trades low-valued PURPLE to acquire more RED (or BLUE) when profitable; Ram tries to acquire RED by selling PURPLE (or sometimes BLUE) when it is individually profitable. Because responses are simultaneous and the proposer cannot pick the recipient, proposers will design offers that are attractive to exactly one counterparty when possible (to avoid random allocation). Uncertainty enters because responders do not know others' private values and may face random matching if both accept; analysts therefore forecast acceptances when the immediate bilateral gain for that responder is positive and likely reject when it is negative. Over three rounds we expect repeated trades along the same profitable margins: Owl sells BLUE for PURPLE/RED to Alligator; Alligator sells PURPLE for RED to Owl; Ram buys RED from Owl by offering PURPLE.

rounds.round_explanation: Opening round: Owl starts by selling cheap BLUE to obtain PURPLE (profitable for BLUE-lovers), Alligator then uses low-valued PURPLE to buy RED from Owl (small net gain for Owl), and Ram then attempts to buy RED by offering PURPLE to Owl. The sequence yields mutually beneficial bilateral swaps where Owl sheds BLUE, Alligator sheds PURPLE, and Ram acquires additional RED.

Round 1 continues the same local-arbitrage pattern. Owl again sells BLUE to acquire RED (Alligator values BLUE highly and accepts), Alligator again trades PURPLE for RED with Owl (mutually profitable for Owl), and Ram repeats the PURPLE-for-RED exchange with Owl when Owl gains slightly from the swap.

Final round: players continue exploiting the same surplus opportunities. Owl still prefers to offload BLUE for RED, Alligator continues to use PURPLE to acquire RED when Owl is the marginal supplier, and Ram makes a last attempt to increase RED holdings by selling PURPLE to Owl. Trades remain small, targeted, and acceptable to a single counterparty in each turn.

parsed_target.game_explanation: Three players with asymmetric private values over red/blue/purple and a common-value green chip will repeatedly propose one-for-one swaps to improve their own portfolio value. Because each proposer can only offer chips they hold and recipients decide simultaneously and privately, proposals will target partners who are likely to hold chips that the proposer values highly but the partner values relatively little. This creates predictable patterns: players who place very low value on a chip (cat values purple low; badger values blue low) will be frequent suppliers, while players who value that same chip highly (badger values purple high; cat values blue relatively high; butterfly values red/purple high) will frequently demand it. Uncertainty arises from (1) private information about others' exact valuations (players only know their own values, not others'), and (2) random selection when multiple recipients accept. Therefore proposals emphasize small, clearly beneficial swaps (1-for-1) that are likely to be accepted by at least one partner, and proposers exploit opponents' apparent comparative disadvantages (e.g., offering blue to cat to get purple). Over three rounds we expect mostly mutually beneficial, small exchanges that shift low-value chips away from players who undervalue them toward players who value them more, with occasional competition for scarce desirable chips creating randomness in who actually receives a trade when multiple players accept.

rounds.round_explanation: Early bargaining: each player starts balanced, so proposers aim for simple 1-for-1 swaps that are clearly profitable for at least one counterparty given their private values. Butterfly and badger both prefer purple; cat values purple lowest and blue highest, so cat will often be the supplier of purple. Proposers therefore offer blue or red to cat in exchange for purple; cat is expected to accept such swaps. When cat proposes, it will try to buy blue (its high-value chip) by offering low-value purple, attracting acceptance from the player who values purple highly (badger) or moderately (butterfly).

Holdings shift: cat accumulates more blue and fewer purple, making it an even more attractive target to buy purple from. Proposers again exploit cat's low purple valuation by offering blue for purple. When cat proposes, it continues selling purple to obtain more blue. Acceptance patterns remain driven by each player's private gains from the swap; some proposals attract multiple acceptances and outcome depends on random selection.

Endgame adjustments: players with surpluses of low-value chips (cat with purple) trade them for more valuable chips (blue or green). Proposers prefer partners who suffer least from giving the requested chip. Because multiple players can find a swap beneficial, randomness in partner selection matters more here; proposers often design offers that both non-senders would accept to ensure execution, but actual recipient depends on the random tie-breaker.

parsed_target.game_explanation: Three players begin with identical chip holdings but diverging private valuations for red/blue/purple (green is common-value). Each proposer can request one color and offer another; recipients decide privately based on their own valuation and current holdings. Proposers therefore try to convert chips they value less into chips they value more while offering just enough to make some other player better off. Recipients accept only when the trade raises their own total value, so acceptance depends on private values and on who currently holds the requested color. Uncertainty enters because proposers do not know other players' valuations and because simultaneous acceptance by multiple players leads to a random recipient. Over three rounds, we expect low-cost, mutually beneficial one-for-one exchanges (sell a chip you value less to buy a chip you value more) to succeed; offers that would make all recipients worse off are declined.

rounds.round_explanation: Early stage: everyone still has large endowments. Proposers attempt obvious gains (convert low-value for them into high-value). Many direct attempts to buy purple fail because others value purple highly; the most promising successful trades are ones that exchange a chip that the recipient values less for one they value more.

After an initial successful exchange, holdings shift: butterfly increases blue, ram increases red. Proposers exploit remaining asymmetries (e.g., offering blue to obtain green is attractive to a recipient who values blue more than green). Trades that create mutual gains (both sides increase value) are likely to be accepted by at least one player.

Late stage: players have updated inventories. Proposers continue to make small, one-for-one offers that convert lower-valued chips into higher-valued ones for themselves; recipients accept when they see a private gain. Because valuations differ consistently, ram frequently accepts offers that trade away blue for red, and butterfly repeatedly sells red to accumulate blue.

parsed_target.game_explanation: This three-round, nine-turn bargaining game centers on asymmetric private valuations over red, blue, and purple chips (green is common-value). Each proposer offers some chips they hold in exchange for chips they prefer. Because each player knows only their own valuations, uncertainty about opponents' willingness to trade matters: proposers target partners who are likely to gain from the swap, and responders accept only when the trade strictly increases their own total chip value. Strategic dynamics that follow: (1) players repeatedly convert lower-value colors into higher-value colors for themselves (e.g., offering red or purple to obtain blue for players who value blue highly); (2) competition for high-value colors can make some offers attractive to multiple recipients, in which case a random accepting recipient is realized; (3) early accepted trades change holdings and therefore later bargaining leverage and the set of mutually profitable exchanges. As an observer using the provided private valuations, I forecast proposals that swap a single chip of a low-value color for one of a higher-value color from the proposer's perspective, and I predict responses by computing each non-sender's private gain/loss from the proposed exchange (respond yes if their private value increases).

rounds.round_explanation: Initial positions: everyone has 10 of each color. Iguana values blue highest, whale values red/green highest, duck strongly prefers blue. Early trades are likely to be simple 1-for-1 swaps where proposers offer a chip they value relatively less to acquire a chip they value more. Responses follow direct private-value comparisons; offers that give a recipient a higher-valued chip in exchange for a lower-valued chip are accepted.

After round 0, whale has accumulated extra red and green, duck has gained a blue, and iguana lost a red but gained a blue. Proposers continue to try simple 1-for-1 swaps that increase their own value. Some offers that would be mutually beneficial for one recipient are not for another, so acceptance patterns vary by pair.

By the final round, holdings reflect prior exchanges (iguana and duck have extra blue, whale has extra red/green). Proposers may repeat successful prior patterns (offering low-value red or purple to acquire blue or green). Competition for blue remains strong; some offers attract multiple acceptances and are resolved randomly.

parsed_target.game_explanation: Three players start with symmetric holdings but different private values for red/blue/purple (greens have a common value). Proposers will tend to ask for colors they privately value highly and offer colors they value less. Acceptors respond only if the trade increases their own portfolio value given their private values and current holdings. Because proposers cannot choose among multiple acceptors, they will favor simple, small, obviously profitable exchanges (1-for-1 or 1-for-2) that are likely to attract at least one partner. Early rounds are used to trade away low-value chips for high-value chips; later rounds consolidate gains and trade marginally profitable units. Uncertainty arises because each player knows only their own values and must predict others' accept/reject behavior from public holdings and observed trades; when both non-senders would benefit, randomness in partner selection can create risk for the proposer and the acceptors. Overall we forecast a sequence of small 1-for-1 exchanges where lion often trades away low-valued blue for higher-valued purple or green, ram repeatedly trades away purple to accumulate blue/red, and owl trades away low-value red to accumulate blue and occasionally accepts green-for-blue swaps when profitable.

rounds.round_explanation: Round 0 is for quick, mutually beneficial 1-for-1 exchanges that exploit clear value differences. Ram (who values blue > purple) will trade purple for blue; lion (who values purple highest) will swap blue for purple; owl (who dislikes red) will trade red for blue. Because payoffs are clear, at least one counterparty accepts each reasonable proposal.

After initial small exchanges, players have slightly shifted holdings. Lion continues to trade away low-valued blue for higher-valued assets (purple or green). Ram repeats a profitable purple->blue swap. Owl again uses red as the main liquidity to buy blue. Some proposals attract multiple acceptors, producing a random recipient selection.

In the final round players consolidate position: ram trades purple for red to pick up another high-value red, lion continues to convert low-value blue into green, and owl pursues an additional blue by offering purple. Where trades are clearly value-increasing for multiple partners, both non-senders accept and a recipient is randomly selected; otherwise only the beneficiary accepts.

parsed_target.game_explanation: This three-round, three-player chip-trading game pits three players with different private valuations for red, blue, and purple chips (green is common-value) against each other. Each proposer will try to trade chips they value relatively less for chips they value more, while responders accept only if the exchange raises their own private payoff. Early trades reveal only holdings (not valuations), so proposers must anticipate acceptance based on plausible incentives: players with low blue value will be willing to trade blue away for higher-valued red/purple, and players who highly value purple or blue will offer their low-valued chips (red or blue) to acquire them. Uncertainty comes from not knowing others' private valuations and from the random resolution when multiple responders accept. Over the three rounds we expect frequent small (1-for-1) exchanges converting low-value chips into higher-value ones for the proposer, with responders accepting only when the trade strictly improves their own private payoff. Because proposers cannot choose among multiple acceptors, they sometimes face a tradeoff between proposing a favorable deal that many will accept (risking a random partner) and targeting a deal likely to be accepted by a single preferred partner.

rounds.round_explanation: Initial holdings are symmetric (10 of each color). Proposers will offer chips they personally value least (butterfly: red/purple; ram: blue; owl: red) to buy chips they value more (butterfly/ram/owl want blue/purple/blue respectively). Responders decide based solely on their private valuations and current holdings, so one-sided beneficial swaps (for the responder) will be accepted and sometimes both non-senders will accept, forcing a random recipient.

Holdings have shifted: butterfly and owl have increased blue counts, ram has increased purple. Proposers continue to trade low-value chips for higher-value ones. Ram often targets purple by offering blue (which he values least). Simultaneous acceptance can occur when both non-senders find the offered swap beneficial, leading to random partner selection.

By the final round most players hold larger quantities of their preferred chip types (butterfly/owl: blue; ram: purple). Proposals are smaller, incremental adjustments: converting low-value reds or blues into higher-valued blues/purples. Responders continue to accept only when their private valuations yield a positive net gain; multiple acceptances still resolve randomly.

parsed_target.game_explanation: Three players have different private valuations for red/blue/purple chips (green is common-value). Dolphin values blue most, owl moderately values blue, and whale strongly values red. Because proposers can only ask for one color while offering another, proposers will typically offer chips they value least and request those they value most. Dolphins and owls will therefore repeatedly propose selling red (or purple) to buy blue; whale prefers red and will often accept trades that convert their lower-valued chips (blue or green) into red. Uncertainty arises because each player only knows their own values and must infer whether a trade is attractive to others; this can lead proposers to choose offers likely to be accepted by the player(s) who gain most from the swap. In this forecast, proposers choose small one-for-one exchanges; whale is the natural buyer of red and thus accepts many offers for red, while whale-initiated offers to buy red by offering green are attractive mainly to owl (who values green more than red) and are accepted by owl. No trades require random resolution because at most one other player strictly benefits on each turn.

rounds.round_explanation: Early on, dolphin and owl both try to acquire blue by offering red (they value blue more than red). Whale strictly prefers red and will accept blue-for-red offers. Whale also can propose green-for-red trades that are attractive to owl. Expect dolphin/owl proposals for blue to be accepted by whale; whale proposals to be accepted by owl when they swap green for red.

With updated holdings, the same incentives persist: dolphin and owl continue proposing red-for-blue swaps to accumulate blue; whale continues to accept those swaps to accumulate red. Whale again offers green-for-red when it expects owl to gain (owl values green slightly more than red) causing owl to accept.

Final opportunities repeat prior patterns. Dolphin and owl keep attempting red-for-blue swaps; whale continues to accept blue-for-red offers (gaining high-value red) and to buy red by offering green to owl. By the end, whale has accumulated many reds, while dolphin and owl have more blue/green consistent with their valuations.

parsed_target.game_explanation: This three-round, three-player bargaining game will be driven by each player's private chip valuations and the common knowledge of current holdings. Players propose one-for-one (or one-for-multiple) exchanges where the proposer requests chips of one color and offers chips of another color. Proposers will only make offers that are expected to increase their own total value (buy value > sell value). Responders accept if the trade raises their own private value, but they do this simultaneously and privately, so proposers cannot choose partners; if more than one responder accepts, the recipient is chosen at random. Green chips have a common, mid-range value (0.50) for everyone and often serve as a neutral medium; red, blue, and purple have heterogeneous private values and therefore create trade opportunities when one player values a color substantially more than another. Uncertainty comes from not knowing others' private valuations: a proposer may structure the trade to be attractive to at least one likely responder (offering enough of a chip others value), but may face declined offers if recipients would lose value. Expect small, incremental exchanges where proposers convert lower-value holdings into higher-value colors for themselves while giving recipients a package that compensates them for the loss of the requested color.

rounds.round_explanation: Early round: players still hold many chips. Dolphin (who values RED highly and PURPLE little) can cheaply trade purple for red; alligator and duck both find such offers attractive. Duck tries a straightforward trade for red (buy RED sell BLUE) but this is not beneficial for potential partners and is declined. Alligator seizes an opportunity to trade red for purple, which benefits both proposer and recipients.

Mid-game: players have adjusted holdings. Dolphin repeats a low-cost way to gain red by offering purple and successfully trades with alligator. Duck opportunistically converts a purple into a red (profitable for duck) by offering a single purple; alligator accepts. Alligator then proposes another small red-for-purple swap; both recipients find it attractive and one accepts.

Endgame: holdings have become more lopsided but profitable single-chip swaps remain. Dolphin trades a cheap purple for a blue (beneficial to both proposer and a responder). Duck again converts a purple into a red by offering one purple; alligator accepts. Alligator finishes by buying an additional purple from dolphin by offering a blue; dolphin accepts because the swap raises dolphin's value.

parsed_target.game_explanation: All three players start with identical endowments but different private valuations for red/blue/purple (green has a common value). The dominant dynamic is repeated, small exchanges where proposers try to trade away colors they value relatively less (often purple or blue for some players, red for others) to acquire colors they value more (most commonly blue or green). Because acceptors only agree when a proposed exchange strictly raises their own private value, proposers tend to offer terms that create positive gains for at least one plausible counterparty. Uncertainty arises from private valuations: proposers must target offers that are likely to be profitable to at least one other player without knowing which. When a proposal is attractive to both non-senders, both will accept and a random recipient is realized; when only one benefits, that person accepts and the trade executes straightforwardly. Over three rounds we therefore expect several low-quantity, incremental trades (mostly 1-unit exchanges, one 2-unit sale early) that reallocate chips toward each player's individually highest-valued colors (duck and whale accumulate blue; swan accumulates green and some reds/purples when profitable).

rounds.round_explanation: Early-round pattern: proposers test mutually beneficial small swaps. Duck uses low-valued purple to buy blue; swan seeks green by offering low-valued blue; whale repeatedly tries to accumulate blue by offering reds. Some offers are attractive to both non-senders (creating random assignment), others only to one player.

With some information revealed by early trades, players iterate on successful patterns: duck continues converting purple into blue; swan repeats buying green by selling blue (acceptable to whale in this round); whale continues acquiring blue by offering reds targeted at the lower-blue-valuing player (swan). Trades are still small and largely one-for-one.

Final round consolidates positions: duck continues buying blue by selling purple to a player who still benefits (swan), swan opportunistically buys green by shedding blue (acceptable to duck), and whale makes a final small blue-for-red swap accepted by swan. Because the game is short and trades are small, each player focuses on locally profitable exchanges rather than maximizing group surplus.

parsed_target.game_explanation: Players enter a short, sequential bargaining game where each proposer requests one color of chip and offers a different color. Because each player cares only about their own chip-value totals and green chips have the same value for everyone, trades will be driven by asymmetries in private values for red/blue/purple. Pig (high value on blue) will repeatedly try to acquire blue, typically by offering a chip others value relatively more (purple) or a chip of neutral/common value (green) to create a positive surplus for one counterparty. Zebra (high value on red) will attempt to accumulate red by offering blue, which often creates a clear one-sided incentive for Pig to accept (Pig values blue highly). Swan also prefers red over blue and will try similar offers to Pig. The main uncertainty for each proposer is who (if anyone) will accept: acceptances are simultaneous and, if multiple accept, the recipient is selected at random, so proposers often tailor offers to make a single specific counterparty strictly better off. Recipients base responses only on their private valuations (gain from offered chips minus loss from requested chips). Thus we forecast a repeated pattern: Pig repeatedly offers purple for blue targeting Zebra (who values purple enough to give up blue), Zebra repeatedly offers blue for red targeting Pig (who values blue enough to give up red), and Swan offers blue for red targeting Pig. All proposed trades are small (one chip each) and are accepted when they strictly increase the recipient's private value.

rounds.round_explanation: Early bargaining: Pig leads by trying to convert low-value purple into high-value blue. Zebra, who values purple, will accept Pig's purple-for-blue offer. Zebra then uses blue to acquire additional red by offering blue-for-red to Pig, which Pig accepts. Swan, facing better deals with Pig than with Zebra, offers blue-for-red and Pig again accepts. The pattern creates clear one-to-one mutually beneficial exchanges for the targeted counterparty.

With updated holdings, the incentives remain the same. Pig again offers purple for blue targeting Zebra (Zebra still benefits). Zebra repeats the blue-for-red proposal targeting Pig, and Swan again proposes blue-for-red targeting Pig. Each proposed one-for-one exchange continues to be strictly value-improving for the intended recipient, so these offers are accepted.

Near the end of the game players follow the established profitable pattern. Pig again trades purple for blue with Zebra; Zebra again trades blue for red with Pig; Swan again trades blue for red with Pig. Each recipient still strictly benefits from the offered chip exceeding the value of the chip they give up, so these last-round proposals are accepted.

strict_validation_errors: rounds[0].turns[1].recipient_id must be one of the players who accepted the trade.
repair_notes: rounds[0].turns[1]: reassigned recipient to a feasible accepter.

parsed_target.game_explanation: This three-round, three-proposal-per-round bargaining game centers on asymmetric private valuations over red, blue, and purple chips (green is common-value). Two players (zebra and turtle) strongly value blue (1.00) while swan values blue least (0.40) and is relatively indifferent among red/purple/green (≈0.50). Turtle also values red highly (0.90); zebra values red moderately (0.60). Given these priors, bargaining dynamics will feature repeated small, bilateral exchanges where the proposer tries to give up a chip they value relatively less in order to acquire a chip they value more. Swan’s low value for blue makes them the natural supplier of blue in trades (they accept offers that convert their blue into red/purple/green). Zebra and turtle compete for blue, but because proposers cannot pick the partner when multiple accept, proposers will prefer offers likely to be accepted by a single target (typically swan). Uncertainty enters through private valuations (each player knows only their own values), simultaneous accept/decline responses (so multiple accepts lead to a random recipient), and evolving holdings after each executed trade. Behavior is governed by individual value-maximization: responders accept only if the trade increases their own total value (or is at least weakly non-worse in plausible tie cases); proposers target partners who are likely to accept given their assumed private preferences.

rounds.round_explanation: Early bargaining: proposers exploit swan’s relatively low value for blue to acquire blue chips by offering red or purple. Zebra and turtle target swan for blue; zebra’s initial offer trades red for blue (swan strictly benefits). Swan then flips some blue back into red when advantageous, creating possible multi-accept situations because zebra and turtle both value blue highly. Turtle uses low-valued purple to buy blue from swan. Holdings update immediately after each accepted turn, changing future incentives.

After initial reallocations, zebra continues to extract blue from swan by offering red. Swan, still preferring red/green/purple to blue, repeatedly sells blue and occasionally proposes a trade that both zebra and turtle would accept; random selection resolves multi-accepts. Turtle again targets swan for blue using low-valued purple as the currency. Each accepted trade incrementally shifts blue toward the two players who value it most, while swan accumulates other colors it values relatively more.

Late-stage repetition: patterns from earlier rounds continue. Zebra repeatedly purchases blue from swan by offering red; swan continues converting blue into other colors it values more. Swan’s proposals to swap blue for red can attract both zebra and turtle, creating random allocation when both accept. Turtle persists using purple to buy blue from swan. Over the three rounds the result is a net transfer of blue toward zebra and turtle (the highest valuers) and of red/purple toward swan.

parsed_target.game_explanation: Three players repeatedly propose one-for-one chip trades. Each proposer will try to give up chips they value relatively less and request chips they value relatively more. Because green chips have a common known value (0.50) and the other colors have private, heterogeneous values, trades that move chips from a player who values them less to a player who values them more are individually attractive and likely to be accepted. Strategic uncertainty arises because a proposer cannot choose the partner when multiple players accept; they only know their own valuations and must anticipate which recipients find the offer profitable. Over three rounds (three proposal turns per round), we expect many small, surplus-improving exchanges: players will commonly offer low-value purple (for frog and unicorn) or blue (for kangaroo) in order to acquire higher-value reds or purples for themselves. Because each acceptance is evaluated privately using each responder's valuation, some proposals will attract multiple acceptances and be resolved by random selection, while others will attract only the intended partner. The dynamics will concentrate purple toward kangaroo (who values purple most), blue toward frog (who values blue most), and occasional red/green swaps when those are mutually advantageous.

rounds.round_explanation: Initial endowments are symmetric (10 of each color). Kangaroo strongly prefers purple and will try to acquire it by offering his low-value blue. Frog values blue highly and is willing to give up low-value purple. Unicorn values red/green more than purple and blue and will try to convert low-value purple into red. Early trades are likely to be accepted by the players who gain value, and some proposals may attract more than one acceptor.

After the first round, kangaroo has accumulated extra purple and reduced blue; frog has increased blue and red; unicorn has exchanged a purple for red. Kangaroo continues to trade away his remaining low-value blue to collect green (moderate value) or additional purple; frog continues to trade low-value purple to acquire green or blue; unicorn targets red by offering low-value chips. Trades in this round mostly involve purple being offered by frog and unicorn and blue/green being exchanged when mutually profitable.

By the final round, kangaroo holds a large purple stack and fewer blues; frog holds many blues and a modest number of reds; unicorn has increased red holdings. Proposers will continue to exploit their comparative advantages: kangaroo trades inexpensive blue for green when frog can profit from the reverse, frog trades low-value purple for red from kangaroo, and unicorn converts leftover low-value purple into green or red when possible. Acceptance decisions remain driven by each responder's private valuation.

parsed_target.game_explanation: This three-round, three-player bargaining game features heterogeneous private values on red, blue, and purple chips and a common-value green chip. Proposers can request one color and offer another; responders accept only if the exchange raises their own private chip value. Given the private valuations: zebra highly values blue (1.00), swan highly values purple (1.00), and dolphin most values red (0.80), the predictable dynamics are: zebra will aggressively seek to acquire blue by offering lower-valued chips (green or red) because many partners gain from swapping blue for something they value more; swan will try to acquire more purple by offering blue (which zebra values most and will thus often accept); dolphin will try to convert low-value blue into high-value red or purple, typically by offering blue to zebra or swan.
