Uncertainty comes from (1) simultaneous private accept/decline decisions when both responders would benefit (leading to a random recipient), and (2) proposers not knowing other players' private valuations (they favor offers that are plausibly attractive to likely partners). Over the three rounds we forecast repeated patterns: zebra accumulates blue via profitable green-for-blue or red-for-blue swaps; dolphin trades blue away to acquire more red; swan incrementally accumulates purple by offering blue to zebra. Trades execute when they are value-improving for at least one responder; when both responders benefit, a random accepting recipient is chosen.

rounds.round_explanation: Initial endowments are equal. Dolphin (prefers red) will try to trade away low-value blue for red. Zebra (prefers blue) will try to buy blue by offering green. Swan (prefers purple) will attempt to convert blue into purple. Early beneficial swaps are attractive to exactly one partner (zebra often benefits from giving red for blue) or to both (green-for-blue offers), generating random assignment when both accept.

Holdings have shifted: zebra has grown its blue stock and dolphin has more red. Dolphin continues to trade blue for red. Zebra continues opportunistically buying blue by offering green (both dolphin and swan could benefit from green-for-blue, so random assignment is possible). Swan again targets purple by offering blue—zebra often finds this attractive and will accept.

By round 2 zebra holds a large blue stock and is happy to exchange other colors for more blue. Dolphin continues converting remaining blue into red. Swan persists in buying purple by offering blue; zebra frequently accepts these offers because blue is its top-valued chip. Several turns where both responders would benefit create random assignment but the overall flow favors zebra accumulating blue, dolphin accumulating red, and swan accumulating purple.

parsed_target.game_explanation: This three-round, three-player bargaining game is driven by private, asymmetric valuations over red/blue/purple chips and a common known value for green. Each player will try to acquire chips they value more than the ones they give up. Elephant values BLUE highest (0.60) and is willing to trade away low-value PURPLE (0.30) to get BLUE. Hamster also prefers BLUE over PURPLE (0.40 vs 0.20) and so can profitably exchange PURPLE for BLUE when a partner values BLUE less. Turtle most highly values RED (0.70) and values BLUE the least (0.20), so turtle will propose to convert BLUE into RED; recipients who gain (e.g., elephant, who values BLUE 0.60 > RED 0.50) will accept. Because each proposal asks for one color and offers a different color, the likely pattern is repeated small (1-for-1) exchanges: elephant offers PURPLE to buy BLUE (profitable for elephant, attractive to turtle), hamster similarly offers PURPLE to buy BLUE (profitable for hamster, attractive to turtle), and turtle offers BLUE to buy RED (profitable for turtle, attractive to elephant). Uncertainty arises because proposers do not observe others' private valuations and therefore must propose trades that are robust (i.e., are likely to find at least one accepting partner). If multiple non-senders would gain from the same proposal, random selection determines who actually transacts. The forecast below uses the given private values as analyst priors to predict proposals and accept/decline responses and updates holdings round-by-round.

rounds.round_explanation: Early play: proposers look for simple 1-for-1 exchanges that clearly increase their own value. Elephant will try to trade a low-valued PURPLE for a high-valued BLUE; hamster will seek the same improvement (PURPLE -> BLUE). Turtle will propose exchanging low-valued BLUE for high-valued RED. With the private values, elephant's PURPLE->BLUE is attractive to turtle (turtle prefers PURPLE to BLUE), hamster's PURPLE->BLUE is attractive to turtle as well, and turtle's BLUE->RED is attractive to elephant.

Having observed successful 1-for-1 trades, players repeat the profitable exchanges. Elephant and hamster again find it beneficial to swap their low-valued PURPLE for BLUE; turtle continues converting BLUE into RED. The pattern remains that turtle accepts PURPLE->BLUE offers (since turtle values PURPLE > BLUE) and elephant accepts turtle's BLUE->RED offers (since elephant values BLUE > RED).

By round three the same incentives persist. Elephant and hamster continue to convert PURPLE into BLUE because it raises their own chip-value; turtle continues to convert surplus BLUE into high-value RED. Given private valuations, turtle remains the unique acceptor of PURPLE->BLUE proposals, and elephant remains the unique acceptor of turtle's BLUE->RED proposals, so the sequence repeats.

parsed_target.game_explanation: Players begin with equal nominal holdings across four chip colors but very different private valuations for red/blue/purple; green is a common-value medium. Bargaining dynamics will therefore be driven by each proposer trying to convert chips they value relatively little (typically PURPLE or RED for some, or BLUE for others) into chips they value relatively highly. Because private values differ, mutually beneficial, one-sided, or contested trades appear: low-value holders of a color (e.g., cat for RED/BLUE) are willing to give it up cheaply, while high-value holders (e.g., dog for RED/BLUE) resist giving those colors unless compensated generously. Proposers tend to offer multiple low-valued units to attract acceptance from a single counterparty; recipients accept only when the deal increases their own total value, and when both non-senders accept the partner is selected at random, introducing risk to proposers who cannot choose their partner. Uncertainty for each player comes from not knowing others' private valuations, so offers are sized to appeal to plausible recipients (e.g., offering multiple PURPLEs to someone likely to value PURPLE more than the requested color). Over three rounds we expect a sequence of small, individually profitable swaps (often PURPLE-for-high-value colors or RED-for-PURPLE), many accepted by the low-value holders, and occasional multi-acceptance outcomes resolved by random selection.

rounds.round_explanation: Early-round opportunistic trades: proposers convert their low-value PURPLE into higher-value colors. Badger starts by targeting cat (who values RED relatively little) with a small PURPLE-for-RED swap; dog then targets cat for BLUE using PURPLE; cat uses accumulated PURPLE to buy a GREEN by offering multiple REDs, creating competition among recipients.

With holdings shifted, proposers continue exchanging low-valued PURPLEs for higher-valued colors. Badger trades PURPLEs to pick up a BLUE from cat; dog leverages PURPLEs to buy RED from cat; cat then purchases another GREEN by offering a single RED to dog (who values RED highly), producing straightforward acceptances.

Endgame conversion trades consolidate privately favorable positions. Badger again trades PURPLEs for a BLUE from cat; dog continues to use small PURPLE bundles to pick up another BLUE from cat; finally cat—holding many PURPLEs—offers a small RED to acquire two PURPLEs, a deal that benefits both potential givers and thus is accepted by both non-senders and resolved by random matching (here resolved in favor of badger).

parsed_target.game_explanation: Three players start with identical holdings (10 of each color) but different private valuations for red/blue/purple; green is common-value. Each proposer will try to convert chips they value relatively less into chips they value more by offering low-value chips (for them) to buy high-value chips. Responders accept only if the proposed exchange increases their own private total value. Because players do not observe others' private values, proposers tend to offer generous enough exchanges to induce acceptance (e.g., giving up multiple low-value chips for one high-value chip). When more than one responder would accept, the actual partner is selected at random, so proposers face uncertainty about who benefits and who will be chosen. The game therefore features: (1) proposers pushing beneficial one-sided trades, (2) responders accepting only positive-gain offers, (3) occasional competition among responders for favorable offers, and (4) proposers balancing their own gains against the need to make offers attractive enough to elicit a partner.

rounds.round_explanation: Early bargaining: proposers test the market by offering low-valued red or blue chips to buy higher-valued purple/blue chips. Responders accept only when the deal increases their private value; identical-value swaps tend to be declined.

Proposers who gained information from prior turns make larger or more attractive offers (e.g., offering extra low-value red chips) to overcome responder indifference. Offers that produce clear positive gains for responders are more likely to be accepted, sometimes by multiple players.

Endgame trades aim to secure remaining high-value chips. Proposers who still can profit make offers likely to be accepted by exactly one other player (targeting a known beneficiary) or generous offers that attract multiple acceptances and yield a random partner.

parsed_target.game_explanation: Three players start with equal endowments of four chip types but very different private values for red/blue/purple; green is common-value. Buffalo highly values blue and dislikes red, so Buffalo is the natural trader who will offer red to acquire blue repeatedly. Bear and Dog both value red highly (Dog the most) and will be prime buyers of red; Dog also values purple relatively highly and can trade purple into red. Proposers will typically offer a chip they value relatively less and request a chip they value more; responders accept only when the exchange raises their own private value. Because responders act simultaneously and any multiple-accept case selects a recipient at random, proposers face partner uncertainty: a favorable trade may be taken by either acceptable partner. The bargaining dynamics therefore produce many small, mutually beneficial one-for-one exchanges (red↔blue, red↔purple, red↔green) tilted toward Buffalo selling red for blue and Bear/Dog buying red, with occasional role shifts when random selection assigns the recipient.

rounds.round_explanation: Early moves reveal positional strengths. Bear, who values red above blue/purple, will try to convert a blue into an extra red; Buffalo will use its low red value to trade red for blue; Dog will use purple to buy red when Buffalo is an attractive seller. Responders accept only when they strictly gain by their private values, and multiple-accept situations create random recipient assignment.

Players continue exchanging along their comparative-value edges. Bear converts a marginal purple into blue when Dog is willing to give up blue; Buffalo persists in swapping its low-value red for blue and sometimes gets matched to Bear or Dog randomly; Dog uses blue to top up red when Buffalo is the seller.

Final round sees more of the same pattern: Bear purchases red using cheap green when Buffalo's red is very low value; Buffalo continues to swap red for blue; Dog finishes by converting purple into additional red. Random assignment when both non-senders benefit remains a source of uncertainty for proposers.

parsed_target.game_explanation: With three players who each start with 10 of each color, bargaining will be driven by asymmetric private values for red/blue/purple and a common-value green. Proposers rationally offer chips they value relatively less in exchange for chips they value more; responders accept only when a proposed swap raises their own total value. Because acceptances are simultaneous and the proposer cannot pick among multiple accepters, proposers craft offers that are likely to attract at least one partner (often targeting the player who values the requested color least). Uncertainty arises from (1) proposers not knowing opponents' private values exactly and (2) the random tie-break when multiple responders accept. Over three rounds we forecast a sequence of profitable one-for-one (or one-for-many) exchanges: peacock repeatedly trades away lower-valued red for higher-valued blue/purple; otter frequently sells purple (which he values low) to obtain higher-value chips; squid repeatedly trades away low-value blue to accumulate high-value red/purple. Each accepted trade increases the acceptor's value and the proposer's value; declined offers are those that would hurt both potential responders or offer insufficient compensation.

rounds.round_explanation: Early-round trading focuses on moving low-value holdings (otter's purple, squid's blue, peacock's red) toward players who value them more. Peacock opens by buying purple from the low-value holder (otter). Otter then leverages abundant cheap purple to request red, and squid uses cheap blue to pick up red.

With updated holdings, players repeat profitable swaps. Peacock purchases an extra blue (selling red) from squid, otter again sells purple to acquire blue (both other players find that attractive), and squid converts another blue into red from the player who values blue relatively more.

Final-round trades polish up individually beneficial exchanges: peacock again purchases purple from the player who values it less, otter squeezes any remaining purple to buy red, and squid trades blue for a final purple. Because acceptances are private and sometimes multiple responders prefer the deal, random selection affects which player supplies the requested chip when more than one accepts.

parsed_target.game_explanation: This three-round, nine-turn bargaining game pits three players with heterogeneous private valuations for red/blue/purple chips (green is common-value). Each proposer can request a positive quantity of one color and offer a positive quantity of a different color; acceptance is by other players privately and simultaneously. Incentives push proposers to ask for colors they highly value while offering colors they value less; responders accept when the trade increases their own total chip value given their private valuations. Because acceptance is simultaneous and, if multiple accept, the recipient is chosen at random, proposers sometimes must overpay slightly to attract at least one partner. Uncertainty arises from each player only knowing their own values (so they infer others' preferences from public trade history and quantities offered) and from the random tie-breaking when multiple accept. Over the game we forecast repeated patterns: bear repeatedly seeks blue (high value for bear) by offering red or low-value purple; otter repeatedly seeks purple (high for otter) by offering low-value blue or red; dog strongly values purple and repeatedly buys purple by offering red. Many trades are mutually profitable for the proposer and a single other player (typically bear or dog), leading to an equilibrium of small, incremental one-for-one exchanges that steadily reallocate high-value chips toward those who value them most.

rounds.round_explanation: Early-information round: players start with symmetric endowments and reveal preferences through modest one-for-one proposals. Bear (prefers blue) tries to acquire blue by offering low-value purple; otter (prefers purple) asks for purple offering low-value red; dog (prefers purple most) also attempts to buy purple. Responders accept if net value increases for them given private valuations.

Middle round: players have partial evidence of others' preferences from prior trades. Bear continues to target blue, now sometimes offering red (which is relatively valuable to potential partners) to secure trades; otter persistently targets purple by offering blue; dog keeps buying purple using red. Most proposals are one-for-one and accepted by the player who gains value from the exchange.

Final round: trading continues along established patterns. Proposers know approximate demand and sometimes reuse previously successful offers (e.g., bear offering red for blue, otter offering blue for purple, dog offering red for purple). Because acceptances are private and tie-breaking is random when multiple accept, proposers sometimes craft offers that consistently attract a single responder who benefits from the exchange.

parsed_target.game_explanation: This three-round, nine-turn bargaining game pits three players with heterogeneous private values for red/blue/purple chips and a common value for green. Each proposer will try to trade away chips they value relatively less (for them) in exchange for chips they value more, while responders accept only if a proposed swap raises their own total value. Because players observe trade history but not others' private valuations, proposers anticipate likely acceptances based on typical preference patterns: otter strongly prefers blue, badger values blue and red highly, and cat values all non-green chips equally. Green chips are common-value and often used when no private arbitrage exists. Uncertainty comes from (1) which responder will accept when both would benefit (random selection among acceptors), (2) how proposers balance the size of offers to make deals attractive without overpaying, and (3) whether indifferent players (like cat) will accept neutral trades. The forecast below uses the given private valuations as analyst priors to predict plausible proposals, acceptance decisions, and realized recipients, updating holdings immediately after each executed trade.

rounds.round_explanation: Early round: all players start with abundant stocks. Proposers aim to convert low-value purple or red (for some) into higher-value blue. Because both badger and otter highly value blue, offering extra low-value chips for a single blue is likely to be attractive to others and often accepted by multiple responders (creating random selection risk). Cat, having neutral valuations, accepts positive bargains when it is beneficial.

Mid-game: holdings have shifted so some players have accumulated more of preferred colors. Badger continues to trade away low-value purple for blue; otter still seeks incremental blue at modest red cost. Cat's proposals tend to be neutral or mildly beneficial to others and therefore often fail. When both responders would gain, random selection again decides the executed partnership.

End-game: proposers push for final value extraction. Badger still turns purple into blue when profitable. Cat remains constrained by symmetric valuations and has a hard time proposing value-increasing trades for herself that others will accept, so many of her late proposals fail. Otter makes another small red-for-blue swap to incrementally improve his blue holdings; when multiple responders would gain, randomness determines the trading partner.

parsed_target.game_explanation: This three-round, three-player bargaining game pits three heterogeneous private valuations against identical starting endowments (10 of each color). Each proposer can request one color and offer a different color; non-senders accept only if the exchange raises their own total chip value. From the analyst perspective, mutual-gain trades are scarce because all players value GREEN at the common 0.50, and red is always worth less than blue or purple to every player. That makes it difficult to construct swaps that strictly benefit both the proposer and at least one responder. The most promising profitable trades for a proposer are those that exchange low-valued chips (for the proposer) for high-valued chips (for the proposer). But those trades typically make the recipient worse off unless the proposer is willing to give multiple low-value chips to compensate — which often eliminates the proposer's gain. Given these payoffs, the likely dynamics are: proposers attempt small advantageous swaps (e.g., exchanging red for blue/purple or swapping green for a higher-valued color), responders reject unless they strictly gain, and most proposals will be declined. There is uncertainty about whether a proposer will sacrifice value to entice acceptance (they should not if rational), and about whether indifferent responders accept; the forecast assumes responders accept only when they strictly gain. The forecast below follows the prescribed proposer order and reports three rounds with three turns each; each turn includes the plausible buy/sell proposal and the predicted responses under the stated acceptance rule.

rounds.round_explanation: Opening round: players test simple, direct swaps that raise the proposer's value (e.g., trading lower-valued red for higher-valued blue/purple). Responders rarely strictly gain from these proposals because the offered chips are usually worth less to recipients than the requested chips, so most proposals are predicted to be declined.

After several rejections, proposers try modest variations (changing quantities or colors). Because private valuations keep recipients from strictly benefiting in most plausible offers, rejections remain common. Proposers could attempt to offer larger bundles to compensate responders, but doing so usually removes the proposer's own gain; the forecast assumes proposers avoid net-negative offers.

Final round: proposers make last attempts. Some may be tempted to offer slightly larger bundles to secure an acceptance, but rational self-interest and linear valuations mean giving away multiple chips often destroys the proposer’s gain. Under the analyst prior that proposers avoid net-negative proposals and responders accept only when strictly better off, the remaining proposals are also predicted to be declined.

parsed_target.game_explanation: Three players with different private values for red/blue/purple (green common-value) bargain over three rounds. Each proposer requests one color and offers another; non-senders accept only if the swap raises their own private value. Observed public holdings change after each accepted trade and provide information about likely preferences, but each player knows only their own valuations, so proposers must offer terms that look attractive to potential recipients given those recipients' incentives. Strategic patterns expected: camel (high value for blue) repeatedly seeks to acquire blue by offering lower-valued chips; butterfly (very high value for red) often tries to acquire red by offering blue; lion prefers red and purple relatively highly and uses blue (which he values less) as a currency. When multiple non-senders would benefit, acceptances produce random partner selection, introducing uncertainty for proposers about who will be matched. Across rounds we forecast mostly mutually beneficial 1-for-1 (or small multi-unit) swaps where the proposer trades chips they value less for chips they value more, and recipients accept when their own value increases.

rounds.round_explanation: Initial endowments are symmetric (10 each). Early trades are straightforward value-improving swaps: lion uses blue (low for him) to buy red (high for him) from camel; camel then leverages red to buy blue; butterfly targets red by offering blue to camel. Acceptances follow each player's private gains, and randomization doesn't occur because only one non-sender benefits in those proposals.

Holdings are now unbalanced: camel accumulates blue, lion accumulates red. Lion continues to trade blue for red (he values red more), camel continues to target blue by offering red, and butterfly trades opportunistically to get more red. Several swaps remain 1-for-1 or small multi-unit trades because marginal gains scale linearly with single chips and acceptance is determined by private value comparisons.

With more extreme imbalances (camel holding many blues, lion holding many reds), proposers exploit low-value holdings of others (e.g., offering blue to buy purple from players who value purple more). Multiple acceptances occur when the swap is value-improving for more than one non-sender, in which case the recipient is randomly selected. The final round includes such tie situations and one last targeted swap by butterfly converting low-value purple into a moderate-value blue.

parsed_target.game_explanation: This 3-round, 3-player bargaining game pits three heterogeneous valuers of four chip colors against each other. Each proposer will try to trade away chips they value relatively less (their private low-value colors) to buy chips they value relatively more. Because green is common-value (0.50) it is a convenient medium of exchange for some proposals, but many profitable trades center on PURPLE and BLUE because players' private values differ dramatically there. Uncertainty arises because proposers do not know the other players' private valuations and must craft proposals that are plausibly attractive to at least one counterparty; responders decide based only on their own private values and current holdings. Simultaneous private responses can produce conflicts (multiple accepts) resolved at random, so proposers sometimes aim for offers that are attractive to a single, cheap seller rather than broadly appealing. Over three rounds we expect repeated targeting of the player(s) who value the desired color least (often squid for PURPLE, camel for BLUE), and use of low-value chips as payment (e.g., RED or PURPLE for squid, BLUE for camel) or green as a middleman when cross-player valuations align.

rounds.round_explanation: Early in the game proposers try straightforward, low-friction swaps: Camel targets squid's cheap PURPLE by offering RED; Squid then targets Camel's cheap BLUE by offering RED; Lion looks for cheap PURPLE from squid using RED. Each proposal leverages the known cross-player pattern (squid values PURPLE very low, camel values BLUE low) to secure a single accepting partner.

With some trading history visible, proposers again exploit obvious mismatches. Camel continues to buy PURPLE from squid using BLUE (camel values BLUE low), Squid presses to acquire more BLUE offering RED to camel, and Lion uses GREEN as a neutral medium to buy PURPLE from squid (green is equally valuable to all, making it attractive to a low-PURPLE seller). These offers are crafted to be individually profitable for the targeted recipient given their private valuations.

In the final round proposers continue the same targeting logic to eke out last gains: Camel again purchases PURPLE from squid using BLUE, Squid buys additional BLUE offering RED (targeting camel), and Lion finishes by buying another PURPLE from squid using GREEN. By now squid holds relatively many BLUE and GREEN and relatively few PURPLE, so squid accepts offers that convert its low-value PURPLE into higher-value chips.

parsed_target.game_explanation: Three players start with equal, ample holdings of four chip colors but very different private valuations for red, blue, and purple (green is common-value). Each proposer will try to exchange chips they value relatively little for chips they value more, while anticipating that responders accept only if the trade increases their private total value. Because proposers cannot choose which accepting partner wins when multiple players accept, they will often target trades that at least one other player is likely to prefer (offering a chip another player values highly). Uncertainty comes from each player not knowing opponents' private values; observers (analyst) can forecast responses by applying the private valuations as priors. Strategic considerations include (1) offering chips that are cheap to the proposer but valuable to at least one other, (2) keeping offers small (unit trades) to increase the chance of acceptance, and (3) the risk that multiple acceptances lead to a randomly chosen recipient, which can make some proposers prefer trades that are likely to be accepted by exactly one partner. Over three rounds, we expect repeated patterns: Alligator tends to sell low-valued purple to acquire blue or red; Peacock tends to sell low-valued red to acquire purple or blue; Squid tends to sell blue to acquire purple or red. Many simple 1-for-1 exchanges increase the value for both proposer and at least one responder, so most proposed unit trades are accepted.

rounds.round_explanation: Early in the game players make straightforward, mutually beneficial 1-for-1 trades. Squid will try to convert a low-value blue into higher-value purple; Alligator will convert low-value purple into high-value red; Peacock will exploit its low value for red to get purple. Because different players value the same colors differently, several proposals will be acceptable to more than one responder, producing random recipient selection on those turns.

With updated holdings, players continue to trade along the same preference gradients. Squid still finds blue inexpensive and requests red; Alligator continues to exchange purple for blue; Peacock trades its remaining low-value red for blue. Multiple-accept situations repeat when the offered chip is attractive to more than one responder.

Near the end of the game players continue profitable unit trades but holdings have shifted: Alligator has accumulated more reds and blues and fewer purples, while Peacock has more blues/purples and few reds. Squid still trades blue for purple when advantageous. Alligator repeatedly sells scarce purple to acquire additional blue or red. Because accept/decline decisions are deterministic given private values, the same kinds of profitable 1-for-1 swaps continue to execute.

parsed_target.game_explanation: This three-round, nine-turn bargaining game will be driven by each player's asymmetric private valuations for red, blue, and purple chips and a common valuation for green chips (0.50). Players use green as a liquidity instrument: offering green in exchange for other colors is cheap for proposers and attractive for recipients when the green payment exceeds the recipient's private value of the chip they surrender. Because proposers cannot choose among multiple acceptors, they trade off making an offer attractive enough to induce acceptance (possibly from multiple partners) while avoiding overpaying. Uncertainty enters because each responder only knows their own values, so accept/decline decisions depend solely on whether the swap increases their private expected value; when both non-senders would gain, both typically accept and the partner is then chosen at random. Given the private values provided, we forecast repeated small, favorable green-for-high-value-chip exchanges: Alligator repeatedly targets blue (value 1.00 to them) by offering green (0.50), Owl repeatedly uses green to buy red (0.80 for Owl) by paying 2 greens to overcome other players' higher red values, and Ram systematically accumulates purple (value 1.00 to Ram) by offering green. The dynamics produce multiple mutually beneficial exchanges; because benefits for recipients are usually positive, most proposals are accepted by one or both non-senders, and recipient identity is determined by chance when both accept.

rounds.round_explanation: Opening round: proposers use green as the primary bargaining currency. Alligator (who values blue highly) starts by offering green for blue; both opponents benefit and accept. Owl then pays 2 greens to buy a red (2 greens > red value to most recipients), making the offer attractive enough to secure a trade. Ram finishes the round by offering 2 greens for purple to secure a high-value purple for himself.

With updated holdings, proposers continue similar strategies. Alligator again offers green for blue and gets acceptance. Owl repeats a 2-green-for-red offer, which remains attractive to holders of red. Ram again purchases purple by offering two greens, which is profitable for typical red/blue/purple holders and thus accepted.

Final round: Alligator again purchases a blue with green; Owl executes another 2-green-for-red exchange to increase her red holdings; Ram finishes by buying a blue with a single green when only Owl is a viable trading partner (Alligator values blue too highly to accept). The final moves reflect continuing private-value-driven trades and the random resolution when both would accept.

parsed_target.game_explanation: Three players start with equal initial holdings (10 of each color) but very different private valuations for red/blue/purple (green is common-valued at $0.50). That creates a clear pattern: badger highly values blue and red (likely to buy blue repeatedly), butterfly prefers green then red and will trade from mid-valued chips to acquire greens or reds, and cat treats red as its cheapest chip and will use red as a currency to buy higher-valued chips (blue or green). Because players only know their own valuations, proposers target partners who—based on public play and the common ranges—are likely to accept (i.e., partners who lose little value by transferring the requested chip and gain more value from the offered chip). The main uncertainty is who will accept when multiple players could benefit; where only one responder strictly benefits the trade executes deterministically. Over three rounds, we expect repeated 1-for-1 swaps where butterfly and cat use low-valued chips (purple or red) to acquire greens/blues, and badger repeatedly trades away reds to acquire blues. Trades generally occur when the recipient would strictly increase their own payoff by accepting (they evaluate only their private values), so proposers tailor offers to create positive gains for a specific responder.

rounds.round_explanation: Early bargaining: proposers test direct 1-for-1 swaps that make sense given private values. Butterfly can cheaply buy red from cat by offering purple (cat values purple more than red); badger will pursue blue by offering red to butterfly; cat will then use red to buy green from badger. The pattern is targeted bilateral gains rather than multi-party splitting.

Players repeat profitable pairwise patterns now that holdings have shifted. Butterfly again targets green by offering red (badger gains from extra red); badger continues buying blue by offering red to butterfly; cat uses cheap red to buy blue from butterfly. Each proposal is calibrated so exactly one other player gains and accepts.

Final round: similar targeted swaps continue. Butterfly again trades red for green with badger, badger buys another blue from butterfly by offering red, and cat uses red to buy blue from butterfly. Because proposals are chosen so only one responder benefits, every proposal is accepted and executed.

parsed_target.game_explanation: Three players repeatedly propose single-color-for-single-color trades over three rounds. Each player seeks to maximize their own total chip value based on private valuations for red/blue/purple and a common-value green. Because ram values BLUE and PURPLE much more than RED, he will try to acquire blue/purple while offering low-value red. Goose values BLUE above others but values PURPLE relatively low, so goose can profitably offer PURPLE to acquire BLUE. Butterfly has near-equal values for RED, BLUE, and GREEN and moderate value for PURPLE, so she is a flexible counterparty who will accept trades that improve or are neutral for her. Uncertainty stems from simultaneous private accept/decline decisions: a proposer cannot pick among multiple acceptors and each player knows only their own valuations. This creates incentives to craft proposals that are attractive to at least one other player (often by offering multiple lower-valued chips for a single high-valued chip) and to avoid asking for high-value chips without offering sufficiently valuable compensation. Across the game, expect frequent rejections of cheap one-for-one offers (because the recipient's loss exceeds the gain) and some multi-chip offers that create mutual gains and thus get accepted, sometimes by random selection when multiple players accept.

rounds.round_explanation: Opening round: proposers test simple one-for-one exchanges. Cheap offers (e.g., offering RED for BLUE) are likely rejected because recipients (especially ram) value blue highly. Butterfly can make an exchange that attracts ram by offering purple for red, since ram values purple highly and red little.

Players have observed holdings change. Ram still prizes BLUE/PURPLE and declines small one-for-one offers. Goose exploits his low valuation of PURPLE by offering two PURPLE for one BLUE, creating a deal attractive to both ram and butterfly; multiple acceptances lead to a random recipient selection. Proposals that are only marginally beneficial to the proposer or harmful to recipients continue to be rejected.

Late-stage adjustments: ram, having accumulated purple from prior trades, can now credibly propose a multi-purple-for-one-blue swap that is attractive to butterfly. Goose uses small, positive-margin trades to convert low-valued purple into red. Butterfly concludes by proposing a one-for-one exchange between blue and green that is neutral for her but attractive to recipients; multiple acceptances are resolved randomly.

parsed_target.game_explanation: Three players repeatedly trade single-color bundles to maximize their own private chip-value. Common green chips are neutral (0.50) to all, while red/blue/purple values diverge sharply across players: whale highly values purple and blue, iguana values blue modestly but values purple almost nothing, duck values blue and red most. Expect pattern of repeated two-party exchanges where the low-value holder of a chip (especially iguana holding purple) sells it cheaply to a high-value buyer (whale or duck), and buyers pay with chips they value less. Proposers will target trades that raise their own expected value while offering a chip that at least one other player prefers; responders accept only when the swap increases their private total value. Uncertainty comes from simultaneous private responses (multiple acceptances lead to random selection) and limited ability of proposers to pick recipients, so proposers tend to structure offers that are attractive to a specific counterparty (e.g., iguana selling purple to whale). Over three rounds we forecast a pattern where whale repeatedly acquires purple by offering green or blue, iguana repeatedly exchanges low-valued purple to accumulate red or green, and duck converts purple or red into additional blue when profitable.

rounds.round_explanation: Opening moves: whale targets purple (highest personal value) by offering blue, which is moderately valuable to others; iguana, who values purple very little, will accept. Iguana then uses her low-value purple to buy red from whale (whale prefers purple), and duck opportunistically trades purple for blue with whale when the swap benefits him.

Whale continues to accumulate purple, this time paying with green (a common-value chip) which is attractive to iguana as she values purple little and green more. Iguana again trades a low-value purple to get red from whale. Duck repeats a profitable purple→blue swap, again transacting with whale who values purple highly.

Final round: whale again purchases purple with green (iguana accepts), iguana converts another low-value purple into red from whale, and duck finishes by buying an extra blue using a red, with iguana accepting because she prefers blue to red.

parsed_target.game_explanation: This three-round, three-player bargaining game centers on asymmetric private valuations for RED, BLUE and PURPLE while GREEN is a common-value chip (0.50 for everyone). Each proposer attempts to trade away chips they value relatively less in exchange for chips they value more. Because players only know their own values, proposers face uncertainty about who will accept; recipients decide solely on whether the specific exchange improves their own total value. As analyst priors show: Owl values PURPLE and RED highest (0.90 and 0.80) and BLUE relatively high (0.60); Ram strongly prefers PURPLE (0.80) then GREEN and RED; Lion prefers GREEN (0.50) then PURPLE (0.40). This creates predictable patterns: Lion often trades low-value RED or BLUE to acquire GREEN; Ram repeatedly pursues PURPLE by offering GREEN; Owl trades BLUE for RED (or BLUE for RED vice-versa) and sometimes sells BLUE to obtain RED because others (especially Lion) value BLUE more than RED, producing single-acceptor swaps. Because multiple players sometimes could benefit, some proposals carry the risk of multiple acceptances (randomly resolved), but in the forecast below most proposals produce a single acceptor whose private valuation makes the swap immediately profitable for them.

rounds.round_explanation: Early-round incentives: Lion seeks GREEN (0.50) by offering lower-value BLUE (0.20) — Owl (BLUE 0.60, GREEN 0.50) will accept exchanging GREEN for BLUE. Ram then requests PURPLE (0.80) offering GREEN (0.50); Lion (PURPLE 0.40, GREEN 0.50) finds that advantageous and accepts. Owl finishes the round by swapping BLUE for RED because Lion values BLUE more than RED and will accept.

With public history visible, players update holdings but keep private valuations. Lion again trades a low-value RED for GREEN and Owl accepts (GREEN > RED for Owl). Ram continues to convert GREEN into PURPLE by offering GREEN for PURPLE; Lion accepts because he values GREEN more than PURPLE. Owl then buys RED by offering BLUE; Lion again prefers BLUE to RED and accepts.

In the final round players continue the same comparative-value trades. Lion again offers BLUE for GREEN and Owl accepts. Ram again offers GREEN for PURPLE; Lion accepts. Owl finishes by offering BLUE for RED and Lion accepts — consistent with Lion valuing BLUE over RED and Owl valuing RED over BLUE.

parsed_target.game_explanation: This three-round, three-player bargaining game centers on asymmetric private valuations for red/blue/purple chips (green common-valued). Each player privately maximizes their own total chip value. Proposers will typically offer chips they value relatively low in exchange for chips they value higher. Potential recipients will accept a proposed one-for-one exchange only when that swap increases their own private value. Because all players start with equal inventories and observe public holdings and past trades, proposers target counterparties whose private rankings (inferred from past behavior and public payoffs) make them the most likely acceptors. Uncertainty arises because each player knows only their own valuations; proposers form priors about others' preferences and recipients decide based only on their private benefits. When both non-senders would gain, they both accept and the recipient is chosen uniformly at random, introducing execution risk for proposers who prefer a specific partner.

rounds.round_explanation: Early in the game players convert low-value chips into higher-value ones. Butterfly (low value on red) will try exchanging red for green because ram values red highly and green moderately; owl tends to accept trades that give blue (her highest value) in exchange for purple; ram seeks red and will sell lower-value blue or purple. Acceptance decisions are driven by immediate private gains.

With initial gains realized, players continue to trade low-valued holdings for higher-valued ones. Butterfly continues converting red into purple (high for her) targeting ram, who now values receiving red; owl again seeks blue and offers purple (both others can benefit); ram targets red by offering purple because both others can benefit from receiving purple.

In the final round players make straightforward marginal improvements. Butterfly trades a blue (next-lowest for her) for a purple; owl continues to consolidate blue by trading a red; ram pushes to accumulate red by offering blue — in many of these proposals both non-senders gain and so accept, leaving random selection to determine the trading partner.

parsed_target.game_explanation: This three-round, three-player bargaining game centers on asymmetric private valuations for red, blue and purple chips while green chips are common-value. Each proposer will tend to offer chips they value relatively little in exchange for chips they value more. Because recipients decide simultaneously and the proposer cannot pick among multiple acceptors, proposers often make offers that are attractive to at least one partner but sometimes to both (creating random partner selection). Uncertainty for each player is twofold: (1) players know only their own private chip values, so they must infer whether an offered exchange is mutually profitable from public holdings and typical preferences, and (2) when multiple players would accept, the proposer faces the risk that a random recipient, not their preferred partner, is chosen. Throughout, each player focuses solely on maximizing their own ex-post total chip value, so trades that increase a recipient's private value are accepted even if they lower group surplus. The forecast below uses the experiment values as analyst priors to predict plausible proposals, acceptance decisions, and immediate holding updates.

rounds.round_explanation: Early opportunistic trades. Owl (who highly values purple and poorly values blue) offers blue for purple; Dolphin (who values red highly) offers purple for red; Whale (who values green relatively highly and blue moderately) offers blue for green. Because recipients differ in private values, some proposals attract both non-senders while others appeal to only one.

With updated holdings, proposers continue to trade away chips they value less for chips they value more. Offers that exchange low-value chips for high-value ones tend to be accepted; when both non-senders gain, acceptance by both is likely and the proposer faces random partner selection.

Final-round adjustments: players use remaining turns to pick up high-value chips and shed low-value ones. Because the proposer cannot name the partner when multiple accept, proposers often target a single likely acceptor by designing the exchange to be attractive primarily to that player.

parsed_target.game_explanation: This three-round, three-player bargaining game centers on repeated small exchanges that exploit differences in private valuations of the color-specific chips. Green chips have a public, common value (0.50) and thus are not a focus of mutually beneficial asymmetric trades; the action comes from the heterogeneous private values for red, blue, and purple. Alligator and Dolphin both value red and blue fairly highly but with opposite ordering (Alligator: blue > red; Dolphin: red > blue), which creates many 1-for-1 mutually profitable swaps (e.g., Alligator trades red for blue). Duck assigns near-zero value to purple, so Duck is willing to give up purple in multi-for-one packages to acquire reds or blues. Proposers tend to offer chips they value relatively less and request chips they value relatively more; recipients accept only if the exchange increases their own private value. Because players see only public history and their own values, accept/decline decisions are myopic and based on the immediate private value change; when more than one non-sender accepts, the actual recipient is chosen at random, which injects strategic uncertainty for proposers trying to target a particular partner. Repeated rounds allow the same profitable bilateral pattern (Alligator <-> Dolphin red/blue swaps) to recur, while Duck uses purple as bargaining currency to buy reds, sometimes attracting multiple acceptances.

rounds.round_explanation: Early-round dynamics: initial symmetric endowments make simple 1-for-1 red/blue swaps attractive between Alligator and Dolphin because each can gain a small private surplus. Duck prefers to offload purple and is willing to bundle multiple purples to obtain a red; such offers can be attractive to players who value purple more than the red they give up.

After initial trades, Alligator and Dolphin have already begun exchanging red and blue repeatedly because each 1-for-1 swap yields a small gain to both sides. Duck continues to use purple as tradeable low-value currency, offering multi-purple bundles for single reds; such packaged offers can be attractive to both Alligator and Dolphin, occasionally producing multiple acceptances and therefore lottery-like allocation risk for Duck.

Final-round incentives continue to favor the established red/blue swap cycle between Alligator and Dolphin. Duck again uses purple to buy reds; because some offers are attractive to multiple players, acceptance competition can determine who actually transacts. Players remain focused on own-value improvement rather than maximizing total surplus.

parsed_target.game_explanation: Three-player, three-round alternating-proposer bargaining. Each proposer can request one color and offer one different color in a fixed schedule. Players privately value red/blue/purple differently but share a common green value ($0.50). Proposers will generally try to trade away chips they value less for chips they value more; to get an opponent to accept they must offer the opponent a net gain given that opponent's private values. Because proposers cannot pick the accepting partner when multiple players accept, they tend to prefer offers that will be attractive to at least one opponent while preserving positive surplus for themselves. Responders decide myopically (based on the immediate exchange and their private values) whether the swap raises their own total value. Uncertainty comes from (a) not knowing opponents' private valuations, (b) the random selection of recipient when multiple accept, and (c) the evolving holdings which change bargaining leverage over rounds. The forecast uses those incentives and the provided private valuations as analyst priors to predict likely proposals and accept/decline responses.

rounds.round_explanation: Early round: proposers look to convert low-value holdings into higher-value chips. Whale begins by selling common-valued green to obtain high-value purple; other proposers respond if the proposal improves their private value. Duck and Swan will generally accept trades that convert their low-value purple into green or blue when it increases their own value; whale frequently accepts proposals that swap away mid-value blue or red for higher-value purple.

Mid game: players continue to make small one-for-one swaps that increase their own private value. Whale repeatedly tries to acquire more purple by offering mid-value blue; duck repeatedly tries to convert low-value purple into blue; swan targets red by selling purple. These offers are crafted to be attractive to at least one opponent (typically whale, who values purple highly).

Final round: players use remaining leverage to make further beneficial one-for-one swaps. Whale continues to convert green into purple (safe positive surplus), duck continues converting purple into blue where whale is the likely accepter, and swan again exchanges purple for red. With holdings more polarized, whale is the frequent accepting partner because purple is his top value.

parsed_target.game_explanation: This three-round, three-proposal-per-round game will be driven by each player's private marginal values for the four chip types. Green chips are the common-value medium ($0.50) and will act as a neutral fallback; the other colors differ sharply across players. Zebra highly values RED (1.00) and dislikes BLUE (0.20); Pig moderately prefers RED and BLUE (0.50 and 0.40) and dislikes PURPLE (0.10); Swan strongly prefers BLUE (0.90) and also values PURPLE (0.60). Because proposers must offer one color in exchange for another, expect trades where proposers offer chips they value relatively little in order to acquire chips they value more. Recipients will accept if, given their private values, they gain on the margin. This creates predictable bilateral opportunities: Zebra can often buy RED by offering BLUE (beneficial for Zebra and for Swan or Pig depending on their relative RED vs BLUE values); Pig can buy BLUE by offering PURPLE; Swan can buy BLUE by offering RED. When both non-senders would gain, multiple acceptances will introduce random assignment risk to the proposer (the recipient is chosen uniformly among acceptors), which creates strategic uncertainty and may dissuade some marginal acceptances. The forecast below follows these incentives, showing a sequence of small one-for-one swaps (mostly BLUE-for-RED and PURPLE-for-RED) where each accepted trade increases the proposer's value and also increases the recipient's value given their private valuations. Holdings update immediately after accepted trades, changing later accept/reject incentives.

rounds.round_explanation: Early in the game players exploit clear comparative-advantage swaps. Zebra uses cheap BLUE to buy RED; Pig trades low-value PURPLE to acquire BLUE; Swan then reclaims BLUE by offering RED. Multiple acceptances can occur when both non-senders gain from exchanging BLUE and RED.

After the first round holdings shift toward players' comparative advantages (Zebra accumulates RED, Swan accumulates BLUE). Players continue making small one-for-one trades: Zebra again offers BLUE to buy RED (Pig is marginally willing), Pig trades PURPLE for RED (Swan benefits), and Swan buys BLUE by offering RED (both non-senders gain but a single recipient is randomly realized).

With positions further adjusted (Zebra holding many RED, Swan holding many BLUE), players make final opportunistic one-for-one swaps. Zebra again purchases RED by offering BLUE (Pig accepts), Pig purchases RED by offering low-value PURPLE (Swan accepts), and Swan makes a final BLUE purchase by offering RED; when both non-senders would gain, multiple acceptances occur and we realize one recipient at random.

parsed_target.game_explanation: Three players with heterogeneous private values negotiate pairwise chip swaps over three rounds. Each proposer will try to trade away chips they value relatively low (PURPLE or BLUE for some players) to acquire chips they value relatively high (primarily RED, and secondarily GREEN or BLUE depending on the player). Because acceptors decide based only on their own private values, proposals are structured so the proposer has a positive net gain given their private values while also offering enough compensation for at least one counterparty to gain. Uncertainty appears in (1) whether a target seller values the requested chip low enough to accept, (2) whether multiple non-senders will accept (leading to a random recipient), and (3) how holdings evolve and change the attractiveness of future offers. Observationally, zebra-blue-2700 (who heavily favors RED and dislikes BLUE) will often be willing to sell BLUE for higher-value chips; turtle-purple-273 (who dislikes PURPLE) will use PURPLE as a tradeable low-value currency; swan-orange-6532 (high value on RED and BLUE) will accumulate BLUE when it can cheaply convert PURPLE into BLUE. Trades that swap low-value for higher-value chips for the proposer (e.g., PURPLE -> BLUE or BLUE -> RED with sufficient quantity) are the most likely to execute; many attempts to buy RED one-for-one will be declined unless the proposer offers multiple lower-value chips to make the seller better off. The forecast below follows these incentives round-by-round and updates holdings immediately after each accepted trade.

rounds.round_explanation: Early round: proposers test cheap one-for-one exchanges using low-value PURPLE as currency. Turtle can cheaply convert PURPLE->BLUE and targets zebra (who values BLUE very low). Zebra will try to buy RED by paying multiple BLUEs (since RED is highly valued by others). Swan will opportunistically convert PURPLE->BLUE when zebra's BLUE stock is low and purple is relatively attractive.

With updated holdings, turtle leverages remaining PURPLE to buy GREEN (small net gain) because paying two PURPLE can make a green seller indifferent/positive. Zebra again uses low-valued BLUE to purchase REDs (2 BLUE -> 1 RED) which often yields acceptances from those who value BLUE reasonably. Swan continues to convert PURPLE to BLUE whenever zebra's BLUE supply is available and the swap is profitable for both sides.

Final round: players extract remaining easy gains. Turtle repeats a small PURPLE->GREEN trade that leaves sellers marginally better off (randomly assigned). Zebra again converts leftover BLUE into REDs with a 2-for-1 exchange, which is attractive to RED holders who prefer more BLUE in some cases. Swan picks up another BLUE by selling PURPLE when zebra still holds a small BLUE stock.

parsed_target.game_explanation: Three players with differing private valuations negotiate pairwise chip swaps over three rounds. Greens have a common value (0.50) for everyone; red/blue/purple values vary. Kangaroo strongly prefers RED, unicorn values RED and PURPLE moderately, and frog strongly prefers PURPLE but values RED very little. These asymmetries create predictable supply/demand: kangaroo repeatedly offers low-value chips (BLUE or PURPLE) to obtain RED, frog repeatedly sells RED (its lowest-value chip) to obtain PURPLE or BLUE, and unicorn trades when it gains modestly (trading away RED only when receiving nearly-as-valuable chips). Uncertainty stems from each player only knowing their own values and inferring others' preferences from observed offers and acceptances. Proposers typically target trades that look profitable to at least one other player (exploiting others' low value for RED or high value for PURPLE), and responders accept when the immediate private-value gain is positive. Random selection resolves conflicts when multiple responders accept.

rounds.round_explanation: Early moves reveal preferences: kangaroo opens by offering low-value BLUE for high-value RED, expecting frog (who hates RED) to accept. Unicorn then trades away RED for PURPLE when it can swap into something it values more, and frog follows by selling RED for PURPLE. Observed acceptances quickly signal that frog systematically values PURPLE much more and RED much less than others.

With early trades confirming roles, kangaroo continues acquiring RED by offering PURPLE (which kangaroo values less), unicorn targets RED by selling BLUE (a middling asset for unicorn), and frog repeatedly sells low-value RED for higher-value chips. Frog is the frequent acceptor of RED-selling offers because RED is least valuable to frog.

By the final round players exploit known asymmetries: kangaroo continues to buy RED with low-value BLUE, unicorn opportunistically trades away PURPLE or BLUE to get RED when profitable, and frog keeps selling RED into demand. Multiple acceptances occur when both kangaroo and unicorn find the swap individually beneficial; random selection determines the execution.

parsed_target.game_explanation: Three players start with equal quantities of each chip type but different private values for red, blue, and purple; green has a common value of $0.50. Proposers will try to exchange chips they value relatively less for chips they value more. Because acceptors only accept trades that raise their own private value, simple one-for-one exchanges that move a chip from a lower-value holder to a higher-value holder are likely to be accepted. Key sources of uncertainty: (1) each player only knows their own private values, so proposers must target trades that are plausibly mutually beneficial given public holding patterns and typical value rankings; (2) when multiple non-senders accept, the recipient is chosen at random, so proposers face partner risk; (3) small indifferences (break-even trades) leave acceptance ambiguous. Over three rounds we expect repeated simple swaps: zebra (who strongly prefers blue and purple) will repeatedly try to buy blue by offering low-value red; swan (who most prefers green) will try to convert lower-value chips into green; dolphin (who prefers purple then red) will seek purple while sometimes accumulating red. Trades that create clear private gains for a counterparty (e.g., swapping someone’s blue for their red if that person values red more than blue) will be accepted, while trades that would make plausible recipients worse off will be declined.

rounds.round_explanation: Early in play everyone has abundant holdings. Zebra, who values BLUE very highly and RED very little, will propose red-for-blue swaps that are attractive to both other players (both prefer red to blue). Swan will pursue green by offering low-value blue (blue is least valuable to her) for green. Dolphin will ask for purple in exchange for green because swan has lower purple value and will be willing to give up a purple for a green gain.

After initial beneficial swaps, zebra continues to target blue by offering red since both others still gain from that exchange. Swan keeps converting red into green (green is her top value) when a partner values red more than green. Dolphin leverages his moderate blue value to trade blue for additional purple, which he prefers.

With updated holdings, zebra still finds one-for-one red→blue trades attractive and proposes them again; swan continues to exchange red for green when partners value red more; dolphin attempts to turn red into extra purple at the end but faces partners who would be worse off or indifferent, so that late attempt fails.

parsed_target.game_explanation: This three-round, nine-turn bargaining game will be dominated by asymmetric private valuations over the three personalized chip types (RED, BLUE, PURPLE) while GREEN is common-value. Turtle (turtle-pink-5956) most prefers PURPLE then GREEN, hamster (hamster-red-132) most prefers PURPLE then BLUE, and elephant (elephant-blue-3237) most prefers RED then BLUE. Observationally this creates a stable trading pattern: Turtle and Hamster will try to convert low-value RED chips into high-value PURPLE chips, and Elephant will accept those trades because Elephant values RED much more than PURPLE. Elephant will reciprocate by offering PURPLE to acquire RED, which both Turtle and Hamster prefer to keep (they value PURPLE > RED) and so sometimes both non-senders accept Elephant's proposals, producing random assignment of the trade partner. Uncertainty arises because each proposer does not know others' private valuations and so targets trades that are plausibly attractive to at least one other player (small, safe 1-for-1 swaps). Over the game we therefore expect Turtle and Hamster to steadily accumulate PURPLE at the expense of RED, while Elephant accumulates RED and becomes PURPLE-poor; acceptances largely follow private gains for the recipient, with occasional simultaneous acceptance when the offer is clearly favorable to more than one counterparty.

rounds.round_explanation: Opening round: proposers try small, conservative 1-for-1 exchanges. Turtle and Hamster will attempt to trade their low-valued RED for PURPLE; Elephant will accept these because Elephant's RED value far exceeds Elephant's PURPLE value. When Elephant proposes (offering PURPLE for RED), both Turtle and Hamster prefer receiving PURPLE to giving up RED, so both accept and a random recipient is chosen.

Mid game: the prior pattern repeats. Turtle and Hamster continue exchanging RED for PURPLE with Elephant because those trades increase the proposer's value and also improve Elephant's holdings. Elephant's proposals continue to be attractive to both non-senders (they value PURPLE >> RED), so Elephant's partner is sometimes selected at random when both accept.

Closing round: players continue the tried strategy of 1-for-1 RED↔PURPLE swaps. Turtle and Hamster convert leftover RED into PURPLE; Elephant accumulates RED and sheds PURPLE. Elephant's last proposal again attracts both acceptances, producing another randomly assigned partner.
